CHAPTER 7


Learning Engineering
is Ethical
by Jordan Richard Schoenherr *



An act has no ethical quality whatever unless it be chosen out
        of several all equally possible. – William James


What is Ethical in Learning Engineering?
Education and training are replete with questions of values and ethics.1 For example,
questions arise during instructional design and delivery about who should teach a
course, what material will be covered, how that material will be presented, and how
a learner’s knowledge will be assessed. The answer to each of these will have value
implications within, and outside of, the learning context. Additionally, a number
of ethical issues define the learning context, including accessibility, copyright and
intellectual property rights, learner privacy, and diversity.2
       The value-laden nature of instructional design and learning engineering
mustn’t be overlooked.3 In parallel, there has been growing recognition that the
values of learners need to be incorporated into the process of design.4 In other
words, not only are the products of learning engineering value-laden, so too are
its processes. Design decisions and other learning engineering activities should be
informed by frameworks that consider values and ethics—from the perspective of
learners, educators, and communities.
       This chapter considers how to address ethical learning engineering in a
principled, systematic manner. It offers an ethical sense-making approach and a
practical framework for ethical sense-making in learning engineering.


* The author would like to thank Jodi Lis for her insightful feedback throughout the writing
  process for this chapter.


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-11
202 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                             !!"
     Ethics in the Learning Engineering Process
     Using the learning engineering process described in Chapter 1, ethical considerations
     can be identified at each stage.
            The challenge stage involves understanding the ethical affordances (or ethical
     features) of the problem space. This includes understanding the learner population,
     the technologies available to instructional designers and learning engineers, and the
     sociocultural context where learning will occur. At this stage, value-based design
     frameworks can be used to identify the general questions that should be asked during
     the design process and the appropriate means to assess the ethical affordances of a
     design. As well, learners themselves should be engaged to understand what values
     are important to them and how these values might be considered in framing the
     challenge.
            In an iterative creation stage, learning engineers and instructional designers
     will begin to identify vulnerabilities in the design that result from bringing together
     early models and mock-ups, piloting assessment instruments and educational
     interventions. At this stage, a preliminary assessment of performance can help us
     determine whether specific design characteristics or features discriminate against
     some populations of learners. This assessment focuses on discrimination with
     respect to successful participation in designed activities, successful learning, and
     engagement and motivation responses with the learning activities and content (for
     example, level of challenge, connections to interest). Learning engineers must then
     consider how, or whether, to address these issues and be prepared to justify their
     decisions.
            Adoption introduces other social and ethical concerns. Implementation may
     be carried out in unintended ways, leading to unintended unethical consequences.
     As well, during the implementation phase, the learning engineering team must
     consider information ethics, especially issues concerning privacy, consent, and user
     autonomy. These considerations must be incorporated into implementation design.
     This requires an understanding of a community, their relationships, and values. For
     instance, the requirements of privacy are not only determined by legislation but also
     by sociocultural traditions.
            The investigation stage provides an opportunity to investigate possible im-
     provements to the current solution and to potentially correct weaknesses in areas of
                                                 Learning Engineering is Ethical | 203



value sensitivity. This is also a phase in which data are analyzed. Careful consid-
eration should be given to avoid bias in interpretation of those data, in protecting
learner privacy, and in interpreting the data through a lens of ethical sense-making.
       Outside of the process model outlined in Chapter 1, we must also consider
a parallel set of concerns for the technology ecosystem. As learning engineering
technologies (for example, learning management systems, adaptive instructional
systems) and approaches (for example, human-centered design, anti-discrimination
design) become widely adopted, there will be competition and cooperation by
individuals and organizations. Here, ethical questions will also play a key role. We
must consider how technologies can be standardized as well as their transparency,
their accessibility, and their interoperability. As technologies evolve, we must ensure
that learners can access and understand their data and the methods used to create
them by developing standards that reflect the best practices of learning engineering
communities.


                                        !""
Ethics: From Principles to Practice
In both Eastern (for example, Confucius) and Western (for example, Aristotle)
philosophical traditions, the discipline of ethics has been considered a competency.
Virtues reflect qualities or traits that must be cultivated over time. Like any
competency, ethical sense-making requires knowledge to identify the features
of a situation that are ethically relevant. These features, or ethical affordances,
don’t necessarily exist objectively within the world. Rather, much like technical
affordances, ethical affordances require an understanding of how an individual
interacts with their environment. Even if a learning engineer considers the ethical
affordances associated with the intended function of a product, unintended uses
might introduce new ethical issues.


ETHICAL PRINCIPLES, NORMS, AND CONVENTIONS

Given their roles and responsibilities, prescriptive norms apply to learning engi-
neers. Typically, these norms are defined in laws, codes of conduct, or guidelines.
These norms can be defined for a specific knowledge domain, such as health pro-
fessions education, or for a kind of product developed by learning engineers, such
as adaptive instructional systems (AIS) or autonomous systems, more generally.5
204 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     As the multiplicity of principles suggests, although learning engineers might simply
     believe that the design process ends once a product, instrument, or process has been
     completed, they must also consider how their innovations are adopted by specific
     communities.


     PROFESSIONAL CODES OF CONDUCT

     Education, training, and engineering professions have available codes that consider
     social and ethical issues associated with instruction.

     Education. Professional associations at all levels of education have adopted codes
     of ethics. For instance, the Association for Educational Communications and Tech-
     nology’s (AECT) Code of Professional Ethics presents a set of values that express
     a commitment toward learners, society, and their profession.6 In terms of learners,
     ethical practice requires promoting independence in the pursuit of learners’ goals,
     participation in educational programs, and protecting the rights (especially privacy),
     health, and safety of learners. In addition to these positive norms, the AECT code
     also emphasizes nonmaleficence in terms prohibiting actions related to stalking,
     bullying, harassment, defamation, and retaliation. Emphasis is also placed on the
     consideration of varying points of view, which suggests a recognition of the need
     to avoid personal biases. On a broader scale, the AECT also notes that instructional
     designers and educators need to differentiate between individual and organization-
     al views, accurate representation and communication of educational issues to the
     public, and promotion of diversity. Similar principles have also been articulated by
     the National Education Association 7 and the International Society for Performance
     Improvement.8

     Training and Talent Development. Paralleling approaches in academia, the
     learning and development (L&D) industry has also recognized the need to develop
     its own principles. For instance, the Association for Talent Development has devel-
     oped a code of conduct that describes expected behaviors and practice that should
     occur in the context of professional training and development. Like other codes, it
     considers the personal, professional, and social obligations of talent developers. The
     order of the eleven values described in the code indicates priority, with “the rights
     and dignities” of the individual followed by the development of “human potential.”
     The code also addresses issues related to privacy, copyright law, confidentiality, and
     professional integrity. The Talent Management Institute provides a more compre-
     hensive list of twenty-eight values that prioritize “knowledge and compliance of the
                                                 Learning Engineering is Ethical | 205



law,” conduct with stakeholders, and upholding professional integrity followed by
broad ethical issues, such as avoiding biases and prejudice, reporting misconduct,
and assigning credit.9 However, many of these values appear to overlap (for example,
loyalty, prudence, care for clients, fair dealing with clients, and no misrepresen-
tation). Many other codes of conduct and guidelines are also available that reflect
extensions of organizational and business ethics.10

Engineering. Engineering fields also have professional codes that relate to general
and specific affordances. For instance, the IEEE has identified nine ethical principles,
including the safety and health of the public, avoidance of conflicts of interest, as
well as treating individuals fairly while avoiding harassment. Given the breadth of
the society’s membership, technical affordances are only considered at a very general
level. For instance, the fifth principle states that members should “seek, accept, and
offer honest criticism of technical work, to acknowledge and correct errors, to be
honest and realistic in stating claims or estimates based on available data, and to
credit properly the contributions of others.” Despite its generality, this principle and
others place a high value on technical competencies.
       In addition to its code of ethics, the IEEE has recently identified general
principles to ensure ethically aligned design.11 These principles are directed
toward developing trustworthy autonomous and intelligent systems that support
human values. They include human rights, well-being, data agency, effectiveness,
transparency, accountability, awareness of misuse, and competence of designers.
For instance, data agency requires that individuals have control over their identity
and can securely share their data. Transparency requires that the processes used
when processing data are discoverable; whereas awareness of misuse requires that
preventative steps be taken to minimize risk and misuse of data.
       Another notable set of principles has been developed by the Association for
Computing Machinery (ACM). The ACM has identified three broad sets of ethical
principles pertaining to general, professional, and leadership responsibilities.
Both the structure and the content of the ACM code are notable. For instance,
by specifying three levels of responsibility (as an individual, group member, and
leader), the code addresses the specific demands of different roles. The ACM also
adds several principles. For example, the principle of nonmaleficence (avoid harm)
and beneficence (help) are fairly standard, but they also recognize that ethical issues
can arise when modifying, retiring, and replacing systems.

Psychology. In that learning engineering is fundamentally about cognitive and
social psychological processes, learning engineers should also consider principles
206 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     developed in psychology. To address ethical concerns, the American Psychological
     Association (APA) has developed norms to encompass activities such as research,
     practice, and teaching. The APA has identified five equal principles that should
     “guide and inspire” the activities of psychologists.12 As Figure 7.1 demonstrates, each
     of these has applicability to learning engineering. For instance, learning engineers
     shouldn’t only consider design features in terms of the learning environment,
     learning algorithms, and the provision of feedback; they should also consider how
     these learning systems are implemented and the associated ethical implications.
     This is something we will return to below. Similar sets of norms have been adopted
     by other organizations (for example, the National Commission for Health Education
     Credentialing, 2020).13


     NATIONAL AND REGIONAL STANDARDS AND GUIDELINES

     In addition to organizational and professional codes of conduct, learning engineers
     must also be aware of national and international standards. Although a complete
     review of these is outside the scope of this chapter, a few notable frameworks are
     considered.

     Ethical AI. At a general level, UNESCO has developed recommendations for the
     ethics of artificial intelligence noting that “living in digitalizing societies requires new
     educational practices, the need for ethical reflection, critical thinking, responsible
     design practices, and new skills, given the implications for the labor market and
     employability.” 14 They identify four core values:

           1. Respecting, protecting, and promoting human dignity, human rights, and
              fundamental freedoms
           2. Recognizing and promoting environment and ecosystem flourishing
           3. Ensuring diversity and inclusiveness
           4. Living in harmony and peace

     These values inform ten principles of systems design, including human oversight
     and determination, transparency and explainability, awareness and literacy, and
     multi-stakeholder and adaptive governance and collaboration.

     Ethical use of personal information. Perhaps the most noteworthy standards for
     legal and ethical use of data are provided in the General Data Protection Regulation
     (GDPR), a regulation in European Union law on data protection and privacy in the
     EU and the European Economic Area. While it’s law in the EU, it’s also being used
                                                       Learning Engineering is Ethical | 207

FIGURE 7.1. Excerpts from the APA principles and their applicability to learning engineers
208 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     as a guideline for ethical treatment of personal data in other countries. In the United
     States, the state of California adopted the California Consumer Privacy Act that has
     similar provisions to GDPR.
            A significant issue raised in this framework is the so-called “right to be forgot-
     ten.” The guideline states that “[a] data subject should have the right to have personal
     data concerning him or her rectified and a ‘right to be forgotten’ . . . ” 15 Similar
     requirements have been echoed in learning engineering, in terms of standards for
     integrated / comprehensive learner records.
            Governmental and nongovernmental organizations have also suggested ethi-
     cal design principles. For instance, the United Kingdom developed Government De-
     sign Principles that include considering users’ needs, using available data to inform
     design, consistency (rather than uniformity), ensuring broad accessibility of design,
     and understanding the context of use.16 Similar approaches have been adopted else-
     where too. For instance, the community-driven Principles for Digital Development
     defines broadly applicable tenets including closing knowledge gaps in communities
     and presenting data in an easily accessible format as well as diverse, responsible,
     and rigorous data collection and use practices.17 Finally, perhaps the most innovative
     example is the Guiding Principles of the Internet of Education. The group who de-
     veloped the principles frames their approach as an “ongoing conversation across a
     global community of practice.” 18 The ten principles are:

           1. One open internet of education
           2. Distributed governance
           3. Effective collaboration
           4. Open standards
           5. Open participation
           6. Proportional influence
           7. Diversity, equity, and inclusion
           8. Data dignity
           9. Agency and access control
           10. Life-long learning

     These principles create a framework that aligns with many learning engineering eth-
     ical ideals, such as open standards and participation, proportional influence (number
     and diversity of learners), the promotion of life-long learning, and decentralized
     control to support sharing between stakeholders.
                                                Learning Engineering is Ethical | 209




                                       !#"
Ethical Sense-Making for Learning Engineering
An ethical sense-making approach to learning engineering can be defined by princi-
ples that incorporate actionable strategies.

  ► Design requires ethical sense-making. Designing ethical systems and
      conditions for learning requires we acknowledge that the environments we
      work in are often ambiguous. Even when we’re familiar with their features,
      we must question our assumptions. As designers, we must consider both the
      affordances that liberate users and the anti-affordances that constrain their
      actions. We must also consider whether specific groups of learners will
      experience more rewards or costs than others. Ethical sense-making should
      be promoted through design, empowering users to think clearly and critically.
  ► Design affects communities. Engineering designs are human artifacts.
      They’re both tools and symbols. Learning engineering teams must consid-
      er the impact of their designs on communities. Community engagement is
      essential. Understanding how a community sees itself, how they see others,
      and the community’s needs, as well as their deep sociocultural and linguistic
      traditions, takes time, respect, humility, and deference.
  ► Learning engineering affects individuals. Designs affect individual users,
      who are each a unique combination of traits, strategies, and personal histories.
      Designs must promote learners’ understanding of the systems and processes
      they’re using. Designers must ensure that users can critically challenge both
      technical and ethical affordances of a design by promoting autonomy.
  ► Learning engineers are community leaders. Depending on the needs of a
      community, the role of a learning engineering team will vary. Regardless,
      designers must acknowledge that they are responsible for their design—its
      use, disuse, and misuse. They must be accountable to their stakeholders.
  ► Learning engineers will experience conflicts of commitments. Participants
      in the learning engineering process are embedded in multiple communities.
      As professionals, we have commitments to our professions. As employees, we
      have commitments to our organizations. As educators, we have commitments
      to our students. As persons, we have commitments to our community. Conflicts
      of interest are inevitable. They must be declared and mitigated. Team members
      must be conflict competent.
210 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



         ► Learning engineers are advocates. Wherever possible, designs and algo-
              rithms within a learning engineering solution should be transparent. Both the
              affordances and anti-affordances of a design should be made clear to users.
              Transparency always will be relative to the competency of the individual and
              the community. Designers must be advocates and leaders for social and ethical
              issues. They must engage in knowledge translation between communities.



      FRAMEWORK FOR APPLYING ETHICAL SENSE-MAKING
      IN THE LEARNING ENGINEERING PROCESS

      An approach for ethical sense-making in learning engineering is shown in Figure
      7.2. First, the APA’s principles are adopted as a starting point because they address
      the kinds of situations learning engineers will encounter in most experimental and
      applied settings. Next, these principles are applied to the stages of the learning
      engineering process. The corresponding matrix requires that teams consider how
      each ethical principle is presented at each stage by asking a series of corresponding
      questions. (See Chapter 17 for the detailed application of this approach.)


         Principle            Challenge       Creation      Implementation    Investigation

    Beneficence and
    Nonmaleficence

    Fidelity and
    Responsibility

    Integrity


    Justice


    Respect for
    People

   FIGURE 7.2. Sense-making Ethical Evaluation Matrix (SEEM) for Learning Engineering
   CC-BY Jordan Richard Schoenherr
                                                 Learning Engineering is Ethical | 211




                                        !$"
Ethical Examples from Health Care Education
When people think about the health care professions, white lab coats, stethoscopes,
prostrate patients, and a caricature of the Hippocratic Oath (do no harm)19 likely
come to mind. Beyond these stereotypes, the health care professions are defined by
specific professional competencies and guided by numerous legal and professional
standards. For instance, the Canadian Medical Association Code of Ethics defines
a set of virtues (compassion, honesty, humility, integrity, and prudence) that should
inform decision-making inclusive of patients’ values and experiences. Health care
professions are expected to use this code to inform every area of practice, including
medical education.
       Health care and health care education often involve uniquely challenging sit-
uations that illustrate boundary conditions 20 for ethical sense-making (see Figure
7.3). For example, medical training sometimes requires the use of donated human
remains.21 Here, values and ethics range from codes of conduct (see the left side of
Figure 7.4) to more symbolic gestures of respect. For instance, the University of Ot-
tawa has erected monuments to donors (refer to the right side of Figure 7.4), holds an
annual commemorative service for families of donors,22 and publicly acknowledges
these contributions with medals of recognition. Although symbolic, “loved ones,
who sometimes feel their altruistic gifts go unnoticed, cherish this concrete form of
appreciation.” 23
       Educators in the health care professions might be aware of ethical issues in
ways that may not be immediately clear to other teachers and trainers. Even so, they
can still miss some ethical considerations. On occasion, I sometimes collaborate
with health educators. Two case studies from that experience illustrate examples
of ethical blind spots in education related to learner participation in studies and the
recognition of the contributions of others.


LEARNER PARTICIPATION IN STUDIES

In health professions education, activities are often first designed as a means to de-
velop a competency (a hypothetical set of knowledge and skills) in learners. Educa-
tors might decide to conduct educational studies as a secondary objective. However,
I’ve found that they didn’t always perceive the ethical affordances of the situation
ETHICAL BOUNDARY CONDITIONS
The term boundary condition is used in the social sciences as well as in the physical
sciences, mathematics, and engineering. Boundary conditions specify situations in which
relations between variables change depending on the values of other variables. Boundary
condition in ethics reflects the extremes of object or entity. Defining boundaries in the case
of categories of objects (rather than physical entities) can be extremely problematic. For
instance, when determining a boundary, we might attempt to differentiate between ethical
and unethical behavior by identifying a set of criteria, such as what causes more good than
harm or what contains more features associated with good or bad behavior.

In practice, “good” and “bad” behaviors overlap such that in one set of circumstances an
action might be correct, while in another, the same behaviors might be incorrect—like stealing
to support one’s family versus stealing to advance one’s position. In practice, this creates a
region of uncertainty that can result in ambiguity and misclassification, as shown in Figure
7.1. Depending on the individual and circumstances, this region can be larger or smaller.


                                        REGION OF
                                       UNCERTAINTY




             UNETHICAL                                              ETHICAL

   FIGURE 7.3.A. Simplified general model of ethical decision-making



In mathematics and engineering,
                                            UNETHICAL ACTIONS
the boundary conditions are a set                                                          BOUNDARIES
of conditions that must be satisfied
to solve a boundary value problem.
A solution to a boundary value                         ETHICAL ACTIONS
problem is a solution that also
satisfies the boundary conditions.
Figure 7.2 illustrates a boundary                                UNETHICAL ACTIONS
value problem.


                     FIGURE 7.3.B. Illustration of a boundary value problem for ethical actions
                                                       Learning Engineering is Ethical | 213



in the same way that I did. For instance, in a few cases, I had to ask colleagues to
request an ethics review for their educational studies.
       For these educators, if education research didn’t include patients, research
ethics approval often consisted of a short review and a waiver. My concerns also
stemmed from my background. In contrast to many of my medical colleagues, I
focused on obtaining informed consent and recognizing that the learner might feel
pressured to participate in a study or simply fail to withdraw from a study because
they were already enrolled in the course.
       Thus, while informed consent was a principle that both my colleagues and I
shared, I found that those educators didn’t understand its application in this context.
Much like the notion of behavioral traces, they viewed learners’ data as byproducts of
the educational activity. Consequently, they considered the ethical implications with
a different disciplinary lens. In addressing these concerns, my colleagues became
aware of the ethical implications. Thus, whether working in a multidisciplinary team
or with diverse groups of stakeholders, identifying issues related to values and ethics
can be quite revealing.




            Code of Conduct
     in the Anatomy Laboratory
  …2. Fulfill the expectations of the donors and
  honor their wishes by studying the bodies with
  purpose and respect. Treat the donated bodies
  and prosections as you would wish your own body,
  or that of a member of your family, to be treated.
  3. All donated bodies and prosections should be
  handled with respect. Neither the donated bodies
  nor any parts thereof are to be positioned or
  displayed in inappropriate, comical or obscene
  manners.
  …6. NO unauthorized photographs from mobile
  devices or digital camera are ever to be taken…




FIGURE 7.4. Excerpts from McGill anatomy laboratory code of conduct (left).
Monument commemorating individuals who donated their remains (right). Inscription:
“In memory of those who have generously given their body for the advancement of
science and medical education.”
Credit: Faculty of Medicine, University of Ottawa
214 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     RECOGNIZING THE CONTRIBUTIONS OF OTHERS

     Ethics in instructional design and learning engineering requires recognizing
     the contributions of all stakeholders. In medical simulation centers, many tasks
     use existing training systems. (See Figure 7.5, left.) Training sessions that use
     these materials tend to focus on the development of team decision-making and
     communication skills. Instructional designers in health professions education often
     assumed that the use of these technologies was sufficient to promote the acquisition
     of complex skills. However, many novel training tasks were created to address more
     foundational skills. In many cases, elegant training solutions were developed by
     technicians that worked at the simulation center at the request of clinicians. Many
     of these training tasks were exceptionally novel, using a variety of materials and
     techniques, for example, multi-layer synthetic tissue models with shampoo packets
     to model abscesses. (See Figure 7.5, right.) In many cases, technicians described the
     design and developmental process in a manner that suggested their autonomy, with
     little to no input from the clinicians. However, other than interpersonal expressions
     of thanks, technicians were often not formally acknowledged as contributors in
     instructional design in publications or presentations.
             During a formal presentation about the ethics of contribution in research,
     I observed these value conflicts directly. I used the lack of acknowledgment as a
     talking point to illustrate how junior team members, students, and technicians are




     FIGURE 7.5. Research assistant demonstrating the simulation environment with a
     mannequin located in a full-scale model of an emergency room (left). Sample of a used
     synthetic tissue abscess model (right). The model consists of synthetic skin (rubber),
     foam, shampoo packets, and duct tape and staples to secure the layers. Learners
     probe the sample to find the location of the abscess, make an incision, palpate, and
     clean the synthetic wound.
     Credit: Jordan Richard Schoenherr
                                               Learning Engineering is Ethical | 215



often overlooked. A clinician sitting in the audience quickly put up their hand and
confidently asked in a rhetorical manner “Why would we put their [technicians’]
name on a paper? We pay them.” In the case of this clinician, thanks and remuneration
were appropriate forms of recognition for the contributions of technicians whereas
authorship was reserved for others. Following the discussion, I was tentatively
approached by other clinicians. They thanked me for raising and discussing concerns
about the need to recognize team members even though it was a taboo topic. Similar
concerns can also arise in student-educator projects wherein differing expectations
can lead to inappropriate recognition.24


                                       !%"
Ethical Sense-Making
The existence of a plurality of ethical frameworks, guidelines, and codes high-
lights the fact that ethics requires more than mere compliance with a set, or sets,
of principles. Learning engineers must interpret these principles, resolve conflicts
between different (often incommensurable) values, identify ethically relevant fea-
tures of a situation, and consider how relational structures can impact learning and
decision-making. They must engage in an ethical sense-making process and be con-
scious of the ethical dimensions of instructional design.25
       There’s a great temptation to address ethical concerns after we’ve designed
a course or instructional technology. Research suggests that we often rationalize
our ethical intuitions.26 Ethical sense-making requires considering moral reasoning
abilities, the effects of stress and divided attention of moral judgments, and
situational factors that determine what values are made accessible by features of the
environment.27 A few general features of ethical sense-making should be considered
when engaging in learning engineering in that they affect the judgments of learners,
instructional designers, and academic administrators:
   ► Moral development is thought to progress through three broad stages:
      children are driven by punishment and reward, teens and young adults
      by adherence to social conventions or those of society, whereas adults can
      use ethical principles. Ethical principles can reflect a focus on utility, de-
      ontological principles, or care. These are the kinds of principles embodied
      in professional standards and guidelines. However, not everyone successfully
      progresses through these stages.28
216 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



        ► Failures of attention and time limitations can lead us to neglect values
           and ethics.29 For instance, researchers suggest that when individuals are com-
           pleting a task and don’t believe their performance is being monitored, they’re
           more likely to attempt to deceive evaluators. However, reminders of codes of
           conduct immediately before a task can increase academic integrity.30 Sharing
           is also promoted when individuals believe they’re being monitored 31 whereas
           the experience of anonymity can increase in antisocial behaviors.32
        ► Similar norms and conventions are evidenced across cultures. Cross-
           cultural values fall into two distinct clusters based on individual-focused (care
           and fairness / proportionality) and group-focused moral motivations (authority,
           loyalty, and sanctity / purity).33 Contextual factors and group influence can
           determine which norms are relevant at any given time.34 However, individuals
           often tend to devalue members of other groups when considering ethical
           responses.
        ► Psychological studies have also demonstrated that even if we apply certain
           norms to our group, we do not necessarily apply them to others. Learners
           and educators use social categories and identities to understand the learning
           environment.35 The mere mention of sex or race before a task can lower a
           learner’s performance. This is further supported by studies of academic
           impostor syndrome that demonstrate some learners don’t feel they deserve
           their achievements.36
        ► Engaging in effortful reasoning doesn’t guarantee moral behavior. Rea-
           soning can be associated with less ethical behavior.37 These findings suggest
           that individuals often rationalize choices based on their affective responses.
        ► Empathy is an important factor in determining moral judgments.38 Fac-
           tors that promote empathy tend to increase helping behavior. In contrast, when
           individuals are directed toward thinking about abstract values (for example,
           monetary incentives), they tend to request and give less assistance. For in-
           stance, research suggests that framing a task as a business game 39 reduces
           collaboration relative to when a task is framed as a community game.40
            Learning engineering teams must take these factors into consideration. Fac-
     tors that decrease ethical behavior, such as anonymity, divided attention, ambigu-
     ous ethical standards, and rationalization, can present challenges for both learners
     and learning engineers. In that many of these biases might be implicit for learners
     and educators, they require specific consideration during the design process.41 By
                                                 Learning Engineering is Ethical | 217



adopting an ethical sense-making approach to design, we can take these factors into
consideration.


                                        !&"
Ethical Examples from Everyday Education
I was once approached by a student who wanted to discuss concerns they had about
the lecture content. In the lecture, I presented a brief (anonymized) story of an
acquaintance of mine who struggled with a particular mental health issue to illustrate
the diversity and complexity of human behavior and the ubiquity of such concerns.
When they spoke to me, the student stated that they felt the discussion misrepresented
the mental health issue, making reference to inappropriate, normative standards.
Moreover, they felt that the discussion of psychological disorders was inappropriate
in academia because it might cause students psychological distress. These kinds of
discussions are growing in number as the roles of learners and educators are being
negotiated. They illustrate the need to understand the value of ethical sense-making
in the daily business of academia.
       At the core of ethical sense-making is the need to understand context. Simply
identifying a conflict in values isn’t sufficient for resolving the issue. In this case,
further discussion provided considerable insight into the source of the student’s
concerns. First, the student indicated that they had personal, ongoing experience
dealing with mental health issues. They acknowledged that these particular issues
were emotionally charged. Second, they had in fact misattributed something stated
in the textbook to my lectures, a general psychological phenomenon referred to as
failure of source monitoring. After I listened to and acknowledged the student’s
perspective, the student thanked me for hearing what they had to say, noting that
not all lecturers had done so in their experience. The student acknowledged their
reaction and that they understood my intent. I also reinforced that students should
feel comfortable raising their concerns, additionally noting that these topics were
within the scope of the course and should be addressed in academia.
       In this case, the simple act of gathering more information to understand some-
one else’s perspective, to empathize with the student, promoted interpersonal respect
and allowed for the discussion of sensitive issues. By listening, educators not only
support dialogue, but they can also gain an understanding of how learners approach
the material and adapt their courses. This incident also illustrates that educators
can’t be complacent in terms of how material is presented to students. They must
218 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     address aspects of the textbook or other learning resources and clearly distinguish
     between personal and academic perspectives of both learners and educators. Beyond
     these issues, instructors also must ensure that they take into consideration poten-
     tial differences in cultural norms for interactions between students and instructors.
     What might be perceived as disrespectful behavior (for example, question course
     content, curt emails) might not be perceived as such by the student.


                                             !'"
     Ethics in Assessment and Design
     Learning engineering requires a framework that addresses both the underlying
     technical and value-laden dimensions of design. This framework must be applicable
     to traditional approaches to pedagogy and assessment as well as more advanced
     learning technologies that reflect adaptive or intelligent learning systems.


     ETHICAL ASSESSMENTS

     Samuel Messick developed a general value framework for use in assessment.42 (See
     Figure 7.6.) He acknowledged that the development of training and assessment in-
     struments has always been informed by values. In contrast to earlier frameworks,
     Messick assumed that construct validity was the principal means to evaluate the
     utility of instructional design and assessment. The foundation of assessment reflects
     an instrument’s construct validity in terms of the amount and kind of evidence as
     well as whether it reflects a representative sample of the information and processes
     associated with the competency of interest.
            Concurrently, we must also consider whether an assessment instrument is
     suited for its purpose. For instance, a five hundred–item assessment might have con-
     siderable construct validity supporting its use but might not feasibly be implemented
     within an organizational context due to the time required for its completion. Messick
     also stressed the need to understand the broad value implications of an assessment
     instrument. For instance, high or low scores could result in promotion or termination
     within an organization or in a student passing or failing a course. These reflect value
     judgments about whether an employee or student is good or bad. Finally, Messick
     suggests that we go further to consider the social consequences of our assessment.
     For instance, a test can be used to classify and label someone with a learning disabil-
     ity that could result in changes in their social status.
                                                     Learning Engineering is Ethical | 219




                                 Test Interpretation              Test Use

        Evidential            Construct Validity         Construct Validity +
        Basis                                            Relevance / Utility

        Consequential         Construct Validity +       Construct Validity +
        Basis                 Value Implications         Relevance / Utility +
                                                         Value Implications +
                                                         Social Consequences

       FIGURE 7.6. Facets of Construct Validity
       Adapted from Messick (1995)



VALUE-BASED DESIGN

Complementing value-based approaches to psychometrics are valued-based
approaches to design. Here, we consider four broad approaches that attempt to
integrate user-centered design frameworks: value sensitive design, reflective design,
design justice, and anti-discrimination design.

Value Sensitive Design. Friedman and colleagues define value sensitive design
as “a theoretically grounded approach to the design of technology that accounts for
human values in a principled and comprehensive manner throughout the design pro-
cess.” 43 Values must be included throughout the iteration of the design process (out-
lined in Chapter 1), which incorporates conceptual, empirical, and technical aspects
of the process. According to value sensitive design, the three kinds of investigations
can be defined as:
      • Conceptual: Specifying the fundamental values that are relevant to
        design, thereby providing a common point of comparison for all members
        of a design team
      • Empirical: Using qualitative and quantitative methods adopted from
        the social sciences to examine how technology works within the
        implementation context
      • Technical: Examining the extent to which specific technologies support
        or interfere with the realization of human values (retroactive analysis) or
        design technologies to support values that have been identified within the
        conceptual design phase
220 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            The value sensitive design approach incorporates many elements of ethical
     sense-making in that it considers the ethical affordances of a system. These aspects
     of design have become increasingly important given that the exploitation of users’
     patterns of behavior has become increasingly apparent.44 Although the practical
     outcomes of adopting a value sensitive design approach might align with ethical
     sense-making, designers should make more deliberate attempts to identify how so-
     cial and cognitive processes are impacted by specific design decisions.

     Reflective Design. An alternative approach, referred to as reflective design,
     stresses “analysis of the ways in which technologies reflect and perpetuate uncon-
     scious cultural assumptions, with design, building, and evaluation of new computing
     devices that reflect alternative possibilities.” 45 This approach consists of six princi-
     ples and design strategies shown in Figure 7.7.


                       PRINCIPLES                                    STRATEGIES
      Reflection is required to critically identify   Ensure that users can take responsibility for
      what values are embedded within a               and control the meaning-making process
      design, what values are comparatively           when engaged with socio-technical systems
      neglected, and what methods can be
      used to address marginalized values
      Reflection should be used by designers          Engage users in the design process
      to understand their assumptions and             to develop a digital sca!olding that
      role within the design process                  facilitates their comprehension of new
                                                      concepts and new system features
      Designers should facilitate                     Use feedback collected from or about
      users’ understanding of their                   users as input into the system or to
      relationship with technologies                  help guide the evaluation of a system
      Skepticism should be promoted, enabling         Users’ feedback should be collected
      users to critically consider how technology     and used through the design
      a!ects their choices and behavior               process, rather than at the end
      Acknowledge that reflection and                 Systems should be used in an
      action are not mutually exclusive               analogous manner to experiments
      Mutual engagement of designers and              Consider a variety of metaphors
      users in the development and use of             adopted from di!erent disciplines
      technologies can facilitate reflection          to understand the design space

     FIGURE 7.7. Principles and strategies for reflective design
     Adapted from Sengers et al. (2005)



           In its critical approach to design, the reflective design process embodies a num-
     ber of features of ethical sense-making. Rather than adopting a designer-centered
                                                Learning Engineering is Ethical | 221



approach wherein a designer “[stands] aloof, benignly passing down opportunities
for reflection,” reflective design suggests that technologies should be designed to
engage designers and their users in a continuous process of meaning-making. Thus,
the basic features of ethical sense-making can be used in the process of reflective
design to direct designers to vulnerabilities they face in engaging users in effortful
reflection on their relationships with technology.

Design Justice. Perhaps the most critical approaches to design are the design
justice principles (also discussed in Chapter 3). Described as a living document, the
principles are directed toward reformulating and restructuring the design process,
including the role of the designer and other stakeholders.46 Designers are referred
to as facilitators rather than experts and assume that the lived experience of all
stakeholders makes them experts. A central feature of the theory that informs de-
sign justice is that design can reinforce existing biases. For instance, when learners
are forced to choose a response on a multiple-choice question, a design necessarily
limits the correct response, failing to consider why learners selected an alternative
response. In many cases, learners might simply be wrong. However, the response
alternatives selected by an instructional designer might reflect cultural biases.
Within clinical psychology, the pathologizing of homosexuality in early versions of
the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual presents such an example.47 If learners are
presented this information in a course and a corresponding question on a test, they
would be required to respond in a manner that runs counter to their beliefs.
      Instead, much like reflective design, design justice assumes that the goal
of design should be to empower, sustain, and heal communities. Consequently,
communities should be seen as the leaders and controllers of a collaborative design
process that attempts to use existing approaches that are well-adapted to the commu-
nity and to offer new, nonexploitative design solutions. These principles represent an
important framework. However, like all frameworks that have a number of values,
conflicts can arise.

Anti-discrimination Design. The final framework that we will consider here is
referred to as anti-discrimination design. Like design justice, it takes as its focus
discriminatory practices that might be embedded within the design. It additionally
provides a useful typology of three classes of design features:

   ► Non-Affordances and Poor Affordances: Non-affordances reflect a failure
      to include affordances that are apparent to the user. Rather than reflecting a
      design failure, they reflect an absence of specific features. In contrast, poor
222 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           affordances are perceived by the user but lack a clear identification of what the
           features do, for example, bad design.
        ► Discriminatory Non-Affordances and Poor Affordances: Non-affordances
           and poor affordances that result in a disadvantage (or advantage) given to
           a specific set of users. These issues arise when examining design from the
           users’ perspective. For instance, if auditory cues are used as an affordance, the
           design will disadvantage individuals with hearing impairment.
        ► Disaffordances and Dysaffordances: These issues arise when examining
           design from the users’ perspective. Disaffordances fail to recognize relevant
           user identity groups. Dysaffordances additionally require unaccommodated
           users to misrepresent themselves. For example, learners using an online
           learning application might be forced to select a specific disability, gender, or
           racial category due to an absence of other options.48

           Crucially, the anti-discrimination design framework also emphasizes the
     importance of stewardship associated with the role of designer or engineer. For
     instance, D. E. Wittkower notes that:

           . . . even if a developer team somehow were absolutely diverse,
           absolutely self-aware, and absolutely politicized, such a strategy for
           anti-discriminatory design wrongly implies that the socially privileged
           designer has neither ability nor responsibility to engage in anti-
           discriminatory design. In other words, a developer working on her own
           should not excuse herself from the responsibility to attempt to think
           through diverse user experiences.49

     VALUE-BASED DESIGN AND ETHICAL SENSE-MAKING

     At their core, value sensitive design, reflective design, design justice, and anti-dis-
     crimination design share features with an ethical sense-making approach. Namely,
     designers must consider the mental models of the members of the communities
     learning engineering will impact. Rather than limiting themselves to the techni-
     cal features that they, and users, perceive within a system, they must consider the
     ethically relevant features of a system. This requires understanding what learners
     and their community perceive as a value. Designers often will find themselves in
     ethically ambiguous or ambivalent situations. For instance, designers will potential-
     ly be faced with value conflicts, for example, communities might not wish to accept
     certain members, they might wish to promote values that are at odds with those of
                                              Learning Engineering is Ethical | 223



the designer, or human rights. Most troubling, designers might be faced with the
decision of compromising on certain values to assist with a community. By adopting
an approach based on ethical sense-making, instructional designers and learning
engineers can reflect on how they have included values within design as well as how
their design choices can create affordance and anti-affordance.




           Although learning engineers might
              simply believe that the design
              process ends once a product,
            instrument, or process has been
          completed, they must also consider
           how their innovations are adopted
                 by specific communities.
    • Ethical decision-making occurs at every learning engineering stage.

    • Learning engineers must consider values and ethics while selecting the
      content, pedagogical approaches, assessment strategies, and content
      delivery methods as well as throughout the learning engineering process,
      such as during design or experimentation. Many situations might seem
      ethically ambiguous or neutral in the absence of reflection.

    • Learning engineers must consider how the learning context can impact
      learning performance and perception. They must consider learner
      community values as well as the physical and social environments of the
      implementation context.

    • Professionals who engage in learning engineering are guided by
      professional codes of conduct as well as national and international
      guidelines that contain high-level ethical principles.

    • Learners and educators are continuously engaged in ethical sense-
      making, which is sensitive to factors including stress, divided attention,
      the social environment, and task framing. These can increase or
      decrease pro-social behavior and adherence to social norms.

    • Boundary conditions are used to specify situations in which relations
      between variables change depending on the values of other variables.
      Ethical boundary conditions establish gray areas where the same action
      may be ethical or unethical depending on contextual variables.

    • Ethical a!ordances are contextually relevant design features that comply
      with established ethical principles and respect the values of stakeholder
      communities. Anti-a!ordances are contextually relevant design features
      that fail to comply with established ethical principles or fail to respect the
      values of stakeholder communities.

    • Including values and ethics in design requires an understanding of
      the ethical a!ordances of a system. This requires operationalizing how
      learners interact with a system. Stakeholders should be engaged through
      all stages of development, implementation, and technology di!usion to
      ensure their concerns have been taken into account.



LEARNING ENGINEERING IS ETHICAL

KEY POINTS

                         TOOLS
                          SHORT PRACTICAL CHAPTERS
WITH EASY-REFERENCE CHECKLISTS, GUIDES, + TEMPLATES
CHAPTER 8


Tools for Understanding
the Challenge
by Erin Czerwinski and Jim Goodell



Learning engineering is a repeatable process that starts with a challenge.
This chapter introduces practical tools for understanding those learning
engineering challenges to be solved by a learning engineering team.


The challenges learning engineers address are focused on helping every targeted
learner be successful in reaching learning goals. This might include creating things
that help learners make progress toward a goal (for example, refer to the section in
Chapter 2 on addressing learners’ zones of proximal development and scaffolding)
or things that help targeted learners overcome barriers as illustrated in Figure 8.1.
       Learning engineering teams are often asked to examine learning conditions
to determine what factors could be adjusted to improve learning for one or more
targeted populations of learners. This chapter provides tools to help elaborate on




                                 ET
                             ASS                  BARRIER




 Understand           Understand the CHALLENGES in context             Understand
 the LEARNERS                 (barriers and assets)                      the GOAL


FIGURE 8.1. Learning engineering challenges can be thought of as barriers
that must be overcome—and assets to use to assist—on the way to a goal


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-13
232 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     what needs to be learned, the conditions that might need to be addressed, and the
     complexities of that learning. The four tools include task analysis, the five whys
     technique, cause and effect analysis, and failure mode and effects analysis.
            Supporting learning so that every targeted learner can be successful requires
     knowing quite a bit about who the learners are; what they already know, are capable
     of, and have experienced; what resources they bring to their learning; what their
     interests and goals are and what will keep them engaged; the context in which the
     learning is to happen and the resources and constraints associated with those con-
     texts; the capabilities, practices, preferences, and content knowledge of those helping
     the learners to learn (such as teachers, family, and peers); what tends to be difficult
     about learning the new skills; and more. Consequentially, to fully understand the
     learning engineering challenge, consider using the kinds of tools discussed in this
     chapter in conjunction with tools from other chapters, including:

           •   Chapter 9, Tools from the Learning Sciences
           •   Chapter 12, Human-Centered Design Tools
           •   Chapter 15, Tools for Learner Motivation
           •   Chapter 16, Implementation Tools (to understand conditions and constraints)
           •   Chapter 18, Data Analysis Tools



      Analysis is the study we do in order to figure out what to do.
                        – Allison Rossett and Kendra Sheldon 1



     Task Analysis

     Task analysis is used when a targeted skill isn’t already precisely defined. This ap-
     proach is frequently used to unpack the component skills and knowledge needed to
     accomplish a task.


     Various task analysis methods attempt to unpack the declarative, procedural, and
     strategic knowledge a person must have to perform a task in a given context (defined
     by a set of conditions and available tools). Behavioral task analysis identifies the
     observable or directly measurable behaviors. Cognitive task analysis is used for
                                        Tools for Understanding the Challenge | 233



tasks that require high-level reasoning, such as decision-making, problem solving,
or judgments. Task analyses should also uncover necessary mindsets, attitudes, or
habits of mind / practice. Training expert Donald Clark identifies the following kinds
of information captured during a task analysis: 2
      •   Conditions: Tools or equipment needed and the task environment
      •   Performance Measure: How well must it be performed?
      •   Frequency: How often is the task performed, for example, hourly or daily?
      •   Difficulty: Use a standard scale, such as from one to five
      •   Importance: How important is this compared to performers’ other tasks?
      •   Steps: Knowledge and logical steps for performing the task
Uncovered information might also include:
      • Dependent Attitudes: What habits of practice or thinking are helpful?
      • Transferable Skills: What skills used in other contexts can be applied?
Task analyses should identify various methods a person might use to successfully
perform the task using different sets of tools in different contexts (in this way
predicting a set of learner differences and different ways a learner might carry out the
task). Learners come with deficiencies in what they know; they also come with funds
of knowledge—things they already know and are capable of. A single, rigid solution
path or a deficiency-based learning experience can be unmotivating for learners
who have other ways of knowing and / or doing. Performance task analysis includes
identifying the variety of ways the task can be carried out, the variety of ways each
part of the task can be carried out, and the different prior knowledge and skills
learners might engage to learn the task. The most effective learning experiences offer
learners opportunities to learn and demonstrate a skill using multiple approaches.
They are developed with an understanding of learners’ funds of knowledge as well
as what they haven’t yet attained.


HOW TO USE

The analysis process involves interviewing and / or observing a variety of people
who do the task competently and / or analyzing documentation of the tasks being
performed. In an academic context, the list of tasks may be defined by academic
standards. In a training context, the process of task analysis starts by analyzing the
job or role. Let’s say we already know the task, for example, training employees to
estimate the cost of a new carpet installation for an irregularly shaped room. We’ll
need to unpack the task into its steps. Then, for each step, we inquire about what a
234 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     person must:
             •   Know prior to doing the step
             •   Perceive during, for example, the set of rectangles making up the room
             •   Attend to during, for example, the customer, care with customer property
             •   Do during the step
     While interviewing or observing someone doing the task, ask them to tell you:
             •   What they’re thinking at each step
             •   What they’re observing
             •   When they make a decision, what assumptions they make
             •   How they know their decisions are right and the work product is correct
     A template like the one below can be useful. Also see Chapters 6 and 18 for information
     on learning curve analysis; effective task analyses should specify tasks in enough
     granularity to support future learning curve analyses. Learning engineering best
     practices call for competency definitions with data-justified specificity, meaning we
     want to be able to prove that the competency is at the atomic level with data.


     PERFORMANCE TASK ANALYSIS TOOL TEMPLATE
     Task:

        Task Step             Know           Perceive        Attend To           Do




     FIGURE 8.2. Performance Task Analysis template


     Five Whys Technique

     Based on some understanding of current conditions, use this tool to identify why
     the learning is less than optimal. Then use the root cause that is identified as the
     next challenge to be solved by the learning engineering team.


     The five whys technique was developed by Sakichi Toyoda, the founder of Toyota,
                                           Tools for Understanding the Challenge | 235



in the 1930s. Toyota executives would go and see what was happening on the shop
floor (rather than just think about solving a problem from the boardroom), and then
they’d use analysis techniques to examine the issues they identified. The five whys
is a root-cause analysis technique,* and it works well with other human-centered
design approaches. Learning engineers might use the five whys to figure out why
learners are failing at a given task or are challenged by a particular topic.3


HOW TO USE

In its simplest form, use this procedure: (1) Identify a problem, (2) ask why?, and
(3) based on that answer ask why? again. (Repeat up to five times—or more—as
needed.) Sometimes the root cause is discovered in fewer or more than five steps.
It’s helpful to unpack as much as possible. Don’t stop at the symptoms of problems.

Example: The vehicle will not start.
      1.   Why? The battery is dead.
      2.   Why? The alternator isn’t functioning.
      3.   Why? The alternator belt has broken.
      4.   Why? The alternator belt was beyond its useful life and not replaced.
      5.   Why? The vehicle wasn’t maintained to the suggested service schedule.
Note that sometimes depending on where you are in the learning engineering pro-
cess, you may have access to data or research that can inform the answers to your
root cause questions. This is exemplified in the next example where a root cause was
discovered with analysis of data and interviewing some learners to better understand
why they were having difficulties.

Example: Students are failing multiplication word problems on a standardized test.
      1. Why? Students are missing about half of the problems on the tests even
         though they performed better on in-class multiplication word problems.
      2. Why? Based on the data, students are missing the same sets of problems.
      3. Why? Examining the troublesome problems reveals that they all prompt
         students to “find the product” of two or more numbers.



* Caution: Not all problems have a single root cause. For more complex problems the method
  must be repeated asking a different sequence of questions each time. In those cases, use this
  method with the “fishbone diagrams” tool discussed later in this chapter.
236 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           4. Why? Talking to the students reveals that they don’t know what the word
              “product” means in mathematics. (Note, during this step, it’s important
              to go talk with learners, just as the Toyota executives visited the shop
              floor to understand what was going on. A simple template like the one
              that follows may be used to assist with this.)
           5. Why? The teacher succeeded in ensuring that students in the class were
              proficient in multiplication as an operation but failed to expose them to
              the terminology used in the standardized assessment. In-class practice
              problems before the test didn’t use the word “product.”


     THE FIVE WHYS TOOL TEMPLATE
     Condition:

            Why?


            Why?


            Why?


            Why?


            Why?


     FIGURE 8.3. Five Whys template



     Cause and E!ect (Fishbone) Analysis

     This technique helps explore all possible causes of a problem. Use it to investigate
     the root causes of more complex or multifaceted challenges.


     The five whys technique is designed to help identify a single condition or root cause
     that, if changed, could result in an improvement or desired outcome. In contrast,
     cause and effect analysis, developed by Kaoru Ishikawa in the 1960s, is better suited
     for those situations where the multitude of root causes that might be preventing an
                                        Tools for Understanding the Challenge | 237



ideal outcome. This technique is also called fishbone analysis * because a completed
diagram can look like the skeleton of a fish. The technique is fully defined in the
1990 book, Introduction to Quality Control. 4


HOW TO USE 5

      1.   Start by asking why an ideal outcome isn’t being reached.
      2.   Instead of just one, consider multiple reasons.
      3.   Record each answer as a branch in the diagram.
      4.   For each branch ask why?, and record the answer as a subsequent branch
           until the various root causes are found.

Note, in the following example, interacting with the learners is essential to getting at
reasons for difficulties. You may need to use another tool, such as the five whys, to
get at the root causes of each of the identified difficulties.

Example: Some of our learners don’t automatically know that ¼ is greater than ⅛.
“Why don’t learners know the relative value of fractions?” Answering the question
may require some investigation, such as asking learners or doing follow-up assess-
ments. Let’s say we asked learners, “Why do you think ¼ is less than ⅛?” and they
provided these responses:

      “No one taught me about numbers with a line between them.” (From
      this we might theorize the learner wasn’t exposed to the idea of fractions
      or didn’t retain the concept from a prior learning experience.)

      “Because 8 is greater than 4.” (From this we can infer that their mental
      model for quantity is imperfect and must be expanded.)

      “Oh, I was wrong, I was looking at the numbers on the bottom and for-
      got it was a fraction.” (In this case, we might infer that the learner has
      been exposed to fractions but hasn’t yet sufficiently retained that knowl-
      edge or developed skills to consistently apply it.)

Starting with these three initial branches might result in a fishbone diagram as shown
in Figure 8.4.A, and based on this, we might identify different kinds of solutions as
shown in Figure 8.4.B.



* Also known as Cause and Effect, Ishikawa, Herringbone, and Fishikawa Diagrams.
238 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                 No one taught me                         The learner was
                                 about numbers                            introduced to fractions but
                                 with a line in                           didn’t retain the knowledge
                                 between


       CHALLENGE                                                          The learner hasn’t been
                                                                          introduced to fractions
        Learners don’t       Because 8 is more than 4
        know that ¼ is                                       The learner’s mental model
        greater than ⅛                                       doesn’t include the symbolic
                                                             representation of values less
                                                             than one as fractions

           WHY?                  Oh, I was wrong: I
                                                                          The learner needs more
                                 was looking at the
                                                                          exposure and practice to
                                 numbers on the
                                                                          consistently recognize and
                                 bottom and forgot

                                 it was a fraction
                                                                          fractions


     FIGURE 8.4.A. Example fishbone diagram (step 1)

                                                      …didn’t retain
                                                      the knowledge            CONCEPT

                             taught me…                                   • An orange cut into

     FIGURE 8.4.B. Example fishbone diagram (step 2)
                                      Tools for Understanding the Challenge | 239



ALTERNATIVE FORM

The fishbone analysis template in Figure 8.5 is adapted from a model by the Carnegie
Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching. It takes a slightly different approach,
for example, creating main category branches from the problem statement. Use it
when you want to organize potential causes by category, for example, separating
learner motivational or behavioral causes from learner cognition causes.




                                CATEGORY                              CATEGORY

                                   cause
                             cause
   PROBLEM
  STATEMENT
                                   cause
                                        cause

                                CATEGORY                              CATEGORY

FIGURE 8.5. Alternative fishbone diagram template
240 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Failure Mode and E!ects Analysis

     Use this technique to prioritize what challenge (or challenges) to address. This
     technique helps you look at all of the things that possibly could go wrong; it’s most
     useful at the beginning of the create stage to uncover and address potential prob-
     lems that would be more expensive to correct later. (Caution: Don’t let this process
     cause “analysis paralysis” that slows down Agile design and development.)


     This technique (originally known as failure mode, effects, and criticality analysis) was
     first published in 1949 by the US Department of Defense. The technique grew out of
     systems engineering and quality control. It builds on tools like cause and effect analysis
     to try to predict failures before they happen. Failure mode and effects analysis is
     also effective in improving the design of business processes and systems. When
     using it, start by looking in detail at the proposed solution and then identify where
     that solution might fail. 6


     HOW TO USE 7
     (One example process; there are variations.)
           1. Review the learning objectives, domain pedagogies, and what’s known
              about the learner population, learning environment, and other contexts.
           2. Brainstorm potential failure modes.
           3. List potential effects of each failure.
           4. Assign severity rankings. (That is, rate the severity of each effect.)
           5. Assign occurrence rankings.
           6. Assign detection rankings. (How likely is it the failure will be detected?)
           7. Calculate a risk priority number (severity × occurrence × detection).
           8. Develop the action plan (design and development requirements) to
              address the highest priorities.
            An example of failure mode and effect analysis comes from the Prince of
     Songkla University, Thailand, which used it to understand a challenge in their Indus-
     trial Engineering bachelor’s degree program.8 The program includes placing students
     in industrial settings for on-the-job training, but industry partners complained that
     students were being placed in jobs they weren’t yet capable of performing. University
     faculty used this analysis to uncover issues in the complex situation, which spanned
     diverse stakeholders, factors, and education / industrial contexts. They examined all
                                              Tools for Understanding the Challenge | 241



potential failure points and then evaluated each using five-point rubrics:
       1. Severity: Likelihood of the customer noticing the failure or the level of
          worst results, particularly in terms of safety and system functionality
          (5 = Student can’t do the job to 1 = Student comfortable with the job)
       2. Occurrence: Likelihood of a specific failure (5 = Very high probability
          of its occurrence to 1 = Very low probability of its occurrence)
       3. Detection: Degree of existing control plan or verification program that
          can pinpoint potential failures before they occur (5 = Can’t predict and
          no detection of failure to 1 = Can predict and detect)
      Next, they created a risk priority number, which is an index based on the
multiplication of severity by occurrence by detection metrics. An example of (part
of) what this analysis might have looked like is below:

       Failure Mode               Severity        Occurrence         Detection       Risk Priority
 Students lack key
                                      4                 4                 4                 64
 admin info about jobs
 Students lack
                                      4                 3                 4                 48
 essential skills
 Industry partners not
                                      4                 2                 3                 24
 accurately defining jobs

 ...                                 ...               ...               ...                ...

FIGURE 8.6. Partial example of how a failure mode and e!ects analysis might be
scored. The numeric scores in this figure are fictional for the purposes of illustration.

       After identifying all of the potential failure modes, the faculty then ranked
the top five most serious issues. Then the team used the five whys technique, pre-
sented earlier in this chapter, to uncover root cause. They found that four of the five
high-priority issues could have been prevented with better communications between
the department and the industrial partners. Only one of the top-five issues pointed
to the need to better prepare student before on-the-job training. Once they had that
clarity, they were able to identify actions to prevent each issue from reoccurring in
the future.
242 | Learning Engineering Toolkit
