CHAPTER 9


Tools from the
Learning Sciences
by Jim Goodell, Janet Kolodner, and Aaron Kessler



This chapter builds on the foundations outlined in Chapter 2. It
summarizes concepts from the learning sciences, and it provides some
sca!olding to help organize your thinking around their application.


Before using these tools, you should have already examined the challenge (see
Chapter 8) and used a modular design approach to isolate specific opportunities or
problems to be addressed. First use the following checklist to consider the applicable
category or categories that apply to your challenge.

Learning Sciences Concept Categories—Check all that apply:
       Create or improve the conditions for learning
       Help learners understand new ideas
       Help learners retain knowledge
       Help learners apply knowledge
       Help learners build expertise
       Motivate learners
       Measure learning and optimize feedback

Next use the checklist that follows to identify concepts that apply. Then during iter-
ative design, development, and instrumentation; refer back to these checklists and
consider how your solution incorporates the relevant concepts. Use a worksheet to
track which concepts were implemented. Following the checklist on the next page,
the remainder of this chapter includes definitions of the learning sciences concepts
for reference. It also incorporates an example design pattern, to show how you might
integrate the learning sciences concepts into your learning designs.



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-14
244 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



    CATEGORY          SAMPLE CONCEPTS TO CONSIDER
    Create or          Agency: What people think about their own learning matters
    improve the        Cognitive Capabilities: Improve learners’ capacity to learn
    conditions
    for learning       Hierarchy of Needs: Foster physiological / emotional well-being
                       Personalization: Use learner models to inform tailored interventions
                       Variability: Learning is di!erent for everyone
    Help learners      Concrete to Abstract: Start with concrete examples and then expand
    understand         Feedback: E!ective feedback is specific, timely, actionable . . .
    new ideas
                       Metacognition: Help grow or sca!old metacognitive thinking
                       Novice vs. Expert: Help novice learners see and build connections
                       Productive Failure: Safe failure (and feedback) can aid learning
                       Sca!olding: Give support to make the task just challenging enough
                       Spaced Learning: Mitigate forgetting by studying over time
    Help learners      Active Learning: Foster learning through doing
    retain             Cognitive Load: Avoid working memory overload
    knowledge
                       Connections: Emphasize cognitive connections and transferability
                       Personal Connection: Use authentic, personally meaningful tasks
                       Zone of Proximal Development: Find the “just right” challenge level
    Help learners      Challenge: Set the right level of desirable difficulty
    apply              Prior Experience: Remember, there’s no learning from scratch
    knowledge
                       Reinforce: Use it or lose it
                       Transfer: Help learners apply knowledge to new contexts
                       Unlearning: Help learners let go of biases and misconceptions
    Help learners      Communities: Foster real-world applications through social learning
    build expertise    Memory: Help build long-term (vs. working) memory
                       Mentorship: Model ideas and skills
                       Novice vs. Expert: Help novice learners see and build connections
    Motivate           Core Drives: Tap into learners’ core drives to foster motivation
    learners           Engagement: Motivation is learner-centric
                       Hierarchy of Needs: Foster physiological / emotional well-being
                       Motivation Types: Consider intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivators
                       Relevance: Use authentic, personally meaningful tasks
    Measure            Feedback: E!ective feedback is specific, timely, actionable . . .
    learning and       Metacognition: Help grow or sca!old metacognitive thinking
    optimize
    feedback           Novice vs. Expert: Help novice learners see and build connections
                       Productive Failure: Safe failure (and feedback) can aid learning
                       Sca!olding: Give support to make the task just challenging enough
                                             Tools from the Learning Sciences | 245



ACTIVE LEARNING: Foster learning through doing
Learners are more likely to engage enthusiastically and to be able to integrate what
they’re learning with their prior knowledge when asked to achieve a goal that is
meaningful to them, work on a project, or solve a problem. After learning their
new understanding or capability is more likely to be well integrated with what
they already know. However, such project-based learning only works well when
scaffolded so that learners understand the objectives, ask good questions, carry out
robust investigations to answer those questions, reflect on their experiences, and
take advantage of failures.

AGENCY: What people think about their own learning matters
Helping learners understand that many aspects of successful learning, such as
strategy and effort, are under their control fosters agency. Relatedly, when learners
see failure as an opportunity to find out what they still need to learn—rather than
an indicator of self-worth or fixed intelligence—they’re more likely to persevere. A
growth mindset is encouraged with process-focused feedback and an environment
that treats mistakes as opportunities to reflect and learn.

CHALLENGE: Set the right level of desirable difficulty
When learners work at the edge of their mastery (while maintaining high expectations
for performance) it pushes them past their current abilities. Like in athletics, while
practice isn’t always fun and drills can be difficult, a deliberate training process
leads to improvement. The right level of challenge with the right level of support
(scaffolding) promotes effective learning while offsetting the motivational and
cognitive costs of floundering and frustration.

COGNITIVE CAPABILITIES: Improve learners’ capacity to learn
Learning is dependent on general cognitive capacities such as attention, executive
function, working memory, and processing skills. When necessary, interventions
should be applied to develop or strengthen these cognitive capabilities.

COGNITIVE LOAD: Avoid working memory overload
Cognitive load is the effort used in working memory. Working memory (sometimes
referred to as short-term memory) is where new learning starts, but it has limited
capacity and requires greater mental effort. In some cases, a heavy cognitive load can
impede learning. Cognitive load theory suggests that learning experiences should be
246 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     designed so that they don’t overload working memory.

     COMMUNITIES: Foster real-world applications through social learning
     Learning communities (grounded in communities of practice) allow students to
     socially situate, construct, and organize knowledge compatible with experts in the
     domain. Encourage social learning through communities to help learners bridge the
     gap between academic understanding and real-world application.

     CONCRETE TO ABSTRACT: Start with concrete examples and then expand
     Learning starts with concrete information and gradually moves to more abstract
     understanding. Learners often need to start with imperfect but concrete conceptual
     models, anchored in a single context, and then over time replace those with more
     accurate and abstract understanding that can be applied to more than one problem
     or context.

     CONNECTIONS: Emphasize cognitive connections and transferability
     Does the learning experience or environment help learners make connections to prior
     learning? Does it help learners connect across knowledge domains, for example, to
     transfer something learned in school to a real-life task? Help make the connections
     among different concepts, or among theoretical ideas and real-world applications,
     obvious. Help learners practice using new knowledge or skills in different contexts
     or problem variations.

     CORE DRIVES: Tap into learners’ core drives to foster motivation
     People are motivated by different things at different times. Core drives include
     curiosity, meaningfulness, accomplishment, empowerment, ownership, social
     influence, scarcity, unpredictability, and avoidance. Good learning experiences and
     environments tap into these drives so that learners are motivated to engage deeply,
     remain engaged over time, and re-engage after becoming disengaged.

     See also Chapter 15, Tools for Learner Motivation, for specific strategies based on
     the science of motivation.
                                              Tools from the Learning Sciences | 247



ENGAGEMENT: Motivation is learner-centric
Learning isn’t something done to students by a teacher. It’s a process in the learner’s
brain of adding and changing existing knowledge. Learning only happens if learners
actively participate in the process. Learners are more likely to persevere if they
believe their talents can be developed through hard work, good strategies, and input
from others.

FEEDBACK: E!ective feedback is specific, timely, actionable . . .
Descriptive feedback gives learners information about the quality of their ideas and
performance that they can use to adjust. Deliver effective feedback, which is specific,
understandable, timely, non-threatening, and actionable.

HIERARCHY OF NEEDS: Foster physiological / emotional well-being
Before higher levels of motivation can be attained, a person’s basic survival needs
(for example, not starving and feeling safe) and psychological needs (for example,
belonging and esteem) must be met, more or less. Learners with good nutrition,
sleep, and exercise will have better cognitive skills. Aerobic exercise can improve
brain plasticity. Sleep is critical for solidifying learning. Nutrition is important for
brain health. Similarly, learners who feel they’re a part of a positive, supportive
learning community will be better able to focus on learning. To the extent possible,
set these conditions to enable learning.

MEMORY: Help build long-term (vs. working) memory
The difference between a novice and an expert brain has a lot to do with how each
uses the two kinds of memory. Working (short-term) memory is slower, error prone,
more deliberate, has limited capacity and limited retention, requires greater mental
effort, and is the place where new learning starts. In contrast, long-term memory has
long retention, is efficient, operates automatically (without effort), and when trained
error-free. Experts use long-term memory to do complex tasks effortlessly. Help
novices grow their long-term memory by exposing them to many varied patterns
of a concept and by helping them grow, connect, and enhance their mental models.
248 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     MENTORSHIP: Model ideas and skills
     Mentor learners by modeling of ideas and skills while engaging in real world
     activities. This allows novice learners to begin developing expertise. Instructional
     tactics such as demonstration and talk-aloud can help.

     METACOGNITION: Help grow or sca!old metacognitive thinking
     Metacognition is thinking about one’s own thinking and learning. A metacognitive
     learner knows when they’re on the right track and when they’re having difficulties.
     Metacognitive strategies include self-explanation, asking for help, starting over, and
     persevering. A good way of helping learners develop metacognition is to prompt
     them to reflect on or articulate what they’re learning—metacognitive scaffolding.
     Did I already know that? How would I explain that to someone else? Does it conflict
     with what I thought I knew or could do? How well do I know this? On what parts
     of this do I need help or more practice? For measurement and feedback, consider
     assessing learners’ metacognitive skills. Also, develop assessments that prompt
     learners to express their thought processes (rather than just giving a final answer).
     See the design pattern below for an example of how to help learners build their
     metacognitive skills through prompting.

     MOTIVATION TYPES: Consider intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivators
     Intrinsic motivators (internal goals) tend to be more powerful than extrinsic rewards.
     Use strategies, such as those outlined in Chapter 15, to foster intrinsic motivation.

     NOVICE VS. EXPERT: Help novice learners see and build connections
     Compared to novices, experts not only have more content knowledge, they’ve also
     organized that knowledge for deeper understanding, rapid retrieval, and contextual
     application. Experts can see patterns and understand under which conditions and
     contexts to apply knowledge. Experts can also select and retrieve important aspects
     of their knowledge without effort. It may be necessary to help learners restructure
     their knowledge so that it’s organized more like the knowledge of experts. That
     takes intentional practice with feedback. Explicitly help learners see and practice
     building connections between concepts at each level of learning. Also, when it
     comes to measurement and feedback, consider measuring efficiency of application
     and transferability (not just retrieval of discrete facts and highly contextualized
     skills) as learners move from novice to experts.
                                             Tools from the Learning Sciences | 249



PERSONALIZATION: Use learner models to inform tailored interventions
In one-on-one tutoring, tutors construct mental models of what a learner knows
and can do based on observations and interactions. In intelligent tutoring systems,
learner models are dynamically built from data about a learner and their interactions.
In both cases these models are useful for diagnosis, feedback, coaching, and the
prescription of learning activities. Tailoring such interventions to learners’ unique
cognition, metacognition, and motivational factors can greatly improve learning
outcomes.

See also Chapter 13, Data Instrumentation Tools, for more insights on collection of
data used to optimize feedback.


PRIOR EXPERIENCE: Remember, there’s no learning from scratch
Learning is an active process in which a learner constructs meaning by mapping
new information onto prior knowledge. As we experience life our brains try to fit
new information into what we already know about the world. Learning works best
when we have the background knowledge to make sense of the new information.
Learning doesn’t work if the prerequisite knowledge on which to anchor the new
knowledge is missing. Help learners by understanding their prior experiences and
then connecting new knowledge or skills to them.

PRODUCTIVE FAILURE: Safe failure (and feedback) can aid learning
Failure provides an opportunity to learn. When a learner tries out some thinking or
a new skill and it doesn’t work as well as they expected, the opportunity presents
itself to figure out why and correct or improve their capabilities. Use a wrong answer
during learning-embedded assessments as an opportunity to provide feedback, or
provide feedback on project-based activities that help learner reflect on why they
failed and how to correct those issues, gaps, or misconceptions in the future.

REINFORCE: Use it or lose it
A newly learned skill or knowledge component is generally forgotten over time if
not reinforced with practice and applied. Use techniques like spaced learning, or
create tasks that require learners to build upon and practice their new knowledge and
skills, so that they’re reinforced over time.
250 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     In Chapter 4, Learning Engineering is Engineering, we introduced design
     patterns—repeatable solutions to commonly occurring engineering
     problems. The following design pattern is an example of an instructional
     strategy that addresses the learning sciences concept of metacognition.



     Example Design Pattern: Metacognitive Prompting *

       Explicit verbal, text, or visual reminders prompt learners to engage in specific
       cognitive processes, such as remembering previous instructions. Metacognitive
       prompting involves asking questions and giving reminders that encourage
       metacognitive thinking such as planning, monitoring, and evaluating one’s
       thinking processes.


     How It Works: Metacognitive prompts are designed to enhance metacognitive
     skills, including self-awareness, self-monitoring, self-regulation, reflective judg-
     ment (evaluating the accuracy of knowledge), integrative thinking (integrating sepa-
     rate pieces of information or seemingly opposing perspectives), and emotional intel-
     ligence (the ability to understand one’s own and others’ emotions). These skills act
     as a “multiplier” to all other cognitive competencies. Metacognitive strategies help
     learners to independently make sense of situations, transfer their knowledge to new
     contexts, and evaluate themselves. These skills also help learners better plan, prac-
     tice, evaluate their own learning then adjust their behavior to best meet their goals.

     Applicable Contexts: All

     Inputs: None required

     Instrumentation: None required

     Design Considerations: Is the learner already familiar with the concept of
     “metacognition,” or at least, the value of metacognitive skills like self-awareness
     and self-regulation? Will you incorporate the prompts before, during, and / or after
     a learning activity? Will you use metacognitive prompts as a self-directed, teacher-
     led, or group activity?


     * Schatz et al. (2012). Making Good Instructors Great: US Marine Corps Small Unit Decision Mak-
       ing Instructional and Assessment Techniques Handbook, pp. 84–85.
                                              Tools from the Learning Sciences | 251



Pattern:

 1. Explain the concept of metacognition and its benefits. Use direct instruction
    to help learners initially understand the distinction between “thinking” (that
    is, cognition) and “thinking about thinking” (that is, metacognition). Explain
    core metacognitive skills and how these skills “multiply” learning and cogni-
    tive performance.
 2. Before, during, and / or after an activity that introduces new concepts, help
    learners activate metacognitive thinking through questions or prompting.
    First, determine which phases to add the prompts into.

     • Preparation (Pre): Metacognitive prompts during the preparation phase
       generally involve planning, such as encouraging learners to set goals,
       organize their thoughts, anticipate, or predict.
     • Execution (During): Prompts during execution often involve monitoring,
       such as summarizing or elaborating on new concepts, self-assessing
       comprehension, or using mental strategies (like mnemonic devices).
     • Reflection (Post): Metacognitive prompts during the reflective phase
       generally involve evaluating, such as self-assessing performance, reflecting
       on feedback, or considering ways to apply the recent lesson.
 3. Next, determine which metacognitive skills are most appropriate to activate
    and how to prompt them. These can be delivered asynchronously (such as via
    automated scripts in an e-learning course), by a teacher (such as through tutor-
    ing or mentorship), or drawn out through peer-learning activities.
     Here are some examples:
     •     Frame: Ask how individual parts fit into an overall task
     •     Attend: Cue learners to the critical pieces of information
     •     Self-Regulate: Ask learners what “mental tricks” they use
     •     Extend: Ask about similar concepts that relate to the lesson
     •     Reflect: Ask learners to describe (paraphrase) what they just learned
     •     Experiment: Make learners “stretch” to solve new problems

 4. Prompt learners. If possible engage in a dialogue to encourage reflection.
 5. In a group setting, consider leading a group discussion. Allot time for the dis-
    cussion and encourage learners to describe the metacognitive strategies that
    they personally found useful.
252 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     RELEVANCE: Use authentic, personally meaningful tasks
     Learners are more likely to become intrinsically motivated and engaged over long
     periods of time if what they’re asked to do is personally meaningful and authentic,
     if they imagine they’re capable, if they know when and who in the world carries
     out such tasks, if they have the available resources that someone in the real world
     engaging in that task would have, and if associated activities and assessments fit into
     the flow of what they’re trying to achieve. Learners often need help to know how
     a new learning experience is meaningful to them and encouragement that they’re
     capable of learning the associated knowledge and skills.

     SCAFFOLDING: Give support to make the task just challenging enough
     Scaffolding provides the structure and support learners need to construct their own
     learning with tasks more complex than they could handle without that support.
     Scaffolding includes hints, prompts, conceptual frameworks (for example, advance
     organizers), process support, focus support, and strategic guidance. Its goal is
     to provide just enough support to make tasks achievable without losing useful
     complexity or context. Scaffolding may be added and removed based on each
     learner’s current needs. For measurement and feedback, build in the opportunity for
     learners to get hints and worked examples during formative assessments. If possible,
     use data to adjust the level of difficulty for an assessment task, removing and adding
     scaffolding for a desired level of difficulty.

     SPACED LEARNING: Mitigate forgetting by studying over time
     Studying the same amount of content over time (rather than cramming all at once)
     can dramatically improve learning, long-term recall and flexible use of that content.
     Increasing the time between repetitions over time is most effective.

     TRANSFER: Help learners apply knowledge to new contexts
     Transfer refers to ability to apply existing knowledge and skills to new contexts.
     Transfer is difficult. It requires recognition that you know something, understanding
     of how to apply it in a new context or form, and the disposition to do so. Often,
     learners can use existing knowledge when prompted before they can independently
     recognize that their knowledge is relevant. Being able to transfer learning requires
     practice in a variety of circumstances. Create varied conditions and coach learners on
     what knowledge or skills might be useful for different contexts. The better someone
     can reuse their knowledge, the more robust their learning becomes.
                                                Tools from the Learning Sciences | 253



UNLEARNING: Help learners let go of biases and misconceptions
Sometimes a learner has mistaken, imperfect, or incomplete mental models that must
be replaced with corrected or improved ones. Developers of learning experiences
should not only know the subject matter but also common points of learner confusion
and how to address them. Use formative assessments and other opportunities to
identified learners’ gaps, biases, and misconceptions, and then help them unlearn
those unhelpful ideas or skills.

VARIABILITY: Learning is di!erent for everyone
Existing knowledge is the foundation on which new learning is anchored. New
learning is also dependent on cognitive capacities such as attention, executive func-
tion, working memory, and processing skills.* All of these personal characteristics
are variable. In addition, different cultural influences and experiences impact devel-
opment from the beginning of life. To be most effective, it’s important to consider
learners’ variability and, to the extent possible, tailor learning experiences to work
with and build upon those individual or group differences.

ZONE OF PROXIMAL DEVELOPMENT: Find the “just right” challenge level
Tasks that are outside of a learner’s current independent ability—but that are achievable
with help (scaffolding)—are within a learner’s Zone of Proximal Development.
Every learner’s zone is different, and a learner’s zone is different at different points
in time. Learning activities should be adaptive or adaptable to consider the probable
zones and to provide the relevant scaffolding required for success. Good scaffolding
helps learners succeed past what they can do by themselves and helps them grow
their capabilities over time.




* Note: There is no evidence that people have particular fixed learning styles (for example,
  visual versus auditory). Learners’ preferred mode of learning may vary over time, by
  context, and by learning objective. Adaptive and adaptable learning experiences offer
  multiple modes and levels that consider differences in the learners to be served.
CHAPTER 10


Tools for Teaming
by Dina Kurzweil and Erin S. Barry*



Learning engineering requires a team. This chapter defines teams and
learning engineering teams. It examines stages of team development,
practices for team formation, and shared competencies of a learning
engineering team. Finally, this chapter provides insights about e!ective
team communication and common barriers faced by teams with
strategies for overcoming team dysfunctions.



What’s a team?
      A team is a small number of people . . . who are committed to a com-
      mon purpose, performance goals, and approach for which they hold
      themselves mutually accountable.1

For teams to be effective, the common purpose or goal needs to be clear so that team
members can work interdependently to achieve the common outcome.
       Additionally, team members should have a shared commitment and
responsibility to complete the common purpose or goal. It’s important for all
members of the team to understand each member’s role and expectations and have
mutual respect for what each person brings to the table. On some teams, the leader
sets the vision, selects the members of the team, and sets out the procedures to
follow, while the team members execute accordingly. In contrast, other teams allow
all team members to contribute to decision-making about vision, selection of team
members, and procedures in addition to the execution of tasks. To have effective


* The opinions or assertions contained herein are the private ones of the authors and are
  not to be construed as official or reflecting the views of the Department of Defense, the
  Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences, or any other agency of the US
  government. The authors have no financial disclosures.


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-15
256 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     team performance, cohesiveness, morals, and ultimately outcomes, the team should
     clearly understand who’s involved in each step, and they should have the flexibility
     to change these dynamics based on evolving contexts and situations. Finally, teams
     should periodically have meaningful discussions about the overall goals as well as
     roles of each team member. This helps to maintain a positive and safe environment
     where team members feel comfortable speaking up.2


     Why have a team?
     Why should we work in a team? Everyone has unique experiences, perspectives,
     opinions, gifts, communication and leadership styles, strengths, and weaknesses
     that, when brought together, strengthen potential solutions and can solve problems
     that couldn’t otherwise be solved alone. When dealing with larger or complex
     problems, the power of a team can far exceed any one person’s ability. It’s extremely
     rare for someone to independently have all of the capabilities needed to take on
     learning engineering challenges including expertise in engineering processes,
     learning sciences, software engineering, data science, instructional design, artificial
     intelligence and machine learning, pedagogy and andragogy, user experience design,
     and product testing as well as mastery of regulatory policies and technical standards.


     What’s a learning engineering team?
     Learning engineering teams bring together specialists who can help transform
     learning environments, expand best practices, implement learner-centered design,
     and enable data-driven decisions. These specialists have a combination of knowledge
     and experience as well as the ability to filter their individual knowledge through the
     lenses of their combined experience. In other words, the value of effective teams
     doesn’t only come from each member’s own abilities; it also comes from their
     abilities to work together.


     Learning Engineering Team Composition
     Different learning engineering challenges require different mixes of talent, but they
     typically require contributions from the following domains:
            Assessment, measurement, and evaluation
            Data science
                                                               Tools for Teaming | 257



         Education and training professions
         Instructional design
         Learning environment engineering
         Learning experience design
         Learning sciences
         Project management
         Software engineering
       Subject-matter expertise


Team Organization
While organizations may be managed operationally around functional teams, learn-
ing engineering teams may require cross-disciplinary effort drawn from differ-
ent departments. For example, dynamic or interworking teams may be needed to
complete particular tasks. Carnegie Learning’s bi-weekly “data jams” described in
Chapter 6 are an example of this. Below are some different types of teams that can
be found within organizations:
  ► Dynamic or task force teams: These teams are brought together to quickly
      address a task or problem and are dissolved once it’s been completed or solved.
  ► Functional teams: Functional teams are more enduring. Their members
      belong to the same functional area and respond to a single leader, who’s
      responsible for the team and its outcomes. This type of team doesn’t dissolve
      once a project is complete; instead they move on to a new project together.
  ► Interworking teams: These teams are made up of members from different
      areas of activity, and their members usually have the same hierarchical level.
      This type of team usually comes together to focus on a task or problem with a
      multidisciplinary view, and the team dissolves once the project is completed.
  ► Matrixed teams: Matrixed team members usually belong to different
      functional areas and respond to different managers or leaders, but an
      organization assembles them into a temporary team to implement a specific
      project. After its completion, the team dissolves. Typically, members of
      matrixed teams perform other tasks related to their home department, but as
      far as the project is concerned, they answer to the project leader.
258 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Team Formation
     Every learning engineering challenge is different, and the work required in each
     stage of the learning engineering process also varies. The mix of talents needed,
     the roles assigned to team members, the contributions required for each role, and
     the overall work will vary by challenge and stage. For example, the implementation
     phase requires a different team composition than creation phase. Similarly, imple-
     mentation of a global learning platform like Duolingo requires a different team than
     implementation of a single course delivered to a few students or the design of donkey
     carts in West Africa to get children to school. Even when the reporting structures
     and titles for people in an organization are relatively static, the composition of teams
     and assigned roles should be aligned with the challenge, context, and work required
     for the given stage of a project. It may be helpful to think of team formation (or
     re-formation) as a step between each phase of the learning engineering process.
     Between stages of the learning engineering process ask:
           •   What problem is the team being formed to solve?
           •   What team competencies are required to address the problem’s context?
           •   What are the organizational contexts in which the team must operate?
           •   How will the organizational contexts dictate the work to be done and the
               processes that need to be followed?

                                                                                       PHASES
                                                                                                  Implementation

                                                                                                                   Investigation
                                                                           Challenge

                                                                                       Creation




      WHAT AREAS OF EXPERTISE ARE NEEDED?
      Assessment, measurement, and evaluation
      Data science
      Education and training professions
      Instructional design
      Learning environment engineering
      Learning experience design
      Learning sciences
      Project management
      Software engineering
      Subject-matter expertise
     FIGURE 10.1. Worksheet for team roles by learning engineering process phase
                                                                 Tools for Teaming | 259



TEAM VARIATION WITHIN THE LEARNING ENGINEERING PROCESS

The learning engineering process, defined in Chapter 1, is iterative. (It’s not a linear
process!) Steps may be revisited multiple times before moving on, and steps may
be revisited again throughout the process. Similarly, learning engineering team
development takes on many forms, is iterative, and can be repeated at any stage. The
team member roles and overall composition may need to evolve over time. Teams
may need to integrate new people based on requirements and expertise at each stage
of the process. The following describes eight team functions. Figure 10.2 shows
these functions mapped to the learning engineering process from Chapter 1.

      1. Developing: During the challenge phase, the team develops a firm ground-
         ing in the problem or challenge. These are often complex and will likely
         require decisions about what’s to be done.
      2. Coalescing: Also during the challenge phase, the team works together to
         understand the context of the problem and problem space. They should
         have at least one advocate who has a firm grounding in the context of the
         learners (and other stakeholders). That advocate (or advocates) must be
         able to explain the situation to the rest of the team.
      3. Assigning: The talents needed from each team member and their align-
         ment to a particular aspect of the problem will depend on the specific
         problem to be addressed. Make sure to reassess and reassign members
         between stages of the learning engineering process either by adjusting the
         team composition, each person’s expected contributions, or by handing off
         the project or tasks to a separate team.
      4. Planning: During the creation phase, the team plans how it will address the
         challenge using multiple rounds of iterative development. They also curate
         internal products to guide the work, such as standards, experimentation
         protocols, and style guides. This stage should also include plans for who
         will provide peer review and who will provide final sign-offs.
      5. Developing: Also during the creation phase, the appropriate team mem-
         bers will iteratively develop products and instrumentation to address the
         challenge, testing each iteration with end users. Subgroups or individuals
         may focus on developing parts of the overall solution, including instru-
         mentation and implementation plans.
      6. Implementing: During the implementation phase, the team must be
260 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



              prepared to support the implementation of the product and to document
              any issues that arise. This may mean adjustment of implementation plans
              as well as tactical and personnel challenges.
          7. Analyzing: During the investigation phase, the team examines the solu-
             tion’s ability to fully and ideally solve the problem. This is a critical re-
             view point. Upon completion of an analysis the team might discover, for
             example, that it needs more data, additional or different testing, or to make
             new design decisions. In other words, following the analysis phase, it’s
             likely that the learning engineering team will need to revisit one of the
             prior stages (or repeat the analyzing phase with variations).
          8. Iterating: Team members working on each stage of the process must
             look for opportunities to improve the solution in future iterations. Each
             member is responsible for contributing to continuous improvements—
             examining what’s working in the current iteration as well as what might
             be a challenge or opportunity for the next.



                                                                  3
                                                                        TE
                                                                           AM
                                     S
                                  ER
                                                                            CREATION
                              N
                           AR




                                                                         including designing,
                        LE




                                         1                             building solutions, data
                                                                           instrumentation,
                                                                        implementation plans
                                             1
                                                                         4        5               8
                                                 CHALLENGE
                                                Understand the
                                             opportunity to improve
                INVESTIGATION                 learning or learning
                 with data analysis                conditions



                    7

                                                                  IMPLEMENTATION
                                                                      with data collection
                                                  2
                                                                                      6
                                                 CONTE X T




     FIGURE 10.2. Suggested phases during which to apply the outlined team processes
                                                               Tools for Teaming | 261



TEAM DYNAMICS

Bruce Tuckman, an early psychology researcher focused on team dynamics, devel-
oped a four-phase model of the team development process that provides a framework
on the nature of team dynamics and their evolution. The four stages are:

      1. Forming: In this stage, individuals are trying to get to know each other
         and the organization and have not formed a commitment to the team. Proj-
         ect leaders provide direction and outline expectations. Leaders can also
         facilitate a discussion about the results to help the group understand each
         other, their differences, operating and communication styles.
      2. Storming: In this typically rocky stage, team members may challenge
         the leader and each other. The leader coaches members on how to manage
         conflict and focus on goals. The leaders also facilitate related training.
      3. Norming: After individuals have worked through conflicts, the team
         begins to develop. People begin to appreciate differences and work together.
         The leader begins to be more of a facilitator, offering encouragement and
         guidance.
      4. Performing: At this stage, the team is fully functional, members are
         able to manage their relationships, and work toward shared goals. Team
         members feel accepted and communicate openly with the leader. The
         leader focuses on delegating responsibilities and must identify when the
         team is moving into a different stage.


TEAM LEADERSHIP

The choice of who leads a team should account for context. Some teams are small,
such as an instructional designer teaming with an instructor. Larger teams who are
collectively designing learning experiences, platforms, or other complex solutions
may need a project manager who coordinates across the various other specialists
and sets the direction for the work. Similarly, as teams go through different stages
of development, different styles of leadership should be considered for optimal team
performance. During the earlier stages of team development, the leader may need
to be more involved and directive. When teams get to the norming and performing
stages, shared leadership becomes more realistic as everyone understands each oth-
er’s roles and can step in with their needed expertise.
262 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     TEAM CHECKLIST

     Teams formed to address any part of a learning engineering process can leverage
     general team building principles to move from storming to norming and performing.
     Use the checklist below as a guide to move through team formation processes:

        Develop ground rules
         It’s important to develop ground rules, such as how to deal with accelerated
         or delayed project timelines, how concerns will be handled, who to talk to
         about what and when, and how to promote the work of others and the team.

        Have clearly defined roles
         Each member must understand their own role as well as the roles of others.
         This will help members to know what they’re doing, how they can help each
         other when needed, and how to avoid stepping on others’ toes.

        Create a vision and goals
         The team must have a clear way ahead that’s known by all members. The
         vision doesn’t prescribe details of every step on the path forward.

        Provide clear communication
         Communication at all organizational levels must be clear for all to understand.

        Build trust and cooperation
         Once the team is formed, its members must have mutual trust and be willing
         to cooperate with one another. Without those, failure is inevitable.

        Value ideas
         Team members must value each other’s ideas and contributions. If team
         members don’t feel valued, they won’t feel like part of the team, leading to
         lower performance and satisfaction.

        Acknowledge unstated feelings
         Leaders must acknowledge and use social awareness to account for others’
         behaviors and emotions. If a team member isn’t pleased with a certain path
         and it’s noticed by the leader, it’s useful to acknowledge that not everyone
         may agree with the path forward and allow for all thoughts to be heard.

        Allow sharing (and often)
         All members of the team need to share ideas and thoughts around the project.
         Being heard allows individuals to feel like a part of the team.
                                                                 Tools for Teaming | 263



Communication
Communication is an important aspect for teams to be effective and successful.
Communication includes providing a clear message (sending), and importantly, it
also includes making sure that the message sent is understood by the person re-
ceiving the message (receiving). Communication is both verbal (using words) and
nonverbal (sounds, facial expressions, body language, and paralanguage). When
communication is clear, open, and honest, there’s increased trust on the team and
decreased conflict.


COMPETENCIES OF TEAM MEMBERS

Learning engineering teams will be more successful if they have some knowledge
of concepts and vocabulary used in the domains of the other professional specialties
on the team. For example, if a software engineer on the team knows something about
terms used by learning scientists, and if the instructional designer knows about some
of the ways that learning analytics can provide insights. This shared vocabulary
will help all team members work more effectively together. It’s also important that
a member or some members of the team can communicate well with stakeholders,
clients, and leaders of the organization they’re supporting.


COMMUNICATION STYLES

It’s important for us to understand our own communication styles as well as those
of others. There are many tools available to look at communication styles; one tool,
called DISC, is illustrated in Figure 10.3. It helps you identify your preferred style
of communication (dominance, influence, steadiness, correctness) and information
about the other styles. It’s a great tool to use with teams to provide awareness on each
person’s communication style to help you modify your behaviors. Adapting to others’
communication styles can help ensure that the message sent is the message received.
One of many DISC assessment tools is available online at: www.extendeddisc.org/
what-is-disc.


Team Functioning and Dysfunctioning
Some common characteristics seem to be strong indicators of a team that isn’t func-
tioning at its peak or that needs intervention. What makes good teams and what
264 | Learning Engineering Toolkit

      Strong-minded             Emotional           Satisfied             Careful
      Pioneering                Influential         Easy mark             Correct
      Domineering               Attractive          Willing               Precise
      Determined                Stimulating         Even-tempered         Fussy
      Demanding                 Captivating         Patient               Timid
      Self-reliant              Companionable       Kind                  Open-minded
      Persistent                Playful             Self-controlled       Agreeable
      High-spirited             Talkative           Good-natured          Soft-spoken
      Impatient                 Convincing          Contented             Resigned
      Aggressive                Good mixer          Gentle                Respectful
      Nervy                     Poised              Accommodating         Conventional
      Argumentative             Confident           Relaxed               Cooperative
      Restless                  Inspiring           Considerate           Well-disciplined
      Courageous                Optimistic          Sympathetic           Diplomatic
      Positive                  Eager               Lenient               Exacting
      Adventurous               Enthusiastic        Loyal                 Adaptable
      Will power                Entertaining        Good listener         Humble
      Competitive               Life-of-the-party   Obedient              Tolerant
      Vigorous                  Persuasive          Neighborly            Cautious
      Outspoken                 Eloquent            Reserved              Strict
      Dogged                    Animated            Obliging              Devout
      Assertive                 Gregarious          Nonchalant            Docile
      Bold                      Outgoing            Moderate              Perfectionist

     FIGURE 10.3. Communication styles based upon the DISC model
     Table CC-BY the Chute Institute, 2013
                                                                    Tools for Teaming | 265



makes bad teams? See the tool in Figure 10.4 for qualities of dysfunctional teams as
well as ways to work towards fixing these qualities to improve team performance.
      For teams to be effective, there are many elements that need to be considered in
order for all members of the team to work effectively. Team members who embrace
and encourage both leader and follower roles are beneficial for team collaboration.
For effective team performance, cohesiveness, morals, leadership, planning
documentation, and communication plans should be clearly understood. Identify
who’s involved in each step, and have flexibility for changing context and situations.
The characteristics and competencies of the team should be formally recognized.
Finally, teams should have meaningful discussions of the overall goals and roles of
each team member to maintain a positive and a safe environment for all members to
speak up and share thoughts and ideas at any time.3


TEAM DYSFUNCTION AND IMPROVEMENT STRATEGIES

DYSFUNCTIONS             WHAT TO DO TO BE EFFECTIVE
Lack of trust            • Be genuine and show vulnerability
                         • Understand your role and team members’ roles
Avoidance of
accountability           • Hold each other accountable
                         • Use open, honest communication
Lack of cohesion         • Provide constructive feedback
                         • Frame conflict as positive
                         • Address the conflict in a timely manner
                         • Use open, honest communication
Unaddressed or
unresolved conflicts     • Maintain mutual respect
                         • Listen—not just to respond but to hear other perspectives
                         • Work toward resolution so that all parties feel like they’re
                           heard and valued and reach agreement with next steps
                         • Clearly describe roles and responsibility for each member
                         • Clearly communicate roles / responsibilities to the team
                         • Value all team members
Inequality among         • Find ways to show that each member is part of the team
team members             • Find ways to train members to meet their needs on the team
                         • Help team members reach individual goals for themselves
                         • Consider the context of the situation, as it may determine
                           who can / is able to do certain tasks

      and communication         • Make sure that the message sent is the message received
      breakdowns                • Listen to understand, not to respond
      Lack of commitment        • Determine deadlines and who’s involved in meeting them

Endnotes
1   Katzenbach, John R., and Dennis K. Smith. “The Discipline of Teams.” Harvard Business Review,
    March–April (1993), https://hbr.org/1993/03/the-discipline-of-teams-2
