CHAPTER 11


Lean-Agile
Development Tools
by Michelle Barrett and Jim Goodell



This chapter introduces Agile development approaches commonly used
in software development and explains how these tools can be used with
the learning engineering process.


Managing work across functions, time, and budget is difficult, and each manage-
ment strategy has pros and cons. We present a variety of management approaches
well suited to learning engineering.


Agile Development
Agile is a set of methods and methodologies that help a team to think more effectively,
work more efficiently, and make better decisions. Agile is also a mindset, which
opens up the planning, design, and process improvement to the team. Everyone
shares the same information, and everyone on the team has a say in how the practices
are applied.
      Agile was originally coined in February 2001 by a group of software engineers
who were tired of seeing software development projects fail. Frustrated by delivering
software customers didn’t like after years of development, they envisioned a better
and more collaborative way of working that would engage customers early and often
to mitigate the risk that what was being built was not what was wanted, thereby
accommodating change.
      To change a way of working deeply ingrained in many organizations is no
small feat. So those engineers, self-described as “organizational anarchists,” devel-
oped the Agile Manifesto.1




DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-16
270 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           We are uncovering better ways of developing
           software by doing it and helping others do it.
           Through this work we have come to value:

           Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
           Working software over comprehensive documentation
           Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
           Responding to change over following a plan

           That is, while there is value in the items on
           the right, we value the items on the left more.

            Along with the Manifesto, several principles were developed, the first of which
     is satisfying the customer through early and continuous delivery of software. Since
     the introduction of the Agile Manifesto, professionals other than software engineers
     have noticed that applying Agile practices to their work has similarly improved their
     ability to respond to change to better meet customer needs. We’ll return to outline
     some of the practices that have been successfully used in a learning engineering
     context. But first, a quick detour through Lean.


     Lean Development
     Lean has roots in manufacturing, specifically, automotive manufacturing. Eiji
     Toyoda, nephew to the founder of Toyota, experimented with methods to make
     the Toyota manufacturing lines deliver productivity, quality, and variety. What he
     developed was an approach that became the Toyota Production System. Based on
     a study of this system, Lean principles were first described in The Machine that
     Changed the World: The Story of Lean Production—Toyota’s Secret Weapon in the
     Global Car Wars That Is Now Revolutionizing World Industry.2
           Lean principles are all about removing delay and waste, which is defined as
     any action or step in a process that doesn’t add value to the customer. Waste can in-
     clude defects, overproduction, waiting, transportation, inventory, motion, and extra
     processing. To eliminate waste, a relentless focus on continuous improvement is
     employed. Problems are seen as learning opportunities and teams are tasked with
     making improvements in response to those opportunities.
           You’ll see Lean in many contexts now, including manufacturing, entrepre-
     neurship, analytics, organizational design and process, and—you got it—Agile.
     Lean-Agile combines the principles of Lean (continuous improvement) with the
                                                   Lean-Agile Development Tools | 271



principles of Agile (continuous delivery).
       The principles of Lean (continuous improvement) are a good fit with the itera-
tive learning engineering process shown in Chapter 1, and the Agile principles align
well with learning engineering as human-centered design, explained in Chapter 3.


Lean-Agile in Learning Engineering
Burr Settles is one of the early employees of Duolingo, as the tenth engineer and the
twentieth overall employee. What’s more, Duolingo is his first and only corporate
job to date. And to top it off, Duolingo is a relatively young company, with its first
beta product out for testing November 30, 2011. By this time, companies had been
experimenting with Agile for over ten years, and Lean was twenty years old. So
when asked about project management at Duolingo, specifically experiences with
methods that preceded Agile, Burr said, “Oh, they never really tried that.” Duolingo
was operating Agile from the start. “In the beginning, if it couldn’t be done in two
weeks, we didn’t do it.”
      What does it mean to operate Agile? What’s important to understand about
Lean-Agile is that it represents a collection of principles meant to enable teams to
deliver maximum value to customers. Methods and practices aligned with those
principles are widely used today.


Scrum
Have you ever watched a rugby game? It’s beautiful to watch a self-organizing team
of fifteen individuals move the ball down the field to the goal, orchestrating together
and in the moment to respond to changing conditions. It’s from this game that Agile
Scrum found its name.
       Scrum is a framework that values honesty, openness, transparency, empower-
ment, and collaboration. In order to facilitate those values, Scrum includes specific
roles, activities, artifacts, and their specified rules. Because it creates a language for
roles and activities, it can feel daunting to a newcomer, even cult-like. In addition,
because of its structured roles and activities, for teams new to Scrum, the approach
can feel dogmatic and sometimes even contrary to the Agile principles themselves.
But if you keep in mind a self-organized team working together to move a ball down
the field, many of the activities begin to make sense. Everyone on the Scrum team
owns the success of the project.
272 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




     FIGURE 11.1. A rugby scrum Credit: Alain Vermeulen



     ROLES

     Product Owner: This role is responsible for maintaining a product backlog, which
     is simply a list of work to be done, in priority order. The work is described from the
     perspective of the user to whom the work will bring incremental value and focuses
     on what must be done rather than how to do it. These are called user stories. Each
     item in the list is ordered by priority by the product owner. The product owner is
     responsible for understanding the most important needs of the user and company
     and representing that to the team.

     Scrum Master: This role is a servant leader for the team. They help remove imped-
     iments and foster an environment for high-performing team dynamics, continuous
     flow, and relentless improvement.

     Development Team: The team members decide how to complete the work de-
     scribed on each item of the backlog and complete the work. A team is expected
     to represent the skill sets needed to drive the work to completion and deliver the
     incremental value. The team members decide who completes which tasks; this isn’t
     assigned by the Scrum master or product owner.


     ACTIVITIES

     Stand-up: A short daily meeting in which each team member describes work they
                                                 Lean-Agile Development Tools | 273



completed the day before, work they plan on completing the current day, and any
blockers or impediments they may have to completing the work.

Sprint: A regular increment in time (two weeks is typical, but other increments also
are used) during which the team completes work they committed to complete during
planning.

Sprint Planning: A meeting that occurs at the start of the sprint during which
the team examines capacity and pulls in work from the backlog and commits to its
completion by the end of the sprint.

Sprint Review: A meeting that occurs at the end of the sprint during which the
team presents all work completed during the sprint. This includes a demonstration
of the functional value delivered.

Grooming: A meeting held by the product owner during which the acceptance
criteria (for example, what does it mean for the work to be complete and satisfactory
to the user) for items in the backlog are discussed with the team and the team asks
questions, gets clarifications, and estimates a size for the backlog item.

Retrospective: Perhaps the most important meeting of them all, the retrospective
occurs at the end of the sprint. Everyone on the team reflects on what went well, what
didn’t go well, and what they will collectively change in the next sprint to improve.


The . . . “relay race” approach to product development . . . may conflict
with the goals of maximum speed and flexibility. Instead, a holistic or
“rugby” approach—where a team tries to go the distance as a unit,
passing the ball back and forth—may better serve today’s competitive
requirements.
                                        – Takeuchi and Nonaka, 1986 3



Kanban
Kanban is a Lean method used to support teams as they work to continuously
improve their processes. In its simplest form, Kanban helps the team analyze the
process they are using to deliver value for incremental change. To do this, teams
visualize the work as it is flowing through the team’s process so that problems in
274 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     workflow are more easily identified and can be remedied by the team.
            The primary tool of Kanban is a kanban board. A very simple kanban board
     might look like the figure below. On each sticky note is a work item to be completed,
     similar to the user story used in Scrum.
            All teams will have a fixed capacity for work in progress (the middle column).
     At some point, they simply can’t take in any more work without introducing waste
     such as additional wait time or poor quality with rework loops. Therefore, teams
     may choose to impose work-in-progress limits to specified columns in the kanban
     board, representing the maximum allowable items allowed in a column at any point
     in time. Under these conditions, items may only be pulled into the column for work
     if the team is not at the work-in-progress limit.


     Scaled Agile
     In larger companies or institutions, the coordination of multiple Agile teams can
     be daunting. Agile teams are designed to be driven by the team members, thereby
     challenging many long-held governance, budgeting, and release planning processes,



          TO DO                       IN PROGRESS                           DONE




     FIGURE 11.2. An example kanban board
                                                Lean-Agile Development Tools | 275



along with top-down decision-making. However, research indicates that Lean man-
agement and continuous delivery processes positively drive job satisfaction and
software delivery performance, both of which positively drive organizational per-
formance. Therefore, methods for collaboration among Agile teams are needed to
harness the power of Agile while addressing dependencies, milestones, suppliers,
and so on. One well-known method is SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) Agile. This
framework provides for planning across Agile teams in program increments, which
typically represent several sprints of work. The website www.scaledagileframework.
com is a great reference for learning engineering teams navigating the terminology
and methods of Lean, Agile, and SAFe.


Application of Agile Tools and Processes
to Learning Engineering
You now may recognize the learning engineering process as one focused on continu-
ous improvement to provide maximum value to learners and those supporting them.
You may also recognize elements of Scrum and / or Kanban that can help facilitate
team collaboration and delivery of value by your team. It’s possible that members
of your learning engineering team are also members of other teams using Scrum
or Kanban, or that technical work your learning engineering team needs done must
flow through the Scrum processes. If your learning engineering team is operating
within the context of a medium-to-large organization, you could find that you are
expected to participate in SAFe activities for collaboration with other teams.
       Let’s think about the Learning Engineering process introduced in Chapter 1,
and how a team might choose to use Agile to navigate this process. First, the process
starts with a problem to be solved in a given context. Let’s start with a very simple
example of Kanban application to the learning engineering process. Let’s say we
have a problem to be solved in a given context, and we represent the work in the fol-
lowing user story. We create a user story for each of the problems the team will work
on. (See Figure 11.3, and also refer to Chapter 12, Human-Centered Design Tools,
for more information on user stories.)
276 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                             AS A (TRAINER, EDUCATOR , LEARNER, OTHER)
       FIGURE 11.3.          WHO IS (CONTEXT),
         Simple user         I WANT (A GOAL)
         story outline
                             SO THAT (PROBLEM TO BE SOLVED).

                             ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
                             1. MUST…
                             2. MUST…




     Kanban Tools for Learning Engineering
     A learning engineering kanban board might look something like Figure 11.4. You
     will see that the columns in the kanban board mirror the major steps in the learning
     engineering process. In this example board, at this moment, there are three user sto-
     ries in the backlog, one user story in each of the challenge and creation stages, four
     user stories in implementation, one story in investigation, two stories in review, and
     one that has been completed. Thus, this team is actively working on a total of nine
     learning engineering problems simultaneously. Each story that is done represents
     results or findings that will serve to inform the problem to be solved—the ultimate
     value of the learning engineering process. Therefore, this method also supports the
     measurement of value the team provides over time.
            As work is completed, stories are moved across the board. If the team has
     work-in-progress limits, then work can only be pulled into a subsequent step if there
     is capacity to do so, never exceeding a work-in-progress limit. As this board is used,
     steps in the process where the work slows down or builds up will become visible to
     the team, indicating the team might want to consider a process improvement they
     think will improve the flow.
            Regular retrospectives provide the avenue for sharing ideas about process im-
     provement, even for teams using the simple Kanban approach. Feedback from every
     team member should be gathered. A simple and common format is presented below.
            If a learning engineering team implements nothing else from the Lean-Agile
     methods, the combination of Kanban with retrospective is the one to implement.
                                                 Lean-Agile Development Tools | 277




BACKLOG   CHALLENGE     CREATION   IMPLEMENTATION     INVESTIGATION      IN REVIEW   DONE




 FIGURE 11.4. A learning engineering kanban board might look like this



   WHAT WENT WELL?                          WHAT DIDN’T GO WELL?




   WHAT DID WE LEARN?                        WHAT WILL WE DO
                                             DIFFERENTLY NEX T TIME?




 FIGURE 11.5. Regular retrospectives encourage continuous improvement


 Visualizing the work to be done, understanding work-in-progress limits, and con-
 ducting reviews of the board for process improvement will result in considerable
 benefit to the team.


 Getting Started with Lean-Agile
 Adopting new practices like Lean-Agile can feel overwhelming at first. In this
 chapter, we briefly introduced concepts, roles, and activities typically seen in
 organizations that use Lean-Agile methods. We have provided samples of tools that
 can be used for applying Kanban or Scrum methods to a learning engineering effort
278 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     based on our experience deploying these methods to learning engineering teams in
     multiple organizations. However, the best way to understand how these methods
     work is to try them and iterate with your team over time. This tends to work as long
     as the team focuses on continuous improvement and continuous deployment of value
     to customers and stakeholders.
            Duolingo no longer expects all meaningful work to be complete in two weeks.
     As the company grew beyond a software startup, they also matured to recognize that
     Scrum is not a one-size-fits-all approach. For example, Burr and his team have recently
     been working on a project to add a knowledge state modeling service underneath
     Duolingo’s flagship product. This is obviously a large learning engineering effort,
     one with many stakeholders. The overall effort is expected to span more than a two-
     week sprint for development and requires a healthy runway of research preceding the
     start of development. The cadence of Scrum is not appropriate for the early research
     stage but is appropriate for the development stage, and Burr and his teams have the
     freedom to use the methods that fit the stage and state of the project. But Burr never
     lost sight of the ultimate goal—to deliver value to his customers. In fact, when the
     research started in September, he made an oath to stop shaving until there was a live
     feature in the platform using the knowledge state service. And the teams delivered.
            He ended up shaving on March 31, several months ahead of expectation.



     Endnotes
     1 Cunningham, Ward. “Manifesto for Agile Software Development.” Agile Manifesto Website, 2001.
       https://agilemanifesto.org
     2 Womack, James P., Daniel T. Jones, and Daniel Roos. The Machine that Changed the World:
       The Story of Lean Production—Toyota’s Secret Weapon in the Global Car Wars that is Now
       Revolutionizing World Industry. Simon and Schuster, 2007.
     3 Takeuchi, Hirotaka and Ikujiro Nonaka. “The New New Product Development Game.” Harvard
       Business Review 64, no. 1 (1986): 137–146. (See page 137.)
CHAPTER 12


Human-Centered
Design Tools
by Sae Schatz, Khanh-Phuong Thai, Scotty D. Craig,
Jordan Richard Schoenherr, Jodi Lis, and Janet Kolodner



This chapter catalogs some human-centered design tools that build
on the concepts and processes introduced in Chapter 3, Learning
Engineering is Human-Centered.


The goal of human-centered design is to understand the interactions among people
and the other elements of a system and then apply theory, principles, and data to
support human well-being and overall system performance. The “system” we’re
talking about is the collective assembly of all the stakeholder groups, system
subcomponents, and interactions among them. In a school context, this could include
everything from its management climate, classroom layout, and teaching methods
to the EdTech user interface, subject matter being covered, and students’ personal
characteristics. Figure 12.1 shows a concept of the multilayered system in which
learners and learning experiences are embedded. Each of these different layers plays
a role in the ultimate success or failure of learning engineering solutions.1
       To design the most effective learning experiences, we need to use human-
centered design techniques that take into account our whole system—from the context
of use and learner characteristics down to their mouse clicks and data elements. And
we need to plan for human variability, limitations, and imperfections. As discussed
in Chapter 3, disciplines such as user-centered design,2 user-experience design,3
design thinking,4 human-systems integration,5 design justice,6 and participatory
design 7 provide useful tools for this. There are also unique applications of these
techniques for learning contexts, and they go by names such as learner-centered
design,8 learning-experience design,9 universal design for learning,10 and design-
based research.11 (Check out the endnotes for links to additional resources for these


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-17
                                          Public
       Public Health                    Awareness                         Jobs
        Conditions                                                       Market
                   EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT
             Government            Technology               Societal
Economic                                                                       Education
              Initiatives         Advancements           Infrastructure
Pressures                                                                        Policy

                    ORGANIZATIONAL ENVIRONMENT
   Workload         Employee Development                Leadership Involvement
   Staffing         Resource Availability               Organizational Culture
   Processes        Employee Engagement                 Organizational Structure

 PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT         HUMAN-SYSTEM INTERFACES          SOCIAL ENVIRONMENT

 Lighting                       Classroom Tools               Authority Gradients
 Noise                          IT Access                     Social Climate
 Temperature                    Internet Access               Psychological Climate
 Layout                         EdTech Options                Safety and Wellbeing
 Distractions                   User Interfaces               Available Mentors
 Accessibility                  Accessibility                 Peer Interactions


                       LEARNING EXPERIENCE CHARACTERISTICS

       Individual vs. Group      Competing Tasks                Modality
       Task Complexity           Learning Tactics               Time Pressure
       Subject Matter            Cognitive Requirements         Interactivity


                       INDIVIDUAL (LEARNER) CHARACTERISTICS

            Knowledge/Skills     Alertness/Fatigue       Sensory Abilities
            Experience           Motivation/Attitude     Cognitive Abilities
            Mental Models        Identity and Culture    Metacognition




             BEHAVIORS         PERFORMANCE          LEARNING EVENTS

FIGURE 12.1. Examples of interacting subcomponents in a system
                                                Human-Centered Design Tools | 281



disciplines.) The remainder of this chapter includes an introductory set of human-
centered tools that are generally applicable to most learning engineering solutions.


Stakeholder Analyses
Stakeholders are the humans—people or groups—affected by, or in some way in-
volved with, a system. They may include learners, teachers, educational staff, man-
agers, system administrators, learning engineering collaborators, and many others.
Also, different stakeholders may interact with a system at different times. For ex-
ample, curriculum developers may be active stakeholders early in a system’s life
cycle but then only periodically after the final product is released. So, who are your
stakeholders, and how will they interact with your learning experience?

       Identify who your stakeholders are for all life-cycle phases
       Identify how they’ll use, affect, or be affected by your system
       Elicit and document stakeholders’ expectations
       Understand the context of how and when they’ll interact with your system

      A common way to categorize stakeholders is with a stakeholder analysis
diagram. These come in various forms, but often look like Figure 12.2. To create a


            HIGH

                    HELP                        OUR
                    DESK                         DESIGN
                              ED                   TEAM               S
                                                                  NER
                                                              LEAR ★
       INFLUENCE




                              TECH
                              ADMINS




                                                                   TEACHER
                    VR LAB                    DEPT.                 AIDES
                     STAF F                    CHAIR
                                       R
                              REGISTRA                 SOF TWARE
                                                       SUPPLIER



              LOW                  INTEREST                               HIGH

FIGURE 12.2. Identify and map all of your stakeholders
282 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     stakeholder analysis, start by brainstorming about the possible stakeholders and then
     organize them into groups, such as based on their level of interest (or involvement)
     in the project as well as their ability to influence its success.


     STAKEHOLDER NEEDS ASSESSMENT AND ANALYSIS

     Once you’ve identified your stakeholders, the next step is to think about their
     attributes and needs—making sure all are understood, identifying any conflicts,
     and prioritizing them as necessary. It’s important to complete these assessments
     early, so that critical system requirements are well understood before starting
     development. Following a stakeholder needs assessment, you’ll also want to complete
     a corresponding needs analysis. The difference is that a needs assessment simply
     identifies your stakeholders’ needs while the analysis helps you get to the root cause
     of why those needs exist. (See Chapter 8 for root-cause analysis tools.)
            There are many ways to effectively perform stakeholder needs assessments and
     analyses. Pick the tools most appropriate for your situation. Qualitative methods
     focus on exploring personal observations, thoughts, or experiences. Some of these
     research approaches include:

            Stakeholder elicitation, such as interviews or focus groups
            Open-ended reactions or attitudinal surveys
            Behavioral or cognitive task analyses
            Field observation, ethnography, and participatory analyses
            Design-based research (although this can also be quantitative) 12
            Reviews of published case studies

            Quantitative methods use more objective approaches, typically involving
     statistics or computational methods of some kind. Some of these approaches include:

            Trend analyses
            Quantitative analyses of published studies, like meta-analyses
            Likert-style questionnaires with close-ended answer options
            Computational methods, like modeling and simulation
            Experimental and quasi-experimental studies

     Note, whenever you’re conducting research with stakeholders—even just simple
     interviews—make sure to consider research ethics. Refer to the ethical frameworks
     in Chapter 7 and the tools for making ethical decisions in Chapter 17.
                                                 Human-Centered Design Tools | 283



IDENTIFY LEARNER VARIATIONS

It’s important to understand the range of diversity within a population rather than just
designing to its middle or so-called “average.” Learning engineers need to uncover
typical variations as well as edge cases among both dominant and non-dominant
subgroups, particularly from the learner population. This allows for the creation
of designs that accommodate and can be adapted broadly. In addition to using the
research methods described above, consider some basic questions about each learner
subgroup, such as from the checklist below:
       Who are my learners, and what will they learn?
       What are my learners’ different interests related to what they’ll learn?
       Why would these learners want to learn the targeted skills and content?
       Given that, how might they become engaged in learning?
       What prior knowledge and experiences do they bring with them?
       What range of abilities do different learners possess?
       When and where will they learn?
       How do they engage in this environment?
       How do they see themselves in this environment? How do they identify?
       How might we design learning activities that connect to varying identities?
       How might we ensure learners’ equitable access and empowerment?


Participatory Design Techniques
As discussed in Chapter 3, participatory design techniques invite stakeholders
into the design process directly, not only to gain empathetic understanding of their
needs but to guide iterative development of the solution intended to meet those
needs. In other words, participatory design involves interacting with members of
the targeted learning populations and other stakeholders that support them within
the design process itself. For example, you may collaborate with instructors to
understand their teaching experiences, practical constraints, and knowledge about
their learners during the create stage of the learning engineering process. There are
many different ways to integrate stakeholders into the design process, and regardless
of the approaches selected, the goal is to actively engage representatives from each
stakeholder group in the process.
       Stakeholders can play many valuable roles. However, since most people lack a
background in human-centered design, you’ll likely need to use participatory design
284 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     methods to help coax stakeholders into explicitly articulating their tacit knowl-
     edge—or, as the saying goes, to help them make the invisible visible. There are
     numerous useful collaborative methods, including the three outlined here: design
     cards, card sorting, and affinity diagrams. In addition to the following tools, the tools
     for understanding the challenge in Chapter 8 can be used with participatory design.


     DESIGN CARDS

     Design cards are cards that each depict a single concept via text or imagery. Design
     cards can be used in various ways to guide collaborative idea generation and critique,
     prompt the team to consider the wider spectrum of key issues, or help participants
     sequence or prioritize concepts, such as for modeling of workflows or processes.
     And the value of design cards doesn’t end with the initial design phase. For instance,
     they can be used as cues to help stakeholders critique aspects of a design in support
     of development iterations. Using a set of learning sciences concept cards might help
     end users respond to various questions about the prototype design, such as “How
     well did we do in addressing this aspect of learning?”
            A set of cards typically covers a comprehensive topic, but beyond that,
     there’s much variation. A set may reference more generalizable concepts (such as
     instructional tactics) using text, or it might include personalized images created
     for a specific context. For example, in Chapter 3, Medic Mobile used custom-made
     cards to describe elements of a health system. Each card included a single, sketched
     concept, such as community members, health care workers, places, technology, and
     activities. By using the cards during participatory design workshops, stakeholders
     were better able to conceptualize the wider system, engage in detailed discussions
     about the health care workflow, and share stories from their own experiences.
            You can make your own design cards based on the principles in this book or
     your unique learning engineering context, or you can search online for pre-made
     cards suitable for your design challenge. For instance, Transcend, a nonprofit that
     works with K-12 schools, has developed a set of cards that helps educators identify
     the most relevant concepts from the learning sciences for a particular educational
     experience.13 Similarly, InGlobal Learning Design offers a set of design mindset
     cards with thought-provoking and playful prompts that teams can use to help stim-
     ulate collaboration and generate better ideas.14 Famed design firm IDEO also offers
     cards for co-designing schools, such as its equitable learning practices cards.15 (In
     addition to its cards, IDEO launched a nonprofit business for educators that offers a
     number of open-access tools. See www.designkit.org for details.)
                                                 Human-Centered Design Tools | 285



CARD SORTING

Card sorting is one specific use of cards. Simply put, in a card sorting activity,
stakeholders categorize the cards into conceptual piles. This technique is popular
for user interface and user experience (UI / UX) design, because it demonstrates
how end users mentally “lump and split” concepts. UI designers might use that
information to create menus for an app or a website’s navigation. Card sorting can
also be used for other kinds of classification or prioritization activities—anything
where grouping is important. When performing a card sort, simply create the cards
and then either define the groupings (closed sort) or let the end users create their own
(open sort).


AFFINITY DIAGRAMS

Similar to card sorting, affinity diagrams ask stakeholders to sort concepts into log-
ical groupings. Unlike typical card sorts, though, affinity diagrams begin with a
blank set of sticky notes, and they’re designed for groups of stakeholders to complete
collaboratively. Affinity diagrams are useful in situations where a team needs to
sort through a large number of concepts—particularly when some of those concepts
might still be unknown to the larger group. Consider using this technique to rapidly
uncover concepts and their relationships—for instance, if the team is approaching a
brand-new design project or has an incomplete picture of a complex context.
       To complete an affinity diagram, follow these steps: 16
       Ask participants to silently jot down concepts, one per sticky note
       Post the ideas for everyone to see, like on a real or digital white board
       Collectively sort the notes into conceptual categories
       Promote an existing card or create a new header card for each group
       Create superheaders if two or more groups have a strong relationship
       Redraw the concepts and categories as a finished document

Figure 12.3 shows a simplified example of what an affinity diagram might look like
if stakeholders were asked to identify and then sort data science knowledge and
skills into a set of learning objectives.
286 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




    FIGURE 12.3. Example of an affinity diagram with potential data science topics




     Personas
     As described in Chapter 3, personas are representations of key stakeholders. Typi-
     cally, a project will require several (say, three to twelve) personas to represent differ-
     ent stakeholder groups or meaningful variations within a single group.

           [A persona is] not a description of a real, single user or an average user.
           The persona represents patterns of users’ behavior, goals, and motives,
           compiled in a fictional description of a single individual. It also contains
           made-up personal details, in order to make the persona more “tangible
           and alive” for the development team.17

            Personas should be based on qualitative and quantitative demographic data;
     use what’s been learned about the learner population (or populations as well as
     other stakeholders) to guide your generation of real-world persona variations.18 It’s
     important that your sets of personas cover the major variations across those who will
     engage with the implementation of your design. Ideally, personas should help surface
     variations in each relevant group, including different goals, backgrounds, cultures,
     and cognitive and physical abilities consistent with the general demographics. As
     you work on your personas consider the following:
                                                   Human-Centered Design Tools | 287



       Name
       Personality and traits
       Culture and background
       Social context (such as peers, mentors, and family)
       Abilities and disabilities
       Prior knowledge and experiences
       Goals
       Motivations (likes)
       Frustrations (dislikes)
       Environment and other learning context factors
       Access to relevant tools or technologies


Scenarios, User Stories, and Use Cases
Narrative is a powerful tool in human-centered design. For example, learning engi-
neers can write scenarios about the personas they develop. These help highlight a
persona’s motivations, context, and other characteristics and tell the story about how
a representative learner can be expected to navigate the learning experience being
designed. Here’s a brief example:

      Daniel sat at his family’s shared computer and logged into his school’s
      learning management system. His sister played nearby, watching car-
      toons on the television, and Daniel had to work hard to pay attention . . .

      User stories have some similarities to scenarios, but the practice of writing
user stories grew out of software development and Agile management. (See
Chapter 11 for more information on Agile.) User stories are meant to be short (only
one or two sentences), informal, and easy to read, and they should describe how a
person interacts with the intended system. A development project may have dozens
or hundreds of these small user stories. The idea is that each should describe one
meaningful feature of the in-development solution, and the product team should
be able to accomplish the development of a user-story feature in one sprint. Bigger
features that can’t fit into a single user story are called epics, and related user stories
might be combined under a collective epic.
      There are several user story templates; the most common one is below:

     As a     (STAKEHOLDER)     I want to      (TASK)    so that    (DESIRED RESULT)
288 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     An example user story made in this template might be:

           As a teacher, I want to know which students didn’t complete their
           assignments by the deadline so that I can check in with them.

            Finally, use cases are more like recipes. Use cases are written descriptions of
     how different stakeholders are expected to engage with a system. A set of use cases
     should show the variety of ways stakeholders might interact with a system, their
     different reactions, and the various outputs that might ensue. Each use case typically
     includes the following: 19
            Actor: Who’s interacting with the system (which stakeholder or group)?
            System: What product or experience is the actor interacting with?
            Goals: What does the actor want to accomplish?
            Preconditions: What must be true before this use case would take place?
            Triggers: What event caused the use case to begin?
            Basic Flow (Success): How does an actor complete the path successfully?
            Alternative Flows: What different or unsuccessful paths exist?

     A brief (simplified) example is shown in Figure 12.4.

      USE CASE            Access Assignment In-Box

      ACTOR               Teacher

      SYSTEM              Learning Management System (LMS)

      GOALS               Identify students who have (or have not) submitted their assignments

      PRECONDITIONS       Assignment was released (opened) to students in the LMS

      TRIGGERS            Actor with admin privileges opens an active assignment
                          After releasing an assignment in the LMS, the teacher opens
                          the assignment in-box to see the class’s submission statuses.
      BASIC FLOW          If the assignment deadline has passed, then the teacher
                          collects the names of the late students and may follow up
                          with them through the LMS, email, or text messaging.

     FIGURE 12.4. Simplified use case example


     Human-Centered Requirements Definition
     In general, the tools and analyses discussed so far in this chapter inform the design
     of a learning engineering solution, but at some point, design activities need to
                                                 Human-Centered Design Tools | 289



mature into tangible development work. Defining system requirements supports
that process. Requirements are succinct, formal descriptions of what a system must
accomplish, how well it needs to function, and applicable constraints or limitations.
       A successful project is likely to have dozens, if not hundreds, of requirements
to inform its components’ functionality and performance expectations. But not all
requirements are the same. As you consider your goals and constraints, look for
requirements that fit each of these categories (and note the examples of each listed
after the requirement category in parentheses):

       Functional requirements: What a component does (“plays movie”)
       Nonfunctional requirements: Qualities of a system (“usability”)
       Project drivers: Reasons or goals for the project (“reduce attrition”)
       Project constraints: Restrictions on the process (“must be under $1M”)
       Project issues: Project management conditions (“iterative design”)

       Writing effective requirements—especially human-centric ones—is a critical
part of systems development. In fact, research shows that many failed projects are
caused, at least in part, by a failure to sufficiently consider human-centered require-
ments. For example, one large-scale study on technology-based development in the
United States found that only 34 percent of projects were successful, 15 percent
completely failed, and 51 percent were only partially successful. Projects most fre-
quently failed because of “(1) an inadequate understanding of the intended users and
the context of use, and (2) vague usability requirements, such as ‘the system must
be intuitive to use.’” 20 To avoid these traps, it’s important to know a lot about who
will interact with your system, where and how they’ll interact with it, and what they
really care about—and then to translate those design factors into effective system re-
quirements that articulate the corresponding concrete functions and qualities. Also,
make sure to consider concepts unique to a learning experience. For example, which
of the learning science concepts from Chapter 9 apply? What kinds of learner vari-
ability does your system need to support? Which tools for learner motivation (from
Chapter 15) should be included? And from Chapter 13, what data elements need to
be collected, and what instrumentation is necessary to support those requirements?
       There are plenty of existing requirements templates and spreadsheets avail-
able online. Some templates are very simple, like sticky notes on a kanban board
as shown in Figure 11.2; other projects require more details or formal management
processes. Find an approach that fits your needs or customize the example shown in
Figure 12.5, which is adapted from the Volere Requirements Template.21
290 | Learning Engineering Toolkit
                                                                                 RELATIVE
                                     REQUIREMENT                                  IMPORTANCE
                                     CATEGORY

        ID#: UNIQUE ID      Type:                                    Priority:


                       ONE SENTENCE DESCRIPTION OF THE REQUIREMENT


        Rationale: REQUIREMENT JUSTIFICATION
        Originator: STAKEHOLDER (OR GROUP) WHO RAISED THIS REQUIREMENT
        Criterion: A METRIC FOR THE REQUIREMENT FOR TESTABILITY
        Satisfaction: 1 2 3 4 5              Dissatisfaction: 1 2 3 4 5
        Conflicts: ANY CONFLICTING REQUIREMENTS
        Events: IDS OF RELEVANT USE-CASE EVENTS LINKED TO THIS REQUIREMENT

       History: CHANGE LOG INFO            Supporting Materials: LINK TO MORE INFO

DEGREE OF STAKEHOLDER HAPPINESS IF THE            DEGREE OF STAKEHOLDER UNHAPPINESS
REQUIREMENT IS SUCCESSFULLY IMPLEMENTED           IF THE REQUIREMENT ISN’T IMPLEMENTED
(1=MEH TO 5=EXTREMELY PLEASED)                    (1=MEH TO 5=EXTREMELY DISPLEASED)

     FIGURE 12.5. Example of a requirement template


     LOGIC MODELS AND CONJECTURE MAPPING

     A significant challenge in iteratively designing learning experiences is keeping track
     of the reasons for decisions made along the way—for instance, design choices made
     based on theoretical principles, forced by necessary trade-offs (such as from con-
     straints or competing priorities), or revised in response to new information uncov-
     ered through iterative testing. Two specialized techniques, logic models and conjec-
     ture maps, can help you keep track of decision-making over time.
            A logic model is a representation of the design team’s expectations about the
     connections between a system, its policy inputs (resources, strategies, activities),
     and its desired outcomes.

           In the most basic terms, logic models provide a kind of map for a pro-
           gram or initiative, helping clarify a program or policy’s destination, the
           pathways toward the destination, and markers along the way. . . . [A]
           logic model helps clarify both what the program, initiative, or policy
                                                          Human-Centered Design Tools | 291



         is and what it is not. This kind of clarification is helpful in building an
         evaluation design that can capture the program’s or policy’s influence
         and impact.22

   Figure 12.6 shows a simplified logic model structure. In practice, you’d fill in such
   a table with summaries derived from more detailed supporting tables, and you’d fill
   out each of those through a collaborative design activity. Logic models are useful
   throughout a learning engineering project—from initial planning through iterative
   testing, implementation, and evaluation. They’re best developed early (for instance,
   to clarify goals, make underlying beliefs explicit, identify logical assumptions and
   gaps, and build consensus) and then revisited periodically (for instance, to help clar-
   ify when and how to conduct evaluations or to refine components based on testing
   results).
          The US Department of Education’s Institute of Education Sciences has
   published an open-access toolkit for building logic models for learning-oriented
   projects. Its toolkit includes templates, examples, and even suggested agendas for
   participatory design workshops.23

Problem Statement:
  Resources       Strategies                          Short-term         Long-term
                                     Outputs                                               Impacts
   (Inputs)      and Activities                       Outcomes           Outcomes
What resources   What will the    What are the       What changes      What changes     What are the
are or could     activities,      initial products   are expected in   do you want      anticipated
reasonably be    events, and      of these           the short term?   to occur after   changes over
available?       such be?         activities?                          the initial      the long haul?
                                                                       outcomes?




Assumptions:

FIGURE 12.6. Sample components of an education-themed logic model 24

          Similar to logic models, conjecture maps help articulate the logical
   relationships between initial beliefs and desired outcomes, but in contrast to logic
   models, conjecture maps tend to be more detailed and focused. Conjecture mapping
   is used to document the relevant features of a learning experience (such as the
292 | Learning Engineering Toolkit


                                                   MEDIATING
     THEORY                  EMBODIMENT            PROCESSES              OUTCOMES
     Metacognitive             Explanation            Planful         Increased Knowledge
     practice, combined          Guides              Activities          of Metacognition
     with expert
     modeling and            Problem-Based
     coaching, will help        Scenario              Good
                                                   Explanations          Increased Self-
     learners develop
                                                                         Monitoring Skill
     metacognitive skills   EXPLANATION
                                                   Practice and
                             Problem-Based        Experimentation      Increased Reflective
                                Learning                                  Judgment Skill
                             Expert Modeling         Worked
                                of Thinking          Examples
                               (Talk Aloud)

                              Metacognitive       Self-Regulated
                               Prompting            Learning

                            ACTIVITY
                                                   Self-Reflection
                                                  and Assessment


     FIGURE 12.7. Example conjecture map




                               SHIFTS
     FIGURE 12.8.
                                FROM              FROM               FROM
     Example theory             Confused about    Doesn’t think      Thinks math is
     of change activity         fractional        they’re good at    boring and
     (step 3)                   numbers           math               frustrating
                                TO                TO                 TO
                                Does fractional   Believes they      Sees math
                                arithmetic        can do math if     problems as fun
                                reliably          they try           puzzles to solve
      CONCEPTS
        CONCEPT 1
        Activate prior              GOOD FIT           GOOD FIT
        knowledge


        CONCEPT 2
        Problem-based                  GOOD FIT     PARTIAL FIT       PARTIAL FIT
        learning


        CONCEPT 3
        Use gamification
        motivators                                 PARTIAL FIT           GOOD FIT
                                                   Human-Centered Design Tools | 293



materials, technologies, and environment) and explain how they’re predicted to work
together to produce the desired outcomes.25 The idea is to draw an explicit line from
a conjecture (or set of conjectures) derived from theories about how people learn, to
how the conjecture(s) will be embodied within a particular learning experience (such
as through specific instructional interventions or technologies), to the mediating
processes (in other words, how the embodied interventions are expected to effect
change), and finally to the desired outcomes. (See Figure 12.7.) Conjecture maps
can be used in conjunction with logic models or other requirements engineering
processes, but they’re also useful standalone tools, particularly when it comes to
testing, diagnosing, and refining the pedagogical (or andragogical or heutagogical)
components of a learning engineering solution.
       If you’re not sure which conjectures or mediating processes to include, then
it may be useful to complete a theory of change activity. A “theory of change”
is a description of why and how change—such as achieving learning outcomes or
fostering learner motivation—is desirable and achievable in a particular context.
The open-access IDEO Design Kit includes an activity template and worksheet,26
and it describes the activity process in five steps:
      1.   Write out each shift (like learning or motivation) you’re trying to create
      2.   Identify possible concepts or activities that may achieve those outcomes
      3.   Create a matrix of goals by concepts; mark those most likely to succeed
      4.   Narrow down your set of priorities
      5.   Document your final decisions

Figure 12.8 shows an example snapshot of step three in the process. Refer to the
IDEO Design Kit for additional details.


Prototyping
A prototype is a draft version of a design that allows for demonstration of its concepts.27
Prototypes can range from low-fidelity paper drawings to high-fidelity interactive
models that showcase features on a subset of content. Early in the design process, it’s
useful to create quick and inexpensive prototypes like hand-drawn sketches.28 Use
these to refine ideas, communicate with the rest of the learning engineering team,
and get feedback from stakeholders. As design and development work progresses, the
prototypes need to become gradually more realistic. Mid-fidelity prototypes might
include wireframes (user interface sketches done by hand or with a digital design
application,29 such as shown in Figure 12.9) and storyboards (sketches of the flow of
294 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     an experience over time). High-fidelity prototypes are typically functional—albeit
     pared down or incomplete. For example, an advanced software prototype might
     include a fully functional computer-based application with realistic user interactions
     that cover one learning module within a curriculum.30 The IDEO Design Kit offers
     worksheets and advice on how to prototype and iteratively evaluate your prototypes.
     It recommends you “find fast and scrappy ways to do your testing. This stage of the
     design process could take anything from a few days to a few weeks, depending on
     what you’re testing and how many rounds of prototyping you want to do.” 31




     FIGURE 12.9. Early prototypes can be quick, hand-drawn sketches



     End-User Test and Evaluation
     Each part of a learning engineering design can (and, resources permitting, should)
     be tested, but testing doesn’t need to be difficult or complex. Consider this advice
     from famed usability expert Jakob Nielsen. Although he’s remarking on usability
     assessments in particular, his advice broadly applies to user testing, in general: 32

           Some people think that . . . user tests should be reserved for the rare web
           design project with a huge budget and a lavish time schedule. Not true.
           Elaborate usability tests are a waste of resources. The best results come
           from testing no more than five users and running as many small tests as
           you can afford.
     Later, Nielsen adds a sardonic caveat:
                                                  Human-Centered Design Tools | 295



      The most striking truth . . . is that zero users give zero insights.

Testing each part of a learning engineering design
may include validating assumptions—about your
needs analyses, requirements, personas, scenarios
and use cases, learning design elements, and proto-            AVOID THE “LAST MILE ”
types—with people your design is intended to serve.             PROBLEM WHE RE YOU
Don’t reserve testing for finished products. As the               COLLECT DATA BUT
Math Readiness development process described in                DON’ T USE IT TO INFORM
Chapter 3 demonstrated, it’s important to test your             DECISIONS OR ACTIONS
design components early and often—even just the              (BEC AUSE OF LACK OF TIME ,
paper-based sketches.                                            EXPE RTISE , OR SO ON)
       When planning your testing activities, use
the following questions to help guide your measure-
ment approach:

       What features or functions are you testing?
       How precise (sensitive) do your measures need to be?
       Which stakeholder groups need to be included among participants?
       Have you identified an appropriately representative set of participants?
       How many people need to participate to generate sufficient data?
       What conditions (like the testing environment) might skew results?
       How closely does the testing context need to replicate the learning context?
       What assessment tools (like surveys or post-tests) do you need?
       Can you instrument the learning activity to collect relevant data?
       Are your assessment tools suitably valid and reliable?
       What ethical safeguards are needed? (See Chapters 7 and 17.)
       How will you analyze and use the resulting outcomes from the test?
       Have you left enough time to take action in response to the test results?

      The qualitative and quantitative research methods discussed earlier in this
chapter can be used for testing. Early in the design process, testing may involve
lightweight approaches, like reactions surveys and focus groups; however, more
mature prototypes and products will likely require stronger measures of their
performance. For training and education contexts, Donald Kirkpatrick’s four-
levels evaluation model is a handy way to conceptualize learning assessment.33
The model starts with easier to measure—but less reliable and potentially less
296 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     impactful—targets, and then it progresses to more difficult to measure—but likely
     more valuable—ones:
           1.   Reactions: What did learners think? Did they enjoy the experience?
           2.   Learning: What did they learn? (Usually a pre / post-test combination)
           3.   Behavior: Did the experience affect future behaviors, like job activities?
           4.   Results: Was there a noticeable impact, like a company’s performance?


     USABILITY TESTING

     Usability testing is one particular kind of end-user testing. It involves testing a prod-
     uct with representative end users to examine its human-system interface in order to
     identify problems in the design of the solution, uncover opportunities to improve or
     add features, and learn about the target population’s behavior and preferences.34
            Typically, usability testing involves a facilitator who asks participants to
     complete a series of tasks using the system. The facilitator (or their fellow team
     members) observes and records participants’ behaviors, in particular noting anoma-
     lies from expected actions. The facilitator may also encourage participants to use a
     “think aloud” protocol, where they’re asked to say whatever comes to mind while
     they’re interacting with the system, such as “I can’t find the menu option I’m look-
     ing for” or “that animation was cute.” The facilitator may also ask or answer ques-
     tions—although they must be careful to avoid inadvertently skewing the test results.
     Digital.gov from the US General Services Administration has open-access tips and
     training videos for conducting usability testing,35 and the usability.gov site offers
     additional methods, templates, and examples.36
            Usability testing is often combined with a reactions survey of some kind. One
     of the most frequently used is the System Usability Scale (SUS). It’s a “quick and
     dirty” questionnaire that collects end-user usability reactions about ease of use, sat-
     isfaction, and learnability.37 The SUS has ten questions, and participants rate each
     item on a scale from one to five. (See Figure 12.10.)
            Scoring the SUS takes some effort, because some of the questions are nega-
     tively worded while others are positively phrased.
           • For odd-numbered items, subtract one from the user response.
           • For even-numbered items, subtract the user responses from five.
           • Add up the converted numbers per person and multiply that total by 2.5.
     Multiplying the final score by 2.5 converts the range to 0–100 so that scores can be
     more easily understood. After calculating each participant’s individual score, you
                                                       Human-Centered Design Tools | 297



can use basic statistical methods to find group outcomes, like mean, median, and
standard deviation. In general, an overall (aggregate) SUS score for a system of 68
is considered above average, and scores above 80.3 are in the top 10 percent—those
are A-quality systems.38


SYSTEM USABILITY SCALE
           Score each item from 1 (Strongly Disagree) to 5 (Strongly Agree)

1. I think that I would like to use this system frequently.
2. I found the system unnecessarily complex.
3. I thought the system was easy to use.
4. I think that I would need the support of a technical person to be able to use this system.
5. I found the various functions in this system were well integrated.
6. I thought there was too much inconsistency in this system.
7. I would imagine that most people would learn to use this system very quickly.
8. I found the system very cumbersome to use.
9. I felt very confident using the system.
10. I needed to learn a lot of things before I could get going with this system.

FIGURE 12.10. SUS questionnaire



Pulling It All Together
This chapter introduced a broad toolkit of human-centered design techniques—and
these only scratch the surface. But don’t get overwhelmed. Remember, the goal of
human-centered design is to understand the interactions among people and the other
elements of the system in order to optimize human well-being and overall perfor-
mance. Select the most relevant techniques that will help you achieve those goals.
No single technique is required, and it’s OK to adjust techniques to fit your needs
and project constraints. Just remember that witty advice from Jakob Nielsen: While
it’s smart to be lightweight and efficient with human-centered design and testing
activities, zero activities gives zero results. Integration of human-centered design is
core to achieving learning engineering goals.

      Last modified 2014. www.shiftelearning.com/blog/bid/345394/4-steps-to-becoming-a-learner-

    • Quintana, Chris, Namsoo Shin, Cathleen Norris, and Elliot Soloway. “Learner-Centered
      Design: Reflections on the Past and Directions for the Future.” In The Cambridge Handbook
      of the Learning Sciences, edited by R. Keith Sawyer, 119–134. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge
      University Press, 2014. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511816833.020

9   LEARNING-EXPERIENCE DESIGN RESOURCES
    • LXD.org is a community hub for learning experience designers. See: https://lxd.org
    • Gutierrez, Karla. “Learning Experience Design: An Overview and 11 of the Best Resources.”
      SH!FT. www.shiftelearning.com/blog/learning-experience-design-best-resources

10 UNIVERSAL DESIGN FOR LEARNING (UDL) RESOURCES
    • CAST (which once stood for Center for Applied Special Technology) is a nonprofit education
      research and development organization that created the Universal Design for Learning
      framework and UDL Guidelines. CAST provides tutorials and resources for inclusive and
      accessible design for learning. See: www.cast.org
    • Meyer, Anne, David H. Rose, and David Gordon. Universal Design for Learning: Theory and
      Practice. Wakefield, MA: CAST, 2014.
      http://castpublishing.org/books-media/udl-theory-and-practice

      The Cambridge Handbook of the Learning Sciences, edited by R. Keith Sawyer, 153–170. 2nd
      ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
      https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9781139519526.011
    • Research–practice partnerships often use design-based implementation research to adapt, mix,
      and merge curricula and resources for use in particular venues. For more information on these
      practices, including other readings and case studies, see:
           – College of Education. “Research-Practice Partnerships and DBIR.” University of
