CHAPTER 13


Data Instrumentation Tools
by Erin Czerwinski, Jim Goodell, Steve Ritter,
Robert Sottilare, and Khanh-Phuong Thai



This chapter provides process tools for deciding what data to collect and
for designing instrumentation for the collection of those data. It supports
practical application of concepts introduced in Chapter 5, Learning
Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation. This chapter also
includes references to standards-based open-source software resources
for instrumenting learning experiences.


In Chapter 5 we examined the essential role of data collection in the learning engi-
neering process. Instrumentation design and implementation are integral for this.
During the creation phase we’re not only creating learning experiences and things
that impact the conditions for learning but also determining the data we’ll use to
inform iterative improvement of those products. This might mean designing and
developing new sensors or new data pipelines—new ways of capturing, processing,
and tagging collected data to help us answer questions. Other times, the necessary
data are already captured by a learning platform, and we just need to confirm that
they’re being analyzed and used in meaningful ways.


  START WITH THE QUESTION, “What do we want to learn from the data?”

       One approach to answering that question is to consider the first or next
implementation of our learning engineering creation as a research trial. Are we
testing one possible solution against the status quo? Or are we testing two or more
variations? We should have an idea about the desired outcome, that is, a theory
based on the learning sciences and / or a hypothesis based on what we know about
the specific challenge. This research trial will test our hypothesis or compare two or


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-18
304 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     more options for meeting the challenge.
            The next question is, “What are the possible ways we can test that theory or
     outcome using data?” We don’t need perfect measures, only indicators. The most
     accurate way to measure a student outcome on a five-minute learning experience
     may be pre- and post-tests that take twenty minutes each. However, we don’t have
     that luxury in most cases—nor do we typically have laboratory-like conditions in
     which to finely control the testing. Often instrumentation and sensors need to be
     unobtrusive and suitable for ecological (real-world) conditions. Is there another way
     that we can capture enough data to test our creation(s) with little or no additional
     work for learners and teachers?
            We might ask, “Are sensors and a pipeline already in place?” Sometimes a
     learning platform or learning management system already logs learner interactions
     and outcomes. If not, we ask, “What resources do we have to create the needed sen-
     sor(s) and pipeline?” If the data collection mechanisms aren’t already in place and
     we don’t have the technical resources to develop new mechanisms from scratch, then
     there may be some open-source tools or creative approaches that could work. The
     following tools are designed to help in this process.


     Data Instrumentation Decision Tool
     Complete for each question that you want the data to answer:
        Question: What’s the question to answer? Who wants to know? Why?
        Method: How can we answer this question using data? What trial or research
         methods should be used? What analysis techniques? How can we measure
         unobtrusively?
        Metrics: What do we need to calculate from the data to answer the question?
        Data Elements: What data elements must be collected at what grain size for
         the metric and analysis?
        Sources: Are the sensor(s) and pipeline already in place to collect the needed
         elements? If not, what are our options for creating the instrumentation?
        Quality: What assumptions do we have about the quality of data from the
         existing sources? Can we check those assumptions, for example, with data
         profiling tools? Was the instrumentation intended for a different purpose?
         Does the quality hold up for the new purpose and metrics? Are there other
         aspects of quality that will impact its use for this new purpose?
                                                       Data Instrumentation Tools | 305



xAPI: Getting to the Data
As discussed in Chapter 5, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation,
the Experience Application Programming Interface (xAPI) is an open-source data
standard that helps learning engineers instrument sensors to get interoperable,
granular, context-rich, and semantically linked data out of them. In practice, xAPI
includes three main parts: coded statements (like the one below) that are written into
your learning technology, a learning record store (a data store you implement) to
catch and validate the xAPI outputs, and xAPI Profiles that help us have consistency
in our data elements and terminology across different experiences and systems.


EXAMPLE X API STATEMENT IN JSON

{
“actor”: {
    “objectType”: “Agent”,
    “name”: “Ann Advisor”,
    “mbox_sha1sum”: “BF4479FD4A834CFC4033C40AB58F5DBBACC599BB”
  },
“verb”: {
    “id”: “https://w3id.org/xapi/dod-isd/verbs/advised”,
    “display”: {
              “fr”: “conseiller”,
              “en-US”: “advised”
      }
  },
“object”: {
    “objectType”: “Agent”,
    “name”: “A. Learner”
    “mbox”: “mailto:pojulgnatt@abcde.com”,
  },
“timestamp”: “2021-11-09T11:20:32.321−05:00”
}

FIGURE 13.1. An example xAPI statement for an event where “Ann Advisor”
(actor) “advised” (verb) “A. Learner” (object). See also the Yet Analytics xAPI
Statement Validator at http://yetanalytics.github.io/xapi-schema-demo
306 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     And the figure below shows an example web page instrumented with xAPI.


   1. <head>
   2.   <!-- Get reusable scripts that hide the complexity -->
   3.   <script type=“text/javascript” src=“./js/jimsxapiwrapper.js”></script>
   4.
   5.   <!-- Script sets variables, calls scripts to format, sends Statement -->
   6.   <script>
   7.   function send_statement(value){
   8.         var conf = {
   9.              “endpoint” : “https://lrs.adlnet.gov/xapi/”,
   10.              “auth” : “Basic ” + toBase64(“xapi-tools:xapi-tools”)
   11.              };
   12.         ADL.XAPIWrapper.changeConfig(conf);
   13.
   14.         // Set values and set up xAPI Statement in the right format
   15.         var actorName = “Your Name Here”; // Change to your name or alias
   16.         var actorMail = “mailto:Tester@example.com”;
   17.         var verbId = “http://example.com/xapi/interacted;
   18.         var verbDisplay = “interacted”;
   19.         var objectId = “http://example.com/button_example”,
   20.         var resultResponse = value; // “value” is name button clicked
   21.    // Call a function to construct the xAPI statement behind the scenes
   22.         var statement = GOODELL.XAPIWrapper.setupSimpleStatement();
   23.
   24.         // Dispatch the statement to the LRS
   25.         var result = ADL.XAPIWrapper.sendStatement(statement);
   26.    }
   27.    </script>
   28. </head>
   29.
   30. <body>
   31.    <!-- “name” is optional “response” + (actor) (interacted) (button) -->
   32. <p>Instrumentation is easy.</p>
   33.    <button type=“button” name=“True”
                         onclick=“send_statement(this.name)”>True</button>
   34.     <button type=“button” name=“False”
                         onclick=“send_statement(this.name)”>False</button>
   35. </body>
   36. </html>


     FIGURE 13.2. Adapted from Anthony Altieri’s 2017 Learning Solutions
     article. Example results at http://adlnet.github.io/xapi-statement-viewer.
     NOTES: This uses functions in lines 22 and 25 to call a separate script file (jimsxapiwrapper.js) loaded in line
     3 to minimize the code needed in the web page. This is a simplified example for illustration purposes. In real
     implementations the basic authentication used here would be replaced with more robust security protocols.
                                                     Data Instrumentation Tools | 307



Instrumentation in Commercial Platforms
USING DATA THAT ARE ALREADY COLLECTED BY AN
ONLINE PLATFORM TO ANSWER SOME QUESTIONS

Most learning management systems already collect information about the learning
activities a learner launches, times assignments are submitted, assignment scores,
and other basic interactions. These platforms also log information about usage of
the system and resources within the system. Those data may or may not include
the details needed to answer all of your learning engineering questions—but that
information may be able to answer some questions. Be careful when using data from
instrumentation designed for a different purpose; the data labels may suggest mean-
ing that wasn’t intended, like in the story in Chapter 5 where the use of “SUCCEED”
made sense to the software developers but the learning analytics team interpreted
it differently. In any case, it’s good to know what data are already available before
developing new instrumentation.


GOOGLE CLASSROOM CONNECTOR FOR K-12

The Google Classroom Connector, by InnovateEDU, is a free and open-source data
extraction tool for any school district that uses G Suite for Education. No external
software or application is needed. Using the connector, Google Classroom data
are loaded into Google BigQuery, a fully managed data warehouse within Google
Cloud. BigQuery allows you to analyze data using SQL, and it provides the option
to connect to popular business intelligence reporting tools such as Data Studio,
Tableau, Power BI, or Looker. This allows districts to create data dashboards or
reports using up-to-date Google Classroom data, for instance, to provide insights
into student engagement and performance.1


LEARNING MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS AND LEARNING PLATFORMS

Each commercial learning management system (LMS) has its own approach to
making internal data available in analytics tools or for visualization in the platform’s
own tools. Commercial vendors may have proprietary logging, use instrumentation
standards such as Caliper, xAPI, or a mix of the two. The scope of the data is
largely determined by the functions within the system. For example, some online
assessments may be automatically scored but the detailed data about a learner’s
308 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     interaction with a specific assessment item and metadata about that item may not be
     available. If your team is engineering or curating learning activities for delivery in
     a specific learning management platform, you can start by identifying the data that
     are already being and are available to you.


     WEB LOG ANALYTICS

     At a minimum, learner interactions with anything hosted on a website can be col-
     lected from web logs and analyzed using tools like Google Analytics. Web log files
     are automatically created by web servers. These logs record website hits, such as
     document or image views, and contains information about who visited a site, where
     they came from, and what they did on it. However, these logs probably don’t have
     enough detail to answer most learning engineering questions.



     ASSISTMENTS AND E-TRIALS
     Worcester Polytechnic Institute’s ASSISTments is a forever-free online platform for
     assigning and assessing math homework. ASSISTments was developed and proven
     effective under federal grants. The efficacy research was conducted in middle school
     mathematics, but the platform has been used at other grades, including higher educa-
     tion, and for other academic subjects. In 2017, MIT researchers called ASSISTments
     one of the two most promising technology-based interventions in the US K-12
     education system. In 2020, the What Works Clearinghouse funded a study to figure
     out what distance learning tools were effective, and ASSISTments was deemed to be
     one of three—out of the over nine hundred reviewed.2 ASSISTments is used by more
     than 20,000 teachers and their 500,000 students in addition to researchers studying
     pedagogy and learning.
            E-TRIALS (Ed Tech Research Infrastructure to Advance Learning Science)
     is a joint project between The ASSISTments Foundation and Worcester Polytechnic
     Institute that leverages ASSISTments to support researchers to create randomized
     controlled trials that strengthen the science of learning. New infrastructure, funded
     by Schmidt Futures, is being developed to reduce the cost and increase the volume
     of studies conducted using ASSISTments. While it was originally developed with a
     focus on learning sciences and educational research, E-TRIALS could be used for
     A/B testing and randomized controlled trials of EdTech products, online learning
     activities, or instructional content.
                                                    Data Instrumentation Tools | 309



X API LEARNING COHORT
The xAPI Learning Cohort is a free, vendor-neutral twelve-week project-based team
learning experience. Participants form teams based on shared interests and work
together on an xAPI project. xAPI Cohorts run twice a year in spring and fall with
weekly all-group web meetings. See xapicohort.com.


INSTRUMENTATION STANDARDS

Some commercial platforms may use standardized data formats and interfaces.
That’s a good thing! Standards make extracting data easier, so it’s worthwhile to
look for (or to build) systems that use interoperability standards, such as:
      •   xAPI (see the simple example in this chapter)
      •   OpenSimon tools (if a course is on the OpenSimon platform)
      •   Caliper (if a course is on an LMS using LTI and Caliper standards)
      •   Human Performance Markup Language (for fine-grained measures in
          certain training domains)
It’s best to look for systems that use published standards created by voluntary con-
sensus standards bodies, such as the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
Learning Technology Standards Committee, the World Wide Web Consortium,
the Simulation Interoperability Standards Organization, or the International
Organization for Standardization. These organizations plan, develop, establish, and
coordinate standards using agreed-upon procedures, and importantly, they maintain
the standards through regular governance. They also adhere to principles such as
openness, balance of interest, and due process.3



UPGRADE
UpGrade is a free open-source, dashboard-based web service that facilitates design-
ing and deploying randomized A/B tests and fair comparisons of learning experi-
ences in educational software. The tool allows developers of educational software
and online learning experiences to define and run A/B tests to explore what works
best for supporting the most effective learning outcomes. Information, guides, and
source code are available at www.upgradeplatform.org.
310 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Endnotes

       www.landingzone.org/google-connector
     2 Sahni, SD, JR Polanin, Q. Zhang, LE Michaelson, S. Caverly, ML Polese, and J. Yang. A What
       Works Clearinghouse Rapid Evidence Review of Distance Learning Programs (WWC 2021-
       005REV). Washington, DC: US Department of Education, Institute of Education Sciences,
       National Center for Education Evaluation and Regional Assistance, What Works Clearinghouse.
       https://eric.ed.gov/?id=ED610886
     3 “Promoting Innovation, Competition, and Economic Growth: Principles for Effective Domestic
       and International Standards Development.” National Institute of Standards and Technology
       (NIST), US Department of Commerce, February 9, 2012. www.nist.gov/speech-testimony/
       promoting-innovation-competition-and-economic-growth-principles-effective-domestic
     4 Closing quotation from Park, Robert E., Wolfhart B. Goethert, and William A. Florac.
       Goal-Driven Software Measurement. A Guidebook. Pittsburgh: Software Engineering
       Institute, Carnegie Mellon University, 1996. https://resources.sei.cmu.edu/asset_files/
       Handbook/1996_002_001_16436.pdf




                  In goal-driven measurement, the
               primary question is not “What metrics
               should I use?” but “What do I want to
                           know or learn?”

                      – Goal-Driven Software Measurement 4
CHAPTER 14


Software and Technology
Standards as Tools
by Jim Goodell, Andrew J. Hampton, Richard Tong, and Sae Schatz



This chapter gives a high-level map of current concepts from computer
science, computer engineering, and software engineering relevant to
learning engineering. It also covers technology and data standards used
by engineers for scalable interoperable learning solutions.


The most scalable and impactful learning engineering endeavors leverage software
engineering and computing technology. Examples featured in this book such as
Duolingo and edX have a global scale, only possible due to modern software and
telecommunications. Great advances in human-computer interface design, artificial
intelligence, distributed computing, and distributed identity are being applied to rev-
olutionize learning at scale.
       Learning engineering also addresses challenges that go beyond EdTech. For
example, learning engineering may be applied to re-engineer entire school systems
using principles such as modularization, human-centered design, and social systems
design. In these cases, solutions may focus on optimizing social structures, physical
spaces, and human-centric practices, but the process will likely use data analytics
and computer-aided design and may incorporate some technology elements in the
final solution. In other words, nearly all learning engineering challenges will involve
technology at some point, so it’s important for all members of a learning engineering
team to have a baseline level of understanding about these concepts.
       The following reference is intended mainly for those without a background
in computer science or software engineering. It’s intended to build awareness of the
technical tools available to support learning engineering solutions. This list reflects
a point in time and will need to be updated as new technologies and approaches
emerge.



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-19
312 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Software Design and Development

     Software engineering is valuable for learning solutions at scale. Also, as covered in
     Chapter 4, engineers can create complex systems of systems through modulariza-
     tion (a LEGO-brick approach to software). It’s important to understand which soft-
     ware approaches that will enable—or block—long-term goals like modularization.


     Software designers define how applications will look and function. They might use
     diagrams, like those described by the Unified Modeling Language, to plan and com-
     municate how a system should work. However, although they’re usually experts in
     computer science, software designers don’t build the designs they create. Instead,
     software developers are responsible for turning those designs into reality using
     markup, scripting, and / or programming languages.
            Markup languages, such as hypertext markup language (HTML) and ex-
     tensible markup language (XML), are used to add metadata to a file. For example,
     HTML includes markup that specifies the placement of design elements on a web
     page, and XML is used to define structure (in other words, data about the data like
     naming the author of a web page) in data or documents. Markup languages are both
     human and machine readable. Because markup is used to annotate documents, it’s
     considered presentational rather than algorithmic, so implementing markup isn’t
     technically considered programming.
            Programming languages include rules, commands, and syntax, which are
     used to write machine-readable algorithms that a computer compiles or interprets.
     Most software applications are written using human-readable (albeit complex)
     language that’s then converted into machine-level or binary language (0s and 1s) for
     the computer. Popular programming languages include Java, C, C++ (“C plus plus”),
     and C# (“C sharp”).
            Scripting languages are a subset of programming languages. They’re
     typically used in online applications to send instructions to another program. A key
     characteristic of scripting languages is that they’re interpreted rather than compiled.
     That means a separate machine-readable file isn’t created before run time; instead,
     they’re processed line by line, just-in-time. This gives more flexibility but is often a
     bit slower and less robust. Popular scripting languages include Python, Ruby, PHP,
     SQL, and JavaScript (which sounds like it’s the same as Java but isn’t).1
            There are several different programming paradigms, which refer to the
     frameworks, patterns, and styles of software development. Two common paradigms
                               Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 313



       BUTTON OBJECT
- xsize
- ysize
- label_text                             !"#$%&'()
- interested_listeners
- xposition
                                      FIGURE 14.1. Example of a software object
- yposition
                                      for a button with properties and methods.
+ draw()                              Properties include horizontal and vertical size
+ press()                             (xsize, ysize), label_text, and position (xposition,
+ register_callback()                 yposition). Methods include functions to draw
+ unregister_callback()               the button and to respond to a button press.


are procedural and object-oriented programming. Procedural programming
specifies the sequence of steps to reach a desired state, and for that reason, it’s
sometimes called linear programming. Procedural programs also include procedures
(sometimes called functions or modules) that encapsulate certain steps into repeatable
recipes that are called by the code. In contrast, object-oriented programming uses
software objects that contain both data and coded functions. Basically, each object
is like a miniature program that includes a blueprint, and then you can create as
many instances of that blueprint as you’d like. For example, you might have an
object called “math_problem” that includes all of the typical properties (such as
instructions, problem, answer, and steps) and methods (such as displayProblem,
checkAnswer, and giveHint) of an algebra question. Then, rather than write code for
each math problem, you could just create new questions from your “math_problem”
class. Figure 14.1 demonstrates a basic software object with properties and methods.2
       If you’re not a software developer, then it’s probably not important for you
to understand the different programming paradigms; however, it’s useful to know
that they exist and that they influence design considerations, such as composability
(how different components can be assembled, like LEGO bricks) and extensibility
(the extent that software can be extended in the future, without too much difficulty).


OPEN-SOURCE SOFTWARE

Open-source software is released under a permissive license, so that the copy-
right holder grants others the right to see the uncompiled source code and often
includes rights to use, study, change, and distribute the software. The rights granted
under open-source software licenses vary. Open source doesn’t always mean free
314 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     and unlimited use, so check the license to be sure the rights you need are granted.
            Using good quality open-source software, like the OpenSimon Toolkit
     featured in Chapter 13, can give learning engineering teams a head start on their
     development projects because they can just customize the code rather than starting
     from scratch. Plus, many popular open-source products have been heavily resourced
     or significantly developed over time. For example, in the case of the OpenSimon
     Toolkit, it was developed with millions of dollars of funding and informed by over
     a decade of research.
            In some cases, open-source communities aren’t developing whole software
     packages but are instead creating the building blocks of future learning systems.
     For example, Experience Application Programming Interface (xAPI) standards,
     xAPI compliance test suites, and supporting technologies are offered as open source
     through IEEE Standards Association’s OPEN program and the US Government’s
     Advanced Distributed Learning (ADL) Initiative.3
            Open-source software may seem like a golden ticket, but while it does have a
     lot of benefits, it isn’t technically free. Or rather, as former Sun Microsystems chief
     executive Scott McNealy once quipped, “Open source is free like a puppy is free.” 4
     In other words, it requires care and feeding. In practice, that typically means par-
     ticipating in an established open-source community to help maintain the code, flag
     bugs, or recommend features. For brand-new open-source projects, it could mean
     building that community from the ground up.
            For learning engineering teams, it’s important to understand the opportunities
     and trade-offs of open-source software. At their best, however, healthy open-source
     communities can accelerate innovation and help learning engineering teams develop
     their own solutions more efficiently.


                                    SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURES
   DON’ T CONF USE OPEN
                                    A software architecture describes the pieces of a
    SOURCE WITH OPEN                software system (or system of systems), how each
      ARCH ITECT URE:               behaves, and how they interact. Just like there are
      OPE N SOURCE =                different programming paradigms, there are differ-
       COPYRIGHTS                   ent software architectures. It’s important to choose
                                    an architectural design that will support your desired
   OPE N ARC HIT ECT URE =          “-ilities” (or software quality attributes), such as com-
    MODULAR DESIGN                  posability and extensibility, mentioned above.
                                            Web-based architectures (such as for online
                                Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 315



learning) make use of web standards (such as HTML) and internet-connected
systems (such as web browsers and mobile devices). Modern web-based applications
are also built in modular ways, using data and interface standards that make it easier
to accommodate new content, software, and learning scenarios. Modular designs
that use publicly available software interface standards (versus one-off or proprietary
interface designs) let you add or remove software elements—like LEGO bricks.
These are called open systems architectures or modular open systems approach
(MOSA) architectures.


Modern software best practices recommend using modular, versus monolith, de-
signs. Modular architectures divide each major function into its own small code base
or microservice. In contrast, monolithic architectures tightly intertwine all of the
components, making it more difficult to change, remove, or add features. For learn-
ing engineers, the shift from monolithic to modular design can be seen in the evolu-
tion of learning management systems (LMSs). Functions that used to be built into a
single, monolithic LMS are being separated into plug-and-play software elements.
Modular architectures better support continuous innovation and prevent lock-in to
specific platforms, pedagogies, learning contexts, or vendors.


      Many web-based systems use a REST architectural style. REST stands for
REpresentational State Transfer. REST leverages the hypertext transfer protocol
(HTTP)—the standard communication protocol used by web browsers—to allow
systems to access and manipulate web resources through predefined rules. Learning
engineers might use RESTful web services for data storage and retrieval, translation,
search and discovery, data visualization, and so on. RESTful architectures support
modular web-based architectural designs, as recommended above. Also, the software
examples featured in this book use REST, as do many of the featured instrumentation
standards such as xAPI. (See Chapter 18.)


RESTful architectures leverage the World Wide Web infrastructure to send and
receive messages, including between different microservices or between a learner’s
local device and an app or a cloud-based web service.


      Modern web-based architectural best practices often call for the use of
linked data.5 As its name implies, linked data connects information from one system
316 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     to another using World Wide Web protocols. However, unlike typical hypertext-
     based websites, linked data also includes some specific requirements to make the
     connected data better suited for machine-processing, such as using controlled
     vocabularies expressed in standardized formats. (Controlled vocabularies are sets of
     authorized terms or phrases that limit and define what tags can be used.)
            Consider this example for how you might use linked data. Imagine you’re
     creating a competency-based learning outline for a postsecondary computer
     programming course. Rather than coming up with all of the competencies yourself—
     and then updating them each semester as technology evolves—you could connect to
     a linked open data set, like the US Department of Labor’s O*NET.6 This would allow
     you to reference predetermined tasks, skills, knowledge, and other characteristics
     for computer programmers. The overall framework, and each of the elements in it,
     is referenced using a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). URIs are global identifiers
     that give unique names to everything on the web. Using linked data not only saves
     time; it also facilitates shared understanding. In our example, tagging the different
     modules of your course with the standardized O*NET competencies would make it
     easier to compare or integrate that curriculum with similar learning content.
            JSON-LD is a popular format for encoding linked data on the web. It’s based on
     the widely used JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) format, which is a low-overhead
     alternative to XML. JSON is an open standard for file and data interchange that uses
     human-readable text to store and transmit machine-readable data objects. It supports
     attribute-value pairs, arrays of data objects, and any other data that can be serialized
     into text strings. It’s commonly used to structure data for storage or transfer, for
     instance, between local and cloud components of distributed web applications. The
     LD in JSON-LD stands for Linked Data.7 In the O*NET example, you could have
     used JSON-LD to link the O*NET competencies into the metadata of your course.
     The instrumentation standard xAPI also uses JSON formatting, and xAPI Profiles
     use JSON-LD @context files to specify design patterns for instrumentation and to
     link to controlled vocabularies.


     It’s increasingly common for apps to use JSON-LD to leverage the power of linked
     data, and using linked data is a best practice for modern web-based architectures—
     particularly for data-heavy applications. So, whether or not you’re the “technology
     person” on your team, it’s important to understand these general concepts so you
     can look for opportunities to link or exchange messages externally.
                                Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 317



      Applications that produce a high volume of data continuously over a period
of time have additional architectural considerations. For example, an intelligent
tutoring system that incorporates clickstream monitoring might produce thousands
of messages per second.8 Traditional point-to-point architectures (where each data
packet is sent to each software service that might need to use it) will have too much lag
under the strain of all that streaming data. Instead, a streaming data architecture
is required.9 Streaming data architectures are specifically designed to handle high
volumes and velocities of data from multiple sources, and they allow you to process
those data immediately—which is useful for, let’s say, personalizing a learning
experience in response to observed clickstream behaviors.
      Streaming architectures aren’t the only solution to processing large-scale
learner data in real time (although they are the most robust solution). The more im-
portant point is that the amount of data, analysis / response time frame, and scale of
your learning system all affect architectural considerations. Take these factors into
account early in the design process to avoid issues with lag or scalability later.


DESIGN PATTERNS

Design patterns are established templates that improve the efficiency of software de-
velopment, the quality and maintainability of code, and allow for new team members
to quickly contribute to a project.


As introduced in Chapter 4 and shown in Chapter 9, design patterns are repeatable
solutions to commonly occurring engineering problems. Essentially, they’re
templates of established best practices that improve the efficiency of development
as well as the quality and maintainability of code. Design patterns also promote
shared understanding and a common language among teams. Software engineers
use design patterns so that everyone on a team can work together for consistent
coding approaches based on known architectural patterns.
       Design patterns can also apply to learning engineering. Most obviously, the
technology-focused portions of a design can use software design patterns to aid their
development processes. Beyond that, entire learning engineering teams can develop
design patterns that incorporate concepts from the learning sciences and human-
centered design disciplines in addition to the technological actions. Approaching
learning engineering challenges in this way helps integrate the engineering and
science components into more repeatable and scalable processes.
318 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Data and Data Architectures

     Data-driven decision-making is, by definition, part of learning engineering. Vari-
     ous database architectures and platform types address different kinds of learning
     engineering challenges.


     Data architectures address the structure of data and the capabilities of associated
     data platforms. Data platforms are the various components required to acquire,
     store, prepare, deliver, and manage your data (along with the requisite security).10
     Most data platforms include one or more databases, including relational databases
     (for example, built using SQL) and / or non-relational or NoSQL databases (such as
     object databases, graph databases, document stores, key / value stores, triple / quad
     stores, and hybrid platforms). The different database types each have strengths and
     weaknesses. For instance, SQL needs predefined schema and structured data, while
     NoSQL can handle dynamic schema and unstructured data. The different database
     types also scale and perform differently, depending on how they’re accessed
     and how data are structured within them.11 For example, object databases are a
     convenient choice for applications built using object query languages, and they can
     handle complex relationships between objects. Meanwhile, graph databases excel
     at managing highly connected data and complex queries where the relationships
     between data elements are as important to the data elements themselves.
            Structured data follows predetermined patterns, which make it easily search-
     able. For instance, a spreadsheet that includes students’ names, email addresses, and
     test scores in well-defined columns is an example of structured data. In contrast,
     unstructured data, including most audio and video files, has more variations and
     few (if any) predefined controls. Some types of data fall in between these two con-
     cepts, and those data sets are called semi-structured. For example, if you collected
     all of the emails sent by a class of students over a semester, that set of emails would
     have some internal structure (like to, from, date stamps, and other metadata), but
     most of the emails’ contents wouldn’t adhere to a formal pattern.
            Data lakes are repositories of raw data. They’re places to store all different
     kinds of data—structured, semi-structured, and unstructured—in a single place
     “as is.” They’re specifically designed to store, process, and secure large amounts
     of diverse data.12 The data from a data lake, or from other unstructured or semi-
     structured repositories, will need to be transformed before it can be effectively
     analyzed. This process is typically called data wrangling (or data munging), and it
                                Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 319



includes steps like discovery, structuring, cleaning, and validating the data.13
       Systems often employ multiple methods that transform data into other structures
for different purposes. Raw log data may be collected in a triplestore (a set of three
entities encoded as a subject–predicate–object statement), then those data may be
transformed and cleansed into another triplestore for analytical processing. Then,
facts from those data may be transformed into a normalized operational data store
for learner progress tracking and transformed once again to a denormalized data
warehouse (that is, a database where redundant data, like duplications in different
tables or version histories, are added back in) for enterprise analytics and reporting.
       Learning engineering teams will work with data—and often data at large
scales. These teams will need skilled data engineers to help design the data platforms
and pipelines to collect, transport, store, and transform the data. Teams will likely
also need data scientists who study sophisticated ways to extract meaningful insights
from large data sets, like through modeling and advanced algorithms. Or, instead
of full data scientists, teams with less complex analysis requirements may be able
to work with data analysts, who are typically skilled in data cleaning, statistical
analyses, and visualization tools.


DATA AND SOFTWARE INTEROPERABILITY STANDARDS

Standards are published documents that describe agreed-upon data or software fea-
tures like structure, definition, transmission, tagging, and use.14 By using standards
(versus proprietary or one-off designs), you increase the interoperability of your
data and systems, which will make it easier to scale your learning solutions. Data
and interface standards also support modularization, as discussed in the Software




                                  DATA DICTIONARY
                                      DATA MODEL
                                      PACKAGING
   FIGURE 14.2. Data
   interoperability stack              PROTOCOLS              LINKED
   CC-BY Jim Goodell                                            DATA
                                             API             SERVICES
320 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Architecture section above and in Chapter 4, Learning Engineering is Engineering.
     In other words, using data and interface standards can help learning engineering
     teams build learning ecosystems comprised of diverse learning technologies that
     “plug and play” with each together.
            Interoperability isn’t achieved by a single standard. It takes multiple layers
     of standards—often collected from different standards organizations—to achieve
     interoperability. Figure 14.2 shows layers in the data interoperability stack. At dif-
     ferent levels within this conceptual model you may need standards for the following:
        ► Data Vocabulary or Dictionary: Standards that define the meanings and
           allowed values for data elements.
        ► Data Model: Standards that define entity definitions and entity relationships.
        ► Data Serialization: Standards that identify concrete data structures and
           formats to support transport between systems. For example, a standard might
           define a specific schema or binding using a specific serialization protocol or
           format like JSON-LD.
        ► Data Protocols: Standards by which data may be exchanged. Protocols are
           generalizable procedures (or systems of rules) that enable data exchange.
           Examples include packaging protocols, such as JSON, and communications
           protocols, such as HTTP and REST.
        ► Application Programming Interfaces (APIs): APIs are the software
           interfaces that allow applications to communicate with each other and
           exchange data. Standard APIs are specified using standard protocols (like
           REST), and they identify the specific types of data that may be transmitted,
           what commands are used to send and receive data, and how data transfers are
           confirmed.
        ► Linked Data Services: Linked data takes many forms, such as competency
           framework repositories, distributed identity, and certificate authorities for
           credentials. Correspondingly, linked data services provide access to those
           data; for example, a service might provide a machine-readable competency
           definition or verification of someone’s encrypted distributed identity token.
           These services support interoperability and modular systems.


     DATA STANDARDS ORGANIZATIONS

     Although learning engineers won’t need to become experts in all of the relevant
     standards, learning engineering teams working on technology-heavy or large-scale
 • Caliper: Protocol for transmitting and collecting learning events for later analysis
 • CASE—Competencies & Academic Standards Exchange: Standard for competency frameworks
 • CEDS—Common Education Data Standards: Data dictionary and logical data model for education
 • cmi5—Integration of SCORM-like and xAPI capabilities
 • CLR—Comprehensive Learner Record: For secure and verifiable learning and employment records
 • CTDL—Credential Transparency Description Language: Rich description of credential resources
 • EDI—Electronic Data Interchange: Older transmission standard for student transcript data
 • LRMI—Learning Resource Metadata Initiative: Metadata for learning resources and their relationship
 • HyperText Transfer Protocol: Standard protocol for transmitting content and data on the internet
 • Ed-Fi APIs—Enable data exchange in K-12, such as assessment and demographic information
 • EPUB for Education: A profile of the EPUB standard for digital books targeted at education
 • IMS CC—IMS Common Cartridge: Content packaging standard for course materials
 • IMS CP—IMS Content Packaging: Format for packaging content and associated metadata
 • IMS QTI—IMS Question and Test Interoperability: Content packaging for assessment items and tests
 • JSON—JavaScript Object Notation: A lightweight text-based format for data interchange
 • LOM—Learning Object Metadata: Schema for metadata that describes learning objects and resources
 • LRMI—Learning Resource Metadata Initiative: Metadata for learning resources and their competencies
 • OBI—Open Badge Infrastructure: A format for communicating skills and achievements
 • OneRoster: Protocol and format for secure exchange of class roster information between applications
 • PESC—Postsecondary Electronic Standards Council: O!ers a set of educational standards
 • SCD—Sharable Competency Definition: Standard for competencies and frameworks
 • SCORM—Sharable Content Object Reference Model: A format for publishing digital resources for LMSs
 • SIF Data Model: Data model for representing and transmitting educational information
 • SIF Infrastructure: Protocol for transmitting educational information
 • VC: Verifiable Credentials (W3C)
 • xAPI—Experience API: A protocol for reporting learning activities and student experiences
 • XML—EXtensible Markup Language: Common format for representing digital content and data


FIGURE 14.3. Matrix of learning technology standards, organized by their
data categories and the layers they a!ect in the interoperability stack
322 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     efforts should seek out expert advice to ensure that a full and appropriate set of stan-
     dards is incorporated early in the design process. There are numerous standards or-
     ganizations that work diligently to define and maintain software, data, and all sorts
     of other industry standards. For the technologies most learning engineering teams
     will use, the following standards organizations offer many good choices:

           •   Access for Learning (a4l.org)
           •   Common Education Data Standards (ceds.ed.gov)
           •   Dublin Core Metadata Initiative (dublincore.org)
           •   HR Open Standards Association (hropenstandards.org)
           •   IEEE Learning Technology Standards Committee (sagroups.ieee.org/ltsc)
           •   Internet Engineering Task Force (ietf.org/standards)
           •   IMS Global (imsglobal.org)
           •   MedBiquitous (medbiq.org)
           •   PESC (pesc.org)
           •   World Wide Web Consortium (w3c.org)

     Additionally, there are de facto data standards, created and popularized by widely
     used systems such as from LearnLab.org, DataShop, and the Credential Engine.
     Also, regional initiatives, such as the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure
     (digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/ebsi), are influencing international stan-
     dards. Figure 14.3, derived from edmatrix.org, shows a sample of learning technol-
     ogy standards by interoperability layer and data domain.15 (This matrix was created
     by contributors to EdMatrix and coordinated by Brandt Redd.)


     Cloud Computing

     Cloud computing allows for more rapid and iterative development and deployment.
     Instances of prototypes, apps, or web services can be spun up in days (rather than
     months) on platforms that can be instantly provisioned. Cloud infrastructure is also
     scalable with redundancies that reduce the risk of any single point of failure.


     Cloud computing refers to interconnected servers (hardware / software resourc-
     es) that deliver services over the internet. Servers—whether the traditional kind or
     cloud computing—are the computers on which distributed software applications
     run. (Unless a piece of software runs entirely on an end user’s personal computer,
     then one or more servers need to be used.)
                                Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 323



       Cloud computing facilitates the on-demand availability of computer
resources. So, instead of buying an entire hardware server, which you might only
use for eight hours a day, you can acquire cloud-based resources as you need them.
Cloud environments scale to meet growing or shrinking needs. Commercial cloud
providers (such as Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, or Microsoft Azure) also
offer many tools and features, like built-in analytics engines and cybersecurity. Such
services facilitate more rapid software development and implementation, and they
minimize risk. Cloud computing also better supports streaming data architectures,
which often require scalability and significant computing power.
       Due to its scalability, flexibility, security, and other benefits, cloud computing
is well suited to support technology-based learning. However, software designed for
cloud environments should use different components and architectural patterns than
applications built for legacy client-server (or client-only) architectures. Hence, it’s
important to make decisions about cloud environments early in the design process.
Similarly, for project planning, it’s useful to understand the associated cost model.
Rather than pay for a piece of hardware once, commercial cloud providers often use
usage-based pricing models. These tend to be much less expensive than traditional
(non-cloud) server options, but they grow more costly as demand increases. That
means it’s important to plan ahead for things like the number of concurrent end users
and scale of data being produced, analyzed, and stored.


Internet of Things

IoT is one of the emerging technologies supporting the modern distributed learning
ecosystem. These network-connected devices can serve as sensors that collect rele-
vant data as nodes in a distributed architecture. There’s a growing need for learning
engineers to develop software and systems that utilize IoT systems.


The internet of things (IoT) is a network of physical objects embedded with sensors,
software, and computing technologies for the purpose of connecting and exchanging
data over the internet. For example, instrumented medical tools (such as smart
infusion pumps, electronic medical mannequins, and environmental spatial sensors)
could be used to unobtrusively collect data about medical students’ performance
on a training scenario. (This data could then be streamed to a networked software
analysis application, likely on a cloud server, to be analyzed and acted upon, such as
by delivering real-time feedback.) There are countless potential uses for IoT systems
324 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     for learning engineers—from instrumenting communications devices to inform
     military team training to using them to enhance the experience of a children’s
     museum.16 It’s important for learning engineering team members to realize that
     learning technologies have moved beyond online platforms and client-side software
     apps. Increasingly, just-in-time learning is being facilitated by intelligent things like
     smart speakers with natural language processing and other technological devices
     distributed throughout learners’ environments.


     Human-Computer Interaction

     Good human-computer interaction is a critical requirement for effective learning
     technologies. This broad field affects all technology-enabled learning interactions,
     including their usability, data instrumentation, and personalized learning features.


     Human-computer interaction (HCI) is a transdisciplinary field addressing the
     interaction between humans and information technology.17 These interactions might
     include traditional interfaces, like mouse and keyboard, as well as newer ones, such
     as natural language (“Hey Alexa”), gestures and haptics (touch), and virtual and
     augmented reality (or collectively extended reality). HCI also involves user interface
     design, and it examines how different behaviors, norms, and structures form around
     human-system interactions. For instance, subfields and areas of study include:

           •   Social computing
           •   Affective computing
           •   One-to-one computing where every learner has their own device(s)
           •   Inference modeling (see Chapters 6 and 19)
           •   Neurophysiological sensors, such as electroencephalograms (EEGs) and
               other brain-computer interfaces, eye trackers, and heart-rate monitors

           As the list above implies, HCI also incorporates nontraditional interface
     methods. This is particularly applicable to learning engineering to help inform
     data-informed decisions. Instrumented learning experiences are a primary method
     used to collect data. Instrumentation involves hardware and software sensors
     and data pipelines that support the collection of learner experience data. Hardware
     sensors might include wearables and IoT devices. Software sensors may be built into
     learning platforms or online activities, such as within online simulations or game-
     based learning. These concepts are discussed in detail in Chapters 5 and 13.
                               Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 325



Federated and Distributed Identity

Decentralized identifiers enable verifiable, decentralized digital identity. This can
support self-sovereign learner records that give learners (and parents and guardians
of minor learners) greater levels of access and control over their own data, enabling
life-long learning free from institutional data silos.


All of the data—and diverse data sources—used for a learning engineering solution
create a problem: How do we know which data packages belong to which learners? A
single LMS might assign a unique User ID for each learner, but this quickly becomes
cumbersome when using multiple data streams from different sources. For example,
a single learning experience might easily include activities delivered from an LMS,
a separate simulation-based learning experience, a microlearning mobile application
for reinforcement during downtime, and a third-party testing application—and this
doesn’t even account for the various software and hardware sensors that might be
added.
       Federated and distributed identity methods store a person’s (or organization’s)
electronic identity across multiple distinct identity management systems. A special
type of federated identifier, the decentralized identifier (DID), has been designed
so that it may be decoupled from centralized registries, identity providers, and
certificate authorities. In other words, DIDs enable each person (or other entity) to
have a single, verifiable, machine readable User ID across systems.18
       In addition to supporting multiple apps within a single learning experience,
DIDs also help enable the integration of data across different learning experiences,
for instance, in the form of a learner-owned learning wallet or universal learner
record. Using DIDs helps learning engineering solutions become part of the larger
ecosystem of learning, creating opportunities for learners and fellow learning
engineering teams to build a data-driven network of life-long learning experiences.


Analytics

Learning analytics are central to learning engineering, both for decision-making
about the process as well as for software-based feedback loops.


Analytics, or the systematic computational analysis of data, is central to the
326 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     investigation phase of the learning engineering process introduced in Chapter 1.
     Learning analytics is the use of “data mining, machine learning, natural
     language processing, visualization, and human-computer interaction approaches
     among others to provide educators and learners with insights that might improve
     learning processes and teaching practice.” 19 Learning analytics capabilities may be
     integrated as standalone applications that are used after a learning event, such as in
     the Kaplan worked problems example in Chapter 6. They can also be integrated into
     real-time operations, for instance, built into cloud-based streaming architectures
     or incorporated directly into a software system, such as an adaptive engine in an
     intelligent tutor to provide just-in-time feedback to learners, as described in the
     Generalized Intelligent Framework for Tutoring example in Chapter 5.
            Learning analytics is a broad and growing discipline, and many of its techniques
     are discussed in more detail in Chapters 6 and 18, and the open-access Handbook of
     Learning Analytics provides a scholarly primer.20 For this chapter, it’s most important
     to understand that learning analytics don’t simply “happen.” In addition to making
     decisions about how to instrument, stream, and store learning data, appropriate
     algorithms are needed to process those data. Software considerations—such as the
     source or sources of data, data formats, software architectures, and the use of cloud
     computing—all influence which algorithms can be effectively used.


     Artificial Intelligence

     Some of the most impactful advances in learning technology involve AI and spe-
     cifically machine learning. For example, promising advances in deep knowledge
     tracing are allowing systems to make accurate inferences about learner mastery, and
     machine learning capabilities like natural language processing and computer vision
     are revolutionizing human-computer interfaces for learning.


     AI simulates facets of human intelligence in machines, such as problem solving or
     image recognition.21 Machine learning is one kind of AI. It gets computers to act
     without being explicitly programmed. Instead, it uses data to train an algorithm to
     recognize patterns. “In the past decade, machine learning has given us self-driving
     cars, practical speech recognition, effective web search, and a vastly improved
     understanding of the human genome.” 22 Duolingo uses machine learning and large
     data sets to train its spaced-repetition algorithm that predicts when learners should
     practice a word while language learning.
                                Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 327




                   ADAPTIVE INSTRUCTIONAL ENGINE
  PERCEPTS = SIMULATION
  CONDITIONS
                                ADAPTIVE ENGINE


        Policy                       V(x)                 Policy
     Improvement                                        Evaluation
                                                                         PERCEPTS =
         “actor”                                           “critic”          LEARNER
                                                                        ACTIONS AND
                                     CONTROL POLICY                       CONDITIONS


                     AGENT ACTS ON THE              AGENT ACTS ON
                     SYSTEM (ACTION, E.G.,        LEARNER (ACTION,
                     CHANGE DIFFICULTY)            E.G., FEEDBACK)
  AGENT                                                                        AGENT
  OBSERVES AND                                                           OBSERVE AND
  ASSESSES THE                                                              ASSESSES
  SYSTEM (STATE
                                     INTERFACE                         LEARNER (STATE
  AND REWARD)                                                            AND REWARD)


 CONTENT MODEL                                                  LEARNER MODEL
                                learner acts on the system
 INSTRUCTIONAL                                                        LEARNER
     SYSTEM               learner observes changes or
                          responses from the system




FIGURE 14.4. Model of an adaptive instructional system from the AIS Consortium


ADAPTIVE INSTRUCTIONAL SYSTEMS

Adaptive instructional systems (AISs) use AI and user modeling to determine what
to teach, when to teach it, and which methods of instruction to use. For example,
intelligent tutoring systems are one kind of AIS. 23
       Bob Sottilare, coauthor of Chapter 5, developed the Generalized Intelligent
Framework for Tutoring, and he leads the AIS Consortium.24 Other coauthors
developed AISs and authored the IEEE’s Standard for the Classification of Adaptive
Instructional Systems. That standard classifies components of AISs into four
328 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     categories: learner model, adaptive engine, content model, and interface. Figure
     14.4 shows a generalized model of an AIS with the category labels from the IEEE
     standard.25
           AISs, whether sophisticated intelligent tutors or relatively simple adaptive
     hypertext experiences, are essential tools for learning engineering teams. AISs
     allow learning engineers to scale personalized solutions, leading to significantly
     better outcomes than nonadaptive alternatives. At their best, AISs can replicate (or
     even outperform in a few cases) one-on-one human tutoring, but without the costs
     or physical limitations of that model.26
           Jim Goodell, this book’s lead author, chairs the IEEE AIS Interoperability
     Workgroup, developing standards that will enable more modular and interoperable
     AIS architectures—particularly at scale. Standards for AIS interoperability could
     drive innovation, increase the value of learning engineering teams’ R&D efforts,
     and promote economies of scale fueling more innovation. Jim proposes an approach
     that modularizes not only the system components but also the data processing func-
     tions within and between AISs, such as the learning analytics methods featured in
     Chapter 18. This framework uses plug-in algorithms and linked data concepts as
     part of a stack of standards. An early conceptual model of this plug-in architecture
     is shown in Figure 14.5.


     INTELLIGENCE AUGMENTATION

     Intelligence augmentation is the concept of humans and artificially intelligent
     software agents working together to perform some task. That task could be
     helping the human learn something or otherwise augmenting the human’s skills
     to accomplish tasks more effectively than either the person or AI agent could do
     on their own. It’s likely that more and more of the work that humans do will be
     augmented by intelligent software agents. Time spent learning versus time spent
     doing will increasingly overlap. People will learn while doing, and the lines will
     blur between task automation and learning engineering. There could be a growing
     role for learning engineering to create and optimize learning experiences within the
     context of learning while doing with intelligence augmentation playing the roles of
     teacher, coach, mentor, peer, and assistant.
                                   Software and Technology Standards as Tools | 329
