CHAPTER 15


Tools for Learner
Motivation
by Laura Casey, Diana Delgado, Jim Goodell, and Prasad Ram



This chapter discusses learner motivation, one of the most important—
yet often overlooked—elements to successful learning. A tool later in the
chapter o!ers categories of motivational variables and suggestions for
motivating operations.


Chapter 1 presented the learning engineering process, which starts with understand-
ing the challenge. This includes recognizing that learners and teams are contextually
situated and also that learning challenges often involve motivation. Learner motiva-
tion impacts engagement and may be heavily influenced by the context and culture
of the learning environment. This is highly personal. Each learner is motivated by
different things at different times. In examining motivational variables, it’s useful to
understand how they impact learners differently and in different types of activities.
       Learning requires effort and engagement from learners, and behavioral sci-
ence gives insights into the core drives that motivate productive (or nonproductive)
behaviors. Generally defined, a motivating event is one that momentarily alters the
value of a motivational variable by making it more or less desirable. Consequently,
and by virtue of this value-altering effect, the behavior associated with that re-
inforcer will be directly affected; that is, it will be more or less likely to occur.1
Motivating operations can also affect motivational variables beyond a single
learning activity; for example, a particular motivational strategy may help learners
developing more productive attitudes, long term.


MOTIVATING OPERATIONS are deliberate actions to adjust features of a learn-
ing environment or a learning experience in ways that lead to productive learning
behaviors. These include learner-initiated and externally initiated actions.


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-20
334 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            Motivating operations can be taken by any person or automated system, and
     these operations can apply to any kind of motivating variable. Learning engineering
     teams apply a variety of motivating operations to adjust motivational variables to
     increase the probability learners will engage in learning activities and in productive
     learning behaviors that increase the probability of productive learning.
             Chapter 2 examined the Knowledge-Learning-Instruction Framework (KLI)
     having external instructional events and assessment events that may lead to internal
     learning events. We can add to that model “motivational events.” In the field of
     psychology, motivation has been understood as being either intrinsic (for example,
     natural curiosity) or extrinsic (for example, obtaining a good grade) depending on
     whether the source of motivation is internal or external; however, we purposely avoid
     this distinction. Attempting to classify motivating variables as intrinsic or extrinsic
     may not only lead us to a historically unresolved debate but also seems theoretical
     and pragmatically unnecessary. Instead, we suggest an action-oriented focus.
            Use the table in Figure 15.1 to consider strategies for learner motivation. For
     each motivational category, what variables and corresponding motivational opera-
     tions might be useful? The example given is for the category of “autonomy.” In it,
     we want to activate the motivational variable “choice” with the motivating opera-
     tion of allowing learners to “select the topic of a problem-based learning activity.”
     Multiple motivating operations may be employed within a single category, and you’ll
     probably need several techniques to account for different learners’ personalities and
     circumstances. As you plan your motivating operations, also think about how you’ll
     implement them. For example, when will you deliver them (the timing), and how will
     you verify that they’ve successfully tapped into learners’ core drives?
            Each of the categories in the table is briefly summarized below:
        ► Autonomy: Learners motivated by autonomy value independence or freedom.
           For example, they’re more likely to be engaged when given the ability to
           choose the path, pace, or features of their own learning.
        ► Competence: Learners motivated by competence value what they know and
           can do. They find satisfaction in academic achievements. This intrinsic factor
           might be activated by making learners’ progress more visible to them.
        ► Value: Learners can be motivated by something they perceive as valuable and
           that they’d like to have. It might be social value (belonging, being recognized
           as a significant member of a community, or being appreciated by others for
           who they are). Or value could mean earning tangible rewards that lead to a
           sense of ownership and possession. This can be activated by some forms of
                                                  Tools for Learner Motivation | 335



MOTIVATING OPERATIONS (STRATEGY) WORKSHEET

   CATEGORY                   VARIABLE               MOTIVATING OPERATION
 Autonomy           For example:                  For example:
                    CHOICE                        LET LEARNERS SELECT
                                                  THE TOPIC FOR A
                                                  PROBLEM-BASED
                                                  LEARNING ACTIVITY



 Competence




 Value




 Meaning and
 Purpose




 Avoidance




 Unpredictability




 Scarcity




FIGURE 15.1. Tool for considering motivational approaches
336 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           social learning as well as some of the more stereotypical game mechanics such
           as points and leaderboards.
        ► Meaning / Purpose: Learners are motivated by a sense of meaning—a
           purpose or higher calling. They’re motivated to work hard when the results of
           that work make a difference. They find satisfaction in helping others and being
           recognized as a value to others. This can be activated through project-based
           learning, especially in projects that contribute to a local or global cause.
        ► Avoidance: Learners motivated by avoidance will work hard to prevent losing
           something they’ve earned. For example, Duolingo uses streaks, which are the
           number of days in a row you’ve completed a lesson. Each day that you complete
           a lesson your streak increases. As the streak grows the sense of loss in missing
           a day also grows. This can be a powerful motivator for some learners.
        ► Unpredictability: Learners are motivated by unpredictability that makes
           learning experiences more interesting or gives them unexpected rewards. For
           example, hidden messages, images, or features in electronic media, commonly
           know as Easter eggs, can be embedded in a lesson or activity.
        ► Scarcity: Learners are motivated to have or experience things that not
           everyone can readily possess or experience. The rarer the possession, the
           greater its perceived value. For example, schools with dress codes might
           motivate students by offering a rare “dress down day” when some students get
           to wear sweat pants instead of the formal uniform.


     Motivation Across Levels of Life-Long Learning
     Chapter 4 presented engineering control theory principles, open-loop, and closed-
     loop systems, and how these principles apply to adaptive systems of feedback to
     learners. These same principles apply to motivational feedback. Just as there are
     multiple levels of feedback for instruction (as shown in Figure 15.2), motivational
     feedback (motivating operations) may also occur at these multiple levels.
            Motivating operations can have an immediate effect upon a particular form of
     behavior or can exert an influence over more complex classes of behavior that ex-
     tend over longer periods of time. Providing feedback, enthusiastic praise, or access
     to preferred items or activities are often used as immediate, short-term motivators.
     Over time, these short-term motivators can also increase the likelihood of engaging
     in activities over larger units of time; for example, a student who receives appropri-
     ate praise and descriptive feedback may feel better about going to school, attending
                                                       Tools for Learner Motivation | 337



                                   ER TRAJECT
                            E CARE                OR
                        TUR                          IES
                      FU      EN T C AR E ER T R AJE
                          URR                       CT
                         C         NT IAL  LE          OR
                              EDE             VEL        Y
                            CR
                                     N  LE VEL
                                SSO
                              LE
                                    TIVITY LE
                                 AC            V




                                                   EL
                                                                        LIFE-LONG
                                                                  LEARNING LOOPS

                                                        R
                                               LEARNE



FIGURE 15.2. Motivational operations can occur and
influence multiple time frames of learner motivation



a class, participating in a daily activity, reading a book, or working on a long-term
project. Longer-term motivators include dashboards and maps that let learners visu-
alize their progress toward learning goals, credentials, and career goals. (An exam-
ple of this is show in Figure 15.3 from Gooru Navigator.) Learners are also motivated
when they can see that goals are attainable. They may be motivated by some level of
autonomy over their paths or by a sense of ownership, knowing that their learning
path is customized for them.


Data-Driven Motivating Operations
Chapters 5 and 6 examined how data from instrumented learning activities may
be used to predict when a learner is bored, frustrated, or engaged in nonproductive
behaviors. Similarly, learning analytics may be used to predict which motivating
operations will be most effective for which learners in which contexts.
      Predicting the impact of motivational operations is challenging because
the effect of motivational variables (also known as reinforcers) changes based on
338 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                        FIGURE 15.3. Gooru Navigator provides
                                        GPS-like guidance to learners. This
                                        may motivate some learners who want
                                        to visualize their competence or have a
                                        sense of autonomy over their learning.




     learners’ context. For example, points earned in a game are more likely to maintain
     an individual’s behavior of playing the game, if the desired goal is within reach, such
     as beating a competitor or leveling up. Similarly, the value of obtaining good grades
     may fluctuate over time depending on motivating and demotivating factors and
     may be influenced by personal values. A particular learner who frequently receives
     good grades independently of their effort, may be less motivated to work hard on
     an assignment, while another student may put in a full effort, whether based on an
     intrinsic work ethic or in fear of a decrease in grade point average. Therefore data-
     driven decisions about the application of motivational operations should attempt to
     factor in contextual and learner data. In the same way that knowledge inference
     models use prior learner performance data, motivational variable predictors could
     use data from prior observations of levels of engagement and behaviors that occurred
     with variations of motivational variables. (See Chapter 18 for more details on such
     analyses.)


     LEARNER IDENTITY

     Motivation is one aspect of a learner’s identity. We need to understand a learner
     against a learning space structured as a progression of competencies. This covers all
     aspects of learning—cognitive and behavioral, both covert and overt. Competency
     frameworks need to be extremely thorough, and they should be linked to diverse
                                                       Tools for Learner Motivation | 339




  NETWORK                   COLLABORATORS    LEARNING REGISTRY           LEARNERS

 MALINDA FAIRLEY
 Learner Identity
                                            MINDSETS



         KNOWLEDGE                                               CITIZENSHIP




          MUSIC     DANCE    FRENCH




        BIOGRAPHICAL                                             BEHAVIORS
         United High School
         Maine, USA
         Plays Guitar
         Competitive Dance Team             CONTENT
         International Baccalaureate
         Languages: EN, FR




FIGURE 15.4. A model of learner identity may be captured as data structured as a
progression of competencies covering all aspects of learning—cognitive and behavioral,
both covert and overt

learning activities ranging from off-line tasks such as projects, essays, and proofs
to digital activities such as videos and simulations. Learner mastery by competency
should be detailed across all elements of learning, depth-of-knowledge, and decay
functions (to represent the time to refresh the learner on the competency), and com-
petencies should be mapped to local norms such as state standards and courses.
       A very coarse representation of learner identity has limited use; for example,
3.5 GPA, B+, or 78% doesn’t provide adequate information about learners to support
them. Learner identity must be established for each of the competencies across the
entire learning space (sparse matrix). Learner identity for each competency can
be grouped into dimensions, and each dimension can have many attributes. For
instance, a learning identity may include the dimensions below:

   ► Vitals: This includes biographical data and summary data across all other
      elements to provide a quick understanding of the learner.
340 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



        ► Knowledge: This spans across a variety of subjects and courses during the
           lifetime of the learner. Skills are captured as part of knowledge as a “depth of
           knowledge” level encoding.
        ► Mindsets: Factors such as grit, perseverance, self-confidence, and motivation
           have been discussed. There’s often an emphasis on a growth mindset.
        ► Motivational Operation: This is an event or detail that increases or decreases
           a learner’s motivation to work often based on satisfaction, deprivation, or
           consequence of an action or no action from past learning experiences.
        ► Behavior: This is an overt, measurable response to an antecedent that’s
           maintained over time by the consequence that follows. Measurable aspects of
           overt behavior include the latency in responding or acting to an instructional
           cue or antecedent, time spent engaged in the given task, and percentage of
           correct and incorrect responses on items attempted. Predicting future behavior
           is often best done by an analysis of the antecedent, the modus operandi, and
           the consequences that followed. Specifically, did the consequences result
           in the learner gaining access to his or her desired function (teacher, peer,
           artificial intelligence attention; access to a tangible such as a tablet, game,
           recess, candy, music, or tokens; escape from the task at hand for a brief period
           or longer, such as asking for a break or time away, or a sensory response).
        ► Other dimensions and attributes: These can include higher-order thinking,
           context, and so on.


     Role of Motivation in Gamification
     In recent years more and more education and training contexts are integrating
     game-like activities into instructional routines, 2 and studies in game-based learning
     have shown that it increases learning performance. Game mechanics, or rules of
     interaction, can be introduced into any learning context without making the activity
     a game.
           Some of the reasons learners engage with and enjoy gamification are that it can
     provide feedback on performance, fulfill learners’ needs for recognition, and promote
     goal setting. In a nutshell, putting learning activities into a game format allows for
     the design of an environment that guarantees the success of the learner. However,
     fully realized gamification to improve motivation requires good instrumentation
     (see Chapter 5) and a clear representation of learner identity.
           Because behavior is dynamic and complex, effective motivating factors are
                                                      Tools for Learner Motivation | 341



likely to change over time—both in general and in gamified activities. A learner
may value participation and social recognition one day, while on a different day
they may value listening to others or simply interacting non-socially with learning
materials. Strategy games or games that involve role-playing with others may target
different types of motivations. Consequently, it’s useful for learning engineers to
have a toolkit of gamification operations to draw from. If we consider the learning
modality of an online game, Zachary Morford and his colleagues 3 describe the fol-
lowing as elements of gamification that are likely to increase game-playing behavior
and maintain learners’ engagement:

   ► Gamification may provide learners with the option to choose between learning
      modalities. By doing so, learners acquire control over the actions needed to
      access consequences and outcomes and to achieve goals aligned with values.
   ► Game environments may allow for immediate contact with the consequences
      of a learner’s behavior, in environments where those consequences wouldn’t
      be easily accessible.
   ► High variation in learning environments and materials decreases habituation
      (that is, decreased responding due to repetitive or prolonged exposure to a
      source of stimulation). For example, while a long lecture given in a monotone
      voice is likely to decrease students’ attention, the elements of novelty, variation,
      interaction, or even physical activity (as often involved in game-playing) are
      likely to sustain longer periods of their engagement.
   ► When rewards or other positive outcomes are arranged to be delivered in
      ways that are unpredictable to the learner, a more stable and constant pattern
      of game-playing is typically observed versus when all outcomes follow a
      predictable pattern. For example, it’s known that when response-dependent
      rewards are scheduled to be delivered after a variable time interval (not known
      to the learner), responses associated with that reward are likely to show a more
      persistent pattern of responding over time.4
   ► Contacting a variety of highly valued reinforcers while playing a game
      increases the overall likelihood of game-playing behavior and thus exposure
      to and contact with the learning materials.
   ► Because coercive strategies are absent from the learning experience, learning
      becomes a pleasant experience rather than an aversive one. While traditional
      approaches to education often involve doing things to avoid a negative
      consequence (studying to avoid a bad grade or doing homework to avoid a
342 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           reprimand),5 games are self-initiated and maintained by both immediate
           reinforcers and by the long-term natural reinforcers of the learning process
           (which are value-oriented). In gamification, letter grades associated with
           passing or failing can be replaced by words that are indicative of continuing
           progress such as novice, apprentice, and expert.6 Gamification blurs the stark
           work / play distinction that characterizes the former as a non-desirable activity
           that the learner seeks to avoid or escape, and the latter, as a highly desirable
           activity that the learner seeks out. Even low probability actions that a learner
           may not spontaneously initiate in real life (such as leading social interactions
           with peers or writing a descriptive narrative about a topic) may be more likely
           in a role-play situation or as an element of a game.

            Another motivational aspect that accounts for the increased engagement of
     learners who are exposed to gamification is that games afford the possibility to
     apply (and evaluate) integrated skills in relevant contexts. Instructional activities
     designed to resemble a game may include objectives that integrate skills from
     different subject matters. Solving a problem may entail, for example, interacting
     in a simulated situation that requires math, science, and art skills. Also, a gamified
     approach that includes situations that resemble real-world challenges may facilitate
     the generalization of the acquired skills to different contexts and settings. By making
     the learning experience useful and relevant, such games aren’t only continuously
     motivating but can also be ecologically valid. Finally, by accessing learners’
     progress in the relevant skills and objectives, gamified experiences can positively
     impact learners’ motivation. Viewing user-friendly visual representations of their
     performance is often an effective reinforcer and an immediate indicator of learners’
     success.


     Big ‘M’ and Small ‘m’ Motivators
     As discussed earlier, learners have short-term (getting through an assignment or
     activity) and long-term (completing a course or credential) motivations. It’s erroneous
     to assume all learners are driven to do well for the sake of finding a purpose or
     meaning in life. While some may be in search of learning for sake of learning,
     others are seeking a diploma, a trade, college admission, or gainful employment.
     Understanding and identifying the kinds of things that motivate people is a first step
     in identifying what motivating operations might be employed.
                                                   Tools for Learner Motivation | 343



CATEGORIES OF HUMAN MOTIVATION

Across business and academic domains there are multiple perspectives on how to
classify human motivation. (The motivational categories provided earlier in this
chapter and shown in Figure 15.1 represent just one approach to the categorization of
motivation.) Daniel Pink discusses the puzzle of motivation and distills it to auton-
omy, mastery, and purpose.7 Daisy Yuhas in a Scientific American article identified
three critical elements for sustained motivation: 8
      • Autonomy: You gain motivation when you feel in charge
      • Value: You stay motivated when you’re true to your beliefs / values
      • Competence: Through work and growth you gain a sense of competence
Patrick C. Friman identified six sources of motivational reinforcement: 9
      •   Personal growth: Achievements show progress; increase self-confidence
      •   Giving: Naturally reinforcing effect of contributing to others’ well-being
      •   Belonging: Acquiring a sense of connection with others or belonging
      •   Significance: Acquiring a sense of being valued and recognized by others
      •   Certainty: Having control that something will happen reliably
      •   Uncertainty: Excitement of something yet to happen, an unknown outcome
Finally, Yu-kai Chou’s Octalysis framework lists eight gamification core drives: 10
      •   Empowerment
      •   Accomplishment
      •   Ownership
      •   Social Value
      •   Meaning
      •   Unpredictability
      •   Scarcity
      •   Avoidance
       Five of Chou’s categories are what he calls “white hat gamification,” for
example, motivators about conditions people desire, and three of Chou’s categories
are what he calls “black hat gamification,” for example, categories of conditions
that people tend to avoid. It isn’t necessarily bad to use these negative motivators.
For example, an award is considered valuable because of scarcity; an award that’s
plentiful, that everyone receives, may not be as much of a motivator. Another
example is unpredictability; a learning game with Easter eggs that learners can
discover may motivate them to play longer. The following table provides a crosswalk
of the categories from these four different sources. (See Figure 15.5.)
344 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            Even though behavioral scientists and authors have different ways of
     classifying motivational factors, there are overlaps in these frameworks. For
     example, the definitions of significance, meaning, and purpose are separate labels
     for similar concepts in three of the frameworks. It’s not so important which labels are
     chosen. Rather, it’s critical for learning engineering teams to consider the full range
     of relevant variables and to develop strategies that address them with motivating
     operations within a learning engineering solution.


     Human Motivator Categories
      Friman 11             Chou 12              Pink 13               Yuhas 14
      Positive Motivators

      Certainty             Empowerment          Autonomy              Autonomy

      Personal Growth       Accomplishment       Mastery               Competence

                            Ownership                                  Value

      Giving
                            Social
      Belonging

      Significance          Meaning              Purpose

      Negative Motivators

                            Avoidance

      Uncertainty           Unpredictability

                            Scarcity

     FIGURE 15.5. Crosswalk of four human motivator frameworks
                                                            Tools for Learner Motivation | 345
