1   Michael, Jack and Caio Miguel. “Motivating Operations.” In Applied Behavior Analysis, edited
    by John O. Cooper, Timothy E. Heron, William L. Heward, 374–391. 2nd ed. NJ: Merrill Prentice
    Hall, 2007.
2   Corbett, Sara. “Learning by Playing: Video Games in the Classroom.” The New York Times
    Magazine, September 19, 2010. www.nytimes.com/2010/09/19/magazine/19video-t.html
3   Morford, Zachary H., Benjamin N. Witts, Kenneth J. Killingsworth & Mark P. Alavosius.
    “Gamification: The Intersection Between Behavior Analysis and Game Design Technologies.” The
    Behavior Analyst 37, no. 1 (2014): 25–40. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40614-014-0006-1
4   Cooper, John O., Timothy E. Heron, and William L. Heward. Applied Behavior Analysis. 3rd ed.
    Hoboken, NJ: Pearson Education, 2019.
5   Michael, Jack. “A Behavioral Perspective on College Teaching.” The Behavior Analyst 14, no. 2
    (1991): 229–239. https://doi.org/10.1007/BF03392578
6   Corbett, “Learning by Playing.”
7   Pink, Dan. “The Puzzle of Motivation.” TEDGlobal 2009. Video, 18:23. July 2009.
    www.ted.com/talks/dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation
8   Yuhas, Daisy. “Three Critical Elements Sustain Motivation: When It Comes To Cultivating
    Genius, Talent Matters, But Motivation May Matter More.” Scientific American, November 1,
    2012. www.scientificamerican.com/article/three-critical-elements-sustain-motivation
9   Friman, Patrick. 2020. “Metaphysical Sources of Reinforcement.” Presentation at the Behavior
    Analysis Association of Mississippi Convention, Mississippi, November 2–5, 2020.
10 Chou, Yu-kai. Actionable Gamification: Beyond Points, Badges and Leaderboards. Createspace
   Independent Publishing Platform, 2015.
11 Friman, “Metaphysical Sources of Reinforcement.”
12 Chou, Actionable Gamification.
13 Pink, “The Puzzle of Motivation.”
14 Yuhas, “Three Critical Elements Sustain Motivation.”
CHAPTER 16


Implementation Tools
by Jodi Lis, Jessie Chuang, and Jordan Richard Schoenherr



This chapter further unpacks the implementation phase of the learning
engineering process presented in Chapter 1. This chapter includes a
checklist for implementation of a learning engineering solution.


Learning engineering doesn’t end with the completion of a product, process,
or pedagogy. Once developed, the creation must be implemented within specific
environmental contexts. Prior to releasing the engineered solution “into the wild,”
the learning engineering team must consider the specific deployment strategies
along with how data will be collected and used to inform iterative improvement
of the solution. Implementation issues are considered as implementation planning
during the creation stage of the learning engineering process and then during
implementation stage other issues that couldn’t have been anticipated at the time of
design need to be addressed.
        This chapter includes an implementation framework (in the form of a check-
list) that outlines considerations for learning engineering teams when planning and
managing (or monitoring) the implementation phase. Ultimately, the effectiveness
of any learning engineering solution is dependent on the people and organization in
which it’s deployed. It’s up to learning engineers to ensure that all reasonable steps
have been taken during the creation phase to ensure that a solution can be adapted to
all relevant learning contexts. This often means the development of implementation
resources, an implementation plan, and / or an implementation guide. The nature of
these resources depend on the project and factors such as the complexity of the work
needed to successfully implement the innovation.
        The following tool contains a summary of the domains that should be
considered for implementation and a list of key questions for consideration. We also
provide some illustrative examples to demonstrate the kinds of considerations that
can be relevant in the context of learning engineering.



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-21
348 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                 Implementation Considerations Framework
        DOMAINS                                            QUESTIONS
   1. Policies and         • What are the relevant policies in the context of the implementation?
   Strategy                • What are the relevant strategies in the context of the implementation?

                           • Is adequate funding available for implementation?
   2. Budget               • If full funding isn’t available now, should it be rolled out over
                             time, starting with a more limited scope or population?

   3. Material
                           • Are adequate resources in place for implementation?
   Resources

                           • How will the leadership support the roll-out,
   4. Leadership and         implementation and evaluation of the product?
   Organizational
   Capacity                • Does the organization have the human resource
                             talent needed to support the implementation?

                           • What roles and expertise are needed to manage, support, monitor,
                             and implement the solution (such as, administrative support,
   5. Team                   program coordination, technology, data management)?
                           See Chapter 10, Tools for Teaming

                           • What technologies are required to respond to the challenge?
   6. Technology
                           See Chapter 14, Software and Technology Standards as Tools

                           Deployment and Delivery
                           • What’s the process to integrate the solution into the existing culture?
                           • What’s the process to integrate it into the existing environment?
                           • What is the process to deliver the product to the users?
                           Commitment and Support
                           • What’s the strategy to demonstrate the product is beneficial / useful?
                           • What’s the process to manage changes in process and
                             behavior needed for e!ective implementation?
   7. Operationalization
                           User or Learner
                           • What preparation is needed for users to engage with the solution?
                           • What support is provided to the user?
                           Professional Development and Mentorship
                           • Is professional development needed for those supporting learners?
                           • If professional development is needed, how will that be provided?
                           • Will support be provided within the organization?
                           • Are external parties required?
                                                                Implementation Tools | 349




              Implementation Considerations Framework
     DOMAINS                                       QUESTIONS
                     • What is the process to identify, collect, monitor,
                       review, and document data (stakeholder perceptions,
                       learning, usage patterns) during implementation?
8. Instrumentation   • Is the instrumentation developed during the Creation
                       phase to collect data fully deployed and operational?
                     See Chapter 5, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation

                     • What qualitative and quantitative variables will
                       be monitored during implementation?
                     • How will the data be analyzed, interpreted, and represented?
9. Investigation     • To what extent do they represent industry standards or best practices?
During
                     • How might these analyses inform future Challenge
Implementation
                       phases and iterative improvements and cost-benefit
                       analysis regarding the future of the product?
                     See Chapter 6, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics

                     • Does the implementation process comply with organizational,
                       regional, national and international standards, for example,
                       data privacy, consent, anonymity, and confidentiality?
                     • Have ethical considerations beyond compliance been considered?
10. Ethics           • What ethical dimensions have been evaluated?
                     • How have trade-o!s and conflicts of interests been addressed?
                     See Chapter 7, Learning Engineering is Ethical
                     See Chapter 17, Ethical Decision Tools

                     • What is the current scale of implementation?
                     • What plans or strategies are established for scaling up? What are the
11. Scale-up and
                       associated costs (human, financial, social capital) of scaling up?
Sustainability
                     • What foreseeable problems are likely to occur? What are the
                       strategies in place for risk assessment and management?

FIGURE 16.1. Learning engineering implementation checklist
350 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Implementation Considerations Framework Examples
     1. POLICIES AND STRATEGY

     What are the policies / strategies to support the implementation?


     A solution should support and comply with local or organizational policies and
     strategies. It could be to achieve an objective in the strategy, work toward an
     overarching goal, or extend the strategy beyond its initial purpose. In the example
     described below about e-learning in Zambia, e-learning is a pillar in Ministry
     of Health’s eHealth strategy. The e-learning initiative worked toward achieving
     the goal of that pillar. In Chapter 5, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1):
     Instrumentation, sensors were used to encode communication and determine
     how well a team followed communications protocols. Precise communication was
     essential for the safety and work of the team. The investigation involved complying
     and aligning with policies and strategies such as the US Army’s communication
     strategies, security and privacy policies, and technology procedures.


     2. BUDGET

     Is adequate funding available for implementation for sustained use? Budgeting will
     include human resources, technical resources and other materials, training, imple-
     mentation, monitoring, and scale-up.


     Any solution must be budgeted adequately. Consideration must be given to how
     much funding is needed, where the funding will come from, and if the funding
     will be stable and available. Also, if you plan to scale up a solution, then future cost
     must be considered. There are many instances in which the funding is available to
     develop, test, and pilot a solution but the scale-up and expansion of the solution isn’t
     thought about until it’s too late. Budgeting for the creation stage is only part of put-
     ting a solution into practice. It’s important to consider what funding may be required
     and if the source of funding will be stable past the research or pilot phase to scale the
     solution and add more users.
            The objective of Open edX, the open-source software platform described in
     Chapter 4, Learning Engineering is Engineering, was to build a learning platform
     using a broad range of robust, effective learning techniques. From its inception the
                                                            Implementation Tools | 351



plan was for the platform to scale to support thousands of courses and reach millions
of students. Attention was paid to making course creation effective yet economical
with low investment.
      It’s been estimated that the cost of content and platform development of the
original Open edX platform and first course were roughly a half-million dollars.
Beyond the cost to develop and deploy the platform, the team also calculated the
return-on-investment to scale Open edX: $1 in per-student cost or benefit equals
roughly $1000 per course when assuming an average of 1000 students per course,
and $1 in per-course cost or benefit equals roughly $1000 per platform. The financial
plan worked. Open edX became the learning technology behind edX, the online
learning platform co-founded by Harvard and MIT, and Open edX has since evolved
to support higher education, enterprise, and government organizations.


3. MATERIAL RESOURCES

Are resources in place for the product for implementation?


Material resources, consumables, and other accessories may be required for the
solution. These are in addition to the technology and human resources. In Chapter 1,
Learning Engineering is a Process, virtual reality equipment for a class at MIT was
required for the solution. Were there enough headsets for the amount of students in
the classroom? Budgeting and procuring them was essential. Without the headsets,
there would’ve been no solution.
       A simulation environment in a medical school in Chapter 3, Learning
Engineering is Human-Centered, and Chapter 7, Learning Engineering is Ethical,
allowed medical students, student nurses, and others to learn surgical and clinical
skills by practicing them without risking the safety of live patients. The lab required
a set-up with a vast range of material resources. In the operating room simulations,
learners performed the roles of nurse, anesthetist, and other clinicians on a simula-
tion mannequin placed on a gurney in the room with vital signs displayed on moni-
tors along with the rhythmic beeps of the simulated patient’s pulse. Technicians and
educators sat behind a one-way mirror voicing the mannequin and running through
a crisis situation by changing the simulated patient’s vital signs. In multipurpose
training spaces, students moved around in groups of five or six to different simu-
lation stations learning skills on tools and resources ultrasound and laparoscopic
trainers, intubation, whole or parts of cadavers, and simulated tissue. The point is:
352 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     When planning the intervention, ensuring that material resources and consumables
     are available and working properly are essential to the solution.


     4. LEADERSHIP AND ORGANIZATIONAL CAPACITY

     How will the leadership support the roll-out and implementation of the product?


     With any new approach or solution, leadership is needed to support, implement, and
     encourage new, different, innovative, and nontraditional methods. Change manage-
     ment is a key ingredient in learning engineering.
            An example of how leadership enables research, testing, and change is offered
     in Chapter 5, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation. As chief
     learning officer at Kaplan, Inc., Bror Saxberg and his team had questions on what
     was happening around the learning that the current science couldn’t answer. The re-
     search lacked evidence about what worked for their specific student population and
     within the context of their delivery model. To test the impact of their interventions
     on student learning and implement the best approaches at scale, the chief learning
     officer, the vice president of measurement and evaluation, and other members on
     the learning team created a research pipeline for their own experiments to accumu-
     late credible evidence about what measurably improved learning outcomes for their
     students. In this example, the leadership made the human resources available and
     developed the organizational capacity for the research pipeline. Without the support
     and resources provided by the leadership, they couldn’t have been successful.


     5. TEAM (HUMAN) RESOURCES

     Who’s on the team to manage, support, monitor, and implement the product (pro-
     gram coordination, IT, data management, and other needs)? What are their roles?


     Learning engineering can be a team sport, as described in Chapter 10, Tools for
     Teaming. The team that produced the game in Ghana from Chapter 3, Learning
     Engineering is Human-Centered, had many players. (See Figure 16.2 for a summary
     of stakeholders by phase.) Leti Arts, the gaming company, was involved throughout
     the learning engineering process. The roles on the team included a project manager,
     a creative director, game programmers, developers, illustrators, and sound experts.
                                                           Implementation Tools | 353



The international development organization that contracted Leti Arts had a project
manager and technical experts to create, implement and investigate the solution.
Government officials from the Ghana Ministry of Health and Ghana Health Services
along with representatives of health organizations and medical associations were
involved in the creation and implementation phases.




                                                                                     INSTRUMENTATION
                                                                    IMPLEMENTATION




                                                                                                       INVESTIGATION
                                                         CREATION
Leti Arts team                                             x            x                x                x
International organization managing the project            x            x                x                x
Government, Ministry of Health, Ghana Health Services      x            x                x
Local health organizations                                 x
Medical profession associations                            x            x
Peer educators from the community                          x            x
MSM population                                             x            x                x

FIGURE 16.2. Leti Arts example showing di!erent teams during di!erent phases

       Peer educators from the community implemented the game. They learned how
to integrate the game into the training and assistance that they provided to the com-
munity. They supported and introduced the game to the target population. For the
investigation, instrumentation was built into the scenarios. The Leti Arts team gath-
ered the data and prepared reports for the project team. The project team reviewed
the decisions made in the scenarios with the peer educators and noted the areas in
which further reinforcement would be helpful. That information from the investiga-
tion was also incorporated into other outreach strategies.


6. TECHNOLOGY AND INFRASTRUCTURE

What tools and processes are needed?


Wikipedia defines technology as “the sum of techniques, skills, methods, and
354 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     processes used in the production of goods or services.” The case studies through-
     out this book all include a form of technology, from a donkey cart in West Africa
     described in Chapter 1, Learning Engineering is a Process, to complex software
     such as adaptive learning systems used by the US Army in Chapter 5, Learning
     Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation, to designing learning for two-
     and three-year-old children in Chapter 3, Learning Engineering is Human-Centered.
     The technology may be a single solution or may include an interoperable solution
     of several platforms and applications. Implementation focuses on how to build and
     operationalize these technologies and other components needed for the solution.


     7. OPERATIONALIZATION

     What is the process for the solution to be integrated into the existing culture and
     learning environment?


     The answer to this question requires its own learning engineering processes and
     sub-process cycles. Every implementation context may be different, have unique
     challenges, and require different approaches, plans, and solutions to problems.

     See example: Zambia case study below.


     8. INSTRUMENTATION

     What’s the process to identify, collect, monitor, review, and document data (stake-
     holder perceptions, learning, usage patterns) during implementation?


     The early phases of the learning engineering process (understanding the challenge
     and the creation phase) include determining what questions to answer and develop-
     ing a system to gather that data, for example, the instrumentation. A focus of the im-
     plementation phase is to ensure that the data sources are accessible and the systems
     are working to collect and review the data.
            A scenario in Chapter 6, Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics,
     illustrates how a team at Edmentum reviews and interprets analytic results from
     a formative assessment. Edmentum has learning engineers in their company who
     are well-versed in learning sciences, data sciences, and psychometrics, but most
     importantly, can drive the overall learning engineering process. The process to
                                                           Implementation Tools | 355



collect, monitor, and review data is ongoing as products and content are implemented
in the field. As data are collected and insights gained, new cycles of the learning
engineering are initiated. These processes include various team members, who ask
the following kinds of questions:

   ► Learning engineer asks what are the potential causes of the result and what
      additional analyses should be done and actions should be taken.
   ► Learning designer asks if there’s evidence that instruction could lead to
      confusion, is chunked properly, or has distracting elements; and they may
      recommend other pedagogical methods.
   ► Content and assessment developers ask if the content and assessment are
      aligned so that the construct taught is the construct measured.
   ► Psychometrician asks if the passing score is set properly to distinguish
      between learners who have mastered the content and those who haven’t and
      if there’s evidence of multiple correct responses or if students are disengaged.
   ► Implementation consultant asks if there’s evidence that teachers may be
      implementing the system in an unintended way or if there’s anything within
      the context or setting that’s unusual.
   ► Software engineer asks if there’s an error in the calculation of a pass.
   ► User experience or user interface designer asks if there’s evidence of
      construct-irrelevant variance or additional ways to engage learners.


9. INVESTIGATION DURING IMPLEMENTATION

Instrumentation developed during the creation phase will collect data during the
implementation phase. The team must monitor data collection to ensure everything is
working. The team may monitor metrics and do analysis during the implementation
phase to inform mid-implementation course corrections, define challenges for
iterative improvements, and inform future decisions about the product. These metrics
will differ depending on the product, implementation context, and organization.
       What do we want to know? What question do we want to answer about the
impact? Based on the question, what data do we need? What are the qualitative and
quantitative variables? Are we collecting the data we need to answer key questions?
Sometimes questions are answered with predefined metrics and methods. Other
times, important features and new questions are discovered through data mining.
       Based on interviews with several private sector companies, Jessie Chuang,
356 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     vice-chair of IEEE’s Industry Connections / Industry Consortium on Learning En-
     gineering (ICICLE) and chair of Corporate Special Interest Group, identified the
     following metrics that companies often care about monitoring during the implemen-
     tation of solutions:
           •   Engagement
           •   Empowerment of employees
           •   Transformation
           •   Employee well-being
           •   Experience and satisfaction
           •   Diversity and inclusivity
           •   Personalized learning
           In the Introduction Chapter, What is Learning Engineering, we described how
     Duolingo looks at metrics. For every design decision the Duolingo team makes, they
     consider multiple metrics of success. Those metrics include measures of learning,
     measures of user engagement, and how user interactions impact the company’s
     bottom line. They know that to continue to provide free language learning to millions
     of people around the world they need to stay in business. This means that every
     decision is a balance between these metrics. They run controlled experiments for
     almost everything that they change. If they can improve one metric without hurting
     the others, they do it.


     10. ETHICS

     Consequences of the solution may arise that couldn’t have been known or predicted
     in the creation of the solution and instrumentation. It’ll be important during imple-
     mentation to periodically evaluate how the solution is being implemented and the
     data being collected.
           • Do the development and implementation processes comply with
             organizational, regional, national, and international standards, for
             example, data privacy, consent, anonymity, and confidentiality?
           • Have ethical considerations beyond compliance been considered?
           • What ethical dimensions have been evaluated?
           • How have trade-offs and conflicts of interests been addressed?
           Chapter 7, Learning Engineering is Ethical, describes several approaches to
     ethical sense-making and provides examples of ethical frameworks. Also, the ethics
     decision tool in Chapter 17, Ethical Decision-Making Tools, is a matrix for ethical
                                                             Implementation Tools | 357



design. The matrix has domains to consider and review when designing and imple-
menting the solution. As the solution is being implemented it’s important to remain
vigilant about how the processes are developed, how the solution is being used, and
how data are collected, tracked, and applied.


11. SCALE-UP AND SUSTAINABILITY

What’s the plan for the desired scale of implementation?


The intervention may be intended only for a small audience of learners for research,
or it could be piloted with the intent of scaling to support people globally. If the plan
is to scale the solution, a scaling strategy should be thought about at the inception and
established throughout the implementation process. Strategies must be implemented
for risk assessment and management. Open edX was planned from the inception
to scale to thousands of courses and millions of users. Duolingo continues to scale
to more users. The solution in Zambia, illustrated in the following case study, was
designed to scale across the country.



Case Study
HEALTH WORKER E-LEARNING TO SCALE IN ZAMBIA

A viable, trained and experienced health
workforce is essential to provide quali-
ty service delivery. In Zambia, a project
funded by the US Center for Disease Con-
trol and Prevention (CDC) invested in the
education of health workers to improve
their competence in clinical areas from
2016–2020. The project used a blended
learning approach including e-learning
and on-site mentorship to train the health
providers. An international health organi-
zation supported the Ministry of Health to
implement the project.
358 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                        IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS
                        E-learning is a pillar in the Ministry of Health’s (MOH) National
                        eHealth Strategy. It was identified as an approach for continuous
                        personal development to improve health worker’s competence. In
                        2016 when the project began e-learning wasn’t commonly used as a
    1. Policies and     training method. The MOH didn’t have a learning management system
    Strategy            (LMS), and there was no office in the MOH that managed e-learning.
                        The project supported the strategy by deploying a learning platform,
                        developing courses, incorporating e-learning into their continuous
                        professional development and building capacity in the ministry to use
                        digital learning tools.
                        The intervention was a component in an HIV project funded
                        by the US CDC from 2016 to 2020. An international health
                        organization was contracted to manage it. CDC is continuing
    2. Budget
                        to support the hosting and other components after the
                        completion of that project. As e-learning was a new activity for
                        the MOH budget, they’re identifying mechanisms to fund it.
                        Many health workers didn’t have mobile devices with the
                        specifications needed for the LMS mobile application. The
                        project procured tablets and preloaded them with the learning
    3. Material
                        app and content. A process was developed for distribution to the
    Resources
                        health workers. Technical support was provided by the project
                        and MOH IT sta!. The tablets were returned to the project after
                        completion for redistribution to the next cohort of learners.
                        Leadership consisted of MOH officials, project sta!, and sta! from
    4. Leadership and
                        CDC, the donor who provided the funding for the intervention. The
    Organizational
                        project collaborated with the Ministry throughout the process to build
    Capacity
                        capacity within the government to transition the activity to them.
                        Implementing e-learning as a new tool in capacity building
                        involved a learning engineering team. The team consisted of
                        project sta! who orchestrated and managed every stage of the
                        learning engineering process, MOH officials at the national and
                        district levels, health associations, IT experts at the MOH, subject
                        matter experts, health training institutes, and a monitoring team.
    5. Human
                        The project contracted out the development of the courses and
    Resources
                        deployment of the LMS. That team included the instructional
    and Team
                        designer, project manager, technical sta! for the learning platform
                        and experts for graphics, multimedia, e-learning authoring and
                        voicing. At the end of the project, the management of activities
                        and administration of the learning platform was transitioned to a
                        university teaching hospital. They are managing and implementing
                        telementoring and other digital learning activities for health workers.
                        The project procured tablets for the e-learning. The courses and
                        additional reference materials were loaded on tablets using an
    6. Technology       o!-line mobile solution. An o!-line solution was essential because
                        many facilities had no or limited internet connectivity. Courses
                        were downloaded onto the tablet and could be taken o!-line.
                                                                   Implementation Tools | 359




                        IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS
                        Commitment and Support: The project worked the IT department,
                        the health workforce department, and clinical areas at national,
                        regional, district, and community levels of the MOH. Through
                        workshops, site visits, and meetings the Ministry’s capacity and
                        understanding of e-learning and blended learning grew. The Ministry
                        came to support and promote e-learning. The leadership at the
                        Ministry sent official announcements and letters to all health workers
                        in the country to take the courses for continuous professional
                        development and licensure. Health workers were excited to have the
                        opportunity. They liked the engagement and found the courses useful
                        and applicable to providing services. E-learning has become an
                        integral point of professional learning at the MOH.

                        Integration: A blended learning approach was used, consisting
                        of e-learning, on-site mentorship, and a WhatsApp community of
                        practice. Interactive, case-based courses in HIV, TB, and maternal
                        and newborn health were developed. They complemented clinical
                        skills practice during on-site mentorship, which learners continued to
                        receive. WhatsApp was used to communicate, share information, and
                        ask questions. Expert clinicians provided guidance and advice via
                        WhatsApp. Through these activities, the health providers gained new
                        skills and improved the quality of services delivered.
7. Operationalization
                        Professional Development: Selected experienced providers from
                        district training teams served as mentors. Workshops were held
                        to introduce e-learning first to the mentors. Using technology for
                        learning was new to them. They learned how to incorporate it into
                        their mentoring processes and use it to enhance their skills. The
                        mentors also learned how to use the learning application and tablets.

                        Learners Use and Support: After the mentors in the designated
                        cohort learned the blended learning approach and use of the learning
                        application, they trained health workers whom they mentored how to
                        access the e-learning on the tablets that were distributed to health
                        providers. The health providers took the courses on their own time
                        over two months. They also referred to the e-learning and other
                        documentation on the tablet for reference when managing patients.
                        The learners continued to receive on-site mentoring and received
                        continuous development points when they completed the courses.

                        Delivery: The project worked in three regions of the country. Regions
                        had several districts. Implementation started with one cohort of thirty
                        health workers on one district, then increased to two-hundred health
                        workers in three districts in 2017. The MOH rolled out the courses
                        nationwide in 2019, and by 2021 over ten-thousand health workers
                        registered for the courses.
360 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                         IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS
                         The objective of the intervention was to improve the competence
                         of health workers in clinical areas. The learning was tracked and
                         monitored using data from the learning platform, mentoring checklists
                         that the mentors and health workers used during site visits at the
    8. Instrumentation
                         facilities, observation examinations on clinical skills, and facility data
                         to track the performance improvement in delivery of services for HIV
                         and tuberculous. Noting topics within the clinical areas that weren’t
                         improving, adjustments were made within the training and mentoring.
                         Data collected during implementation suggested that e-learning
                         improved service delivery in two ways. Training previously involved
                         sending workers on workshops o!-site, which took the providers
                         away from their clinics. The prior approach was also expensive
                         and ine!ective, as workers retained very little of the information
                         delivered. The design, approach, and delivery of the e-learning
                         improved learning experiences with health workers gaining
                         and retaining new knowledge and skills. The blended learning
    9. Investigation     increased the number of HIV and TB sites in districts that could
    During               provide clinical care to areas that previously didn’t have it.
    Implementation       In keeping with the iterative learning engineering process,
                         the team acknowledged both successes and remaining
                         challenges. The challenges: health worker shortages remain
                         common, and education and training of health workers is
                         under-invested. As a result, the country began exploring and
                         expanding other approaches such as telementoring for training
                         and telemedicine to improve access and quality of care. The
                         iterative learning engineering process can be applied to the
                         creation and implementation of these other approaches.
                         Data collected on building capacity among health workers
    10. Ethics
                         followed the considerations and policies in place by the MOH.
                         From the onset, this five-year project was funded for its full five
                         years, with a plan to transition the activity to the government
                         upon completion. The project worked in conjunction with
                         MOH officials at the national and district levels, national
                         health worker councils and associations, IT experts at the
                         MOH, subject-matter experts, health training institutes, and
    11. Scale-up and     other stakeholders throughout the period of intervention.
    Sustainability       In the last year of the project, a series of meetings was held to
                         finalize the transition. An example of one of these decisions was the
                         administration and management of the LMS. It will initially continue
                         to be hosted by an outside contractor, with the plan to move the
                         platform to a local Zambian vendor to manage and maintain it in the
                         future. The activities are now led by the university teaching hospital,
                         which was already involved with other digital education activities.
CHAPTER 17


Ethical Decision-
Making Tools
by Jordan Richard Schoenherr and Jodi Lis



This chapter builds on the concepts presented in Chapter 7 and o!ers
a decision process tool to guide practitioners in an ethical practice of
learning engineering.


Learning engineering is a value-laden process. Learning engineers must consider
the ethical affordances of instructional design and assessment in addition to
technical affordances. Values and ethics must inform all phases of the design and
implementation process. Although some value-based checklists are available (for
example, Carnegie Mellon University’s Designing Ethical AI Experiences: Checklist
and Agreement), learning engineering requires questions that are specific to the
design process. Consequently, this chapter presents an ethics assessment instrument.
These tools have been developed to be complementary. The Sense-making Ethical
Evaluation Matrix for Ethical Design (SEEM-ED) reflects a bottom-up approach
that learning engineers can use when developing products and processes. SEEM-
ED assumes that there are two relevant dimensions to ethical assessment: (1) ethical
principles and (2) the stage of the learning engineering process.
       Numerous ethical principles can inform the learning engineering process.
Principles can be found in codes of conduct as well as legal standards. Codes tend
to provide high-level values that might not be clearly specified for a specific learn-
ing engineering context. Legal standards reflect minimal standards associated with
compliance rather than more complex, dynamic ethical concerns. Consequently, eth-
ical sense-making requires that we consider the ethical affordances, disaffordances,
and dysaffordances of a design—how a design assists users and how the design can
impede or discriminate against users. This requires considering technological affor-
dances as well as social and mental processes. These topics are reviewed in more



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-22
362 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     detail in Chapter 7.
            SEEM-ED is a design assessment instrument for developers that asks specific
     questions about the design and addresses features of the ethical sense-making process.
     SEEM-ED assumes that ethical principles can be translated into specific questions
     of values and ethics. It additionally assumes that these questions should be asked
     throughout the learning engineering process during the identification of challenges,
     the iterations of the design process, the implementation of a design, and through later
     investigation. Moreover, we must consider how related and complementary designs
     compete and cooperate in sociotechnical systems (Chapter 3).
            A demonstration of the SEEM-ED approach is provided in Figure 17.1. Here,
     we adopt the principles of the American Psychological Association to address issues
     related to teaching, research, and practice. These principles should then be used to
     inform each stage of the learning engineering process.
            A crucial feature of the SEEM-ED approach is that it doesn’t reflect a simple
     checklist. At a quantitative level, each question is assessed in terms of a continuum
     of responses. At a minimum, evaluation should use a three-point scale (represented
     below as Yes, Maybe, and No); however, a five-point scale (for example, Strong-
     ly Disagree, Disagree, Neither Agree nor Disagree, Agree, and Strongly Agree)
     would provide a greater level of precision. At each stage, a representative group
     of stakeholders should use the instrument to provide their ratings and responses
     independently. These responses can then be averaged to obtain an overall score on
     each dimension. If there are concerns about social influences, this process should be
     conducted anonymously.
            At a qualitative level, learning engineers and stakeholders should be able to
     articulate coherent responses to each question such that more questions are raised.
     Below each of the ratings, follow-up question(s) are presented. Each of these
     questions requires that the evaluators consider deeper questions and responses to
     social and ethical issues.
            When using the scale, learning engineers and stakeholders involved in
     participatory design should experience uncertainty such that the scale identifies
     ethical vulnerabilities in the design process. As we progress through the learning
     engineering process, more questions and greater specificity in the kinds of questions
     and answers suggest that SEEM-ED is being used effectively. All stakeholders
     should gain a better idea of the trade-offs between design approaches in terms of
     functionality and value.
                                                           Ethical Decision-Making Tools | 363




   Sense-Making Ethical Evaluation Matrix for Ethical Design
1. FIDELITY AND RESPONSIBILITY
Will the product / process promote trust in users and the general public?        Yes / Maybe / No
How will you assess trust / confidence in the product / process?

Have local regulations (laws, policies, guidelines) been reviewed and            Yes / Maybe / No
incorporated into the design?
How have experts in these domains been engaged?

Have users and other stakeholders been consulted?                                Yes / Maybe / No
Are there any accessibility issues that might limit this process?

Does the design comply with relevant codes of conduct (for example, IEEE)?       Yes / Maybe / No
How have codes and guidelines been identified?

Are there conflicts of interests? Conflicts between codes, laws, and police?     Yes / Maybe / No
How are they resolved?

Have material, financial, and human resources been used efficiently?             Yes / Maybe / No
How have these measures been selected?

2. INTEGRITY
Have developers / designers clearly shared their competencies and limits?        Yes / Maybe / No
What barriers to knowledge translation are possible?

Have any intentional or unintentional errors or biases been corrected?           Yes / Maybe / No
How have corrections been reported / addressed?

Have the features of the product / process been represented accurately?          Yes / Maybe / No
How have you ensured that users understand these features?

Have only those parties contributing to the design been acknowledged?            Yes / Maybe / No
What criteria were used to determine contributorship?

Have all parties contributing to the design been acknowledged?                   Yes / Maybe / No
How have parties been acknowledged? Are di!erent criteria used for each group?

3. BENEFICENCE AND NONMALEFICENCE
Are there foreseeable short- and medium-term harms and benefits?                 Yes / Maybe / No
What risk management framework and mitigation strategies have been used?
Are there foreseeable long-term harms and benefits?                              Yes / Maybe / No
What risk management framework and mitigation strategies have been used?
Do users understand the harms / benefits?                                        Yes / Maybe / No
How have harms/benefits been communicated to users? Which individuals,
communities, and governmental organizations have been involved?
364 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




     Sense-Making Ethical Evaluation Matrix for Ethical Design
  Have the real, perceived, and potential risks been assessed?                                     Yes / Maybe / No
  Which stakeholders and experts are engaged? Are they a representative group?
  Have users’ experiences be assessed in the long term?                                            Yes / Maybe / No
  How have these measures been identified? What kind of risk models will be used?

  4. JUSTICE
  Will the harms / benefits be distributed equally among users and nonusers?                       Yes / Maybe / No
  How have the user and nonuser population been identified? How will you address
  trade-o!s between groups? How have these been justified?
  Will benefits / harms be shared in all geographic regions in which the                           Yes / Maybe / No
  product / process operates?
  Why will these regions be a!ected? How have geographic boundaries been
  identified?
  Is the product / process accessible?                                                             Yes / Maybe / No
  What are the solution’s a!ordances, disa!ordances, and dysa!ordances?
  Are users’ rights protected by the design of the product / process?                              Yes / Maybe / No
  What legal/ethical frameworks have been used? How have you assessed “rights”
  in the context of this project?
  Are users’ rights potentially threatened by the design of product / process?                     Yes / Maybe / No
  Are there alternative designs that might limit potential threats? Have stakeholders’
  experiences and perceptions been used to inform the design process?
  Will user / nonuser rights di!er depending on the geographic region?                             Yes / Maybe / No
  How will you address regional di!erences? How will you communicate these
  di!erences to users?

  5. RESPECT FOR PEOPLE’S RIGHTS AND DIGNITY
  Does the product / process protect users’ rights to privacy / confidentiality?                   Yes / Maybe / No
  How have you assessed privacy? How has confidentiality/anonymity been
  preserved in the data?
  Does the design team have ethics education, training, and experience?                            Yes / Maybe / No
  How much experience and in what context was knowledge acquired? Is it
  appropriate to the user population and geographical region?
  Would / will any feature adversely a!ect the self-determination of the user?                     Yes / Maybe / No
  How have you assessed users’ awareness of the consequences of using the
  product / process? How have users been informed / educated?

  FIGURE 17.1. Sensemaking Ethical Evaluation Matrix for Ethical Design
  The SEEM-ED matrix is not meant to be an exhaustive list of questions. Rather, these questions
  represent a starting point. A critical aspect of using the SEEM-ED approach requires that
  all stakeholders contribute to the development of relevant ethical questions.
CHAPTER 18


Data Analysis Tools
by Erin Czerwinski, Tanvi Domadia, Scotty D. Craig,
Jim Goodell, and Steve Ritter



This chapter builds on the concepts from the foundations Chapter 6
on data analysis. It provides a learning analytics process model and
discusses a few other analysis methods and tools.


This chapter begins with an overview of the learning analytics process model. It’s a
guide to the kinds of analytical methods used to answer different kinds of questions.
These methods may be used within a system for feedback to the learner, to people
supporting learners, and to the engineering team to inform iterative improvements
to engineered solutions. Following this, information about the OpenSimon Toolkit
and learning curve analysis is included.


Learning Analytics Process Model 1
In learning engineering, data analytics are part of an experimentally driven learning
sciences approach to iterative improvement. Data analysts on a learning engineering
team help to develop A/B tests to compare various learning conditions, collecting
new experimental data. They also work with others who are experts in the sub-
ject matter, implementation context, and learning sciences to interpret and answer
“why” questions that the data alone can’t answer. Just like the learning engineering
process is iterative, the learning analytics process may be iterative. Analysis begins
with a question and may result in answers and follow-on questions. The following
subsections describe that iterative process. Each corresponds to part of the learning
analytics process model in Figure 18.1—starting from the top.
       The learning analytics process model is a synthesis of concepts from Ryan
Baker’s Massively Open Online Textbook (MOOT), which is made up of a series of
videos and links to the related edX course. The model can be used as a framework


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-23
   LEARNING ANALYTICS PROCESS MODEL
                     1. What question do we want the data to answer?
    START            2. Do we have the data to answer this question? No                      Instrument to collect data.
    HERE             3. Translate into data questions.
                   Real- world question: Which students need intervention?
                   Data questions: How do I predict the probability? What’s the cutoff for intervention?

                                  PREDICT                             INFER                                    MINE
  ANSWER              How can I accurately predict         What meaning can be inferred          How can I isolate the most
  THE                 new data points (e.g. learner        from the data?                        useful information from a large
  QUESTION            outcomes)?                                                                 data set?

  GOALS               A model to predict a single          An estimate of association            Discover features, patterns,
                      aspect of the data (value or         between an outcome variable           correlations, or anomalies of a
                      category) with high accuracy         and predictor variables.              data set useful for decision
                      and low error                                                              making or further analysis

  EXAMPLES            What’s the probability a             Do the data indicate that a           What features of the data are
                      learner will successfully            learner is bored or frustrated?       most useful for creating a
                      complete this course? Should         Do the data indicate that the         predictive model? What
                      we classify the learner as           learning activity results in          interactions between learners
                      “likely” or “unlikely” to            learning?                             engaged in collaborative
                      complete?                                                                  learning are most productive?
