FIGURE 18.1. Learning Analytics Process Model
CC-BY Jim Goodell and Steve Ritter
                                                              Data Analysis Tools | 367



for use by those doing the analysis and by others on the learning engineering team
who want to understand the kinds of analysis that might be applicable. For a deeper
dive into these topics see Ryan Baker’s MOOT (as well as his MOOC).2


Start with a Question
The process starts by identifying a real-world question that the team hopes the data
can help them answer. Often learning engineering involves experimentation, so the
question might be, “Does learning work best under treatment A or treatment B?”


Translate the Question into a Data Question
The real-world question must be translated into a data question. In Chapter 6 we
looked at a simple hypothetical example where the real-world question might have
been written as, “What number of test prep worked examples should a student
experience to score highest on the LSAT?” A translation of that question might be,
“Given data from previous trials where students experienced zero, eight, or fifteen
worked examples before taking a post-test and using the scores from those post-
tests, can we predict the scores of students exposed to other numbers of worked
examples?” In other words, “Can we predict the number of worked examples likely
to result in the highest score?”


Do we have the data needed to answer the question?
This isn’t a simple question. What we’re really asking is:
      1. Do we have data that can give us an answer or insight?
      2. Are the data sufficient to give us an answer with some level of certainty?
      3. If not, will it be enough to give any value or insight for future iteration?



  If you know almost nothing, almost anything will tell you something.
                           – Douglas W. Hubbard 3

      For the example from Chapter 6 we might have said that the data are insufficient
to predict with any level of certainty. With just three data points (two if we consider
one of the data points was zero worked examples), limited number of participants,
368 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     and the reported margins of error from the original study, we won’t be able to get
     an accurate prediction. However, it might be worth doing just to gain some insights
     about what additional experiments might be needed in a next iteration.


     Is more instrumentation or experimentation needed?
     If the data aren’t sufficient to completely answer the question you might ask what
     additional data are needed and how can you collect them. This might spawn a new
     subproject to develop new instrumentation. Sometimes new experiments need to be
     designed, and sometimes it’s just a matter of collecting more of the same.


     Based on the question, what kind of analysis is needed?
     Look at the table near the top of the Learning Analytics Process Model with the
     headings Predict, Infer, and Mine. Which of these analytics methods is most ap-
     propriate for your data question? Are you trying to forecast something that might
     happen in the future, such as the likelihood of a student passing a course? (If so, use
     predictive modeling.) Are you trying to uncover unobservable or latent factors, such
     as whether a learner is trying to game the system based on their interaction with it?
     (If so, use inference modeling.) Or are you trying to uncover patterns in a large data
     set, such as whether certain test questions show bias for learners from particular
     backgrounds. (If so, use data mining.)


     What analysis technique should I use?
     For each category of analysis there are sets of methods that might be applied. These
     methods are also called analysis or modeling techniques. Our example from Chap-
     ter 6 used a kind of linear regression or curve-fitting. That falls into the “regression
     prediction” category. In that case, the curve was modeled as a quadratic equation. If
     we wanted to predict which students in a data set might answer a problem correct-
     ly given previous answers to similar questions we might use “classification” tech-
     niques. The subsections below outline some common modeling techniques.


     PREDICTIVE MODELING

     The goal of predictive modeling is to develop a model that can infer a single aspect
     of the data (called a “label”) from some combination of other data. It may be used
                                                               Data Analysis Tools | 369



to make predictions about the future; for example, Duolingo uses a model to predict
whether a learner will be able to translate the French phrase le chapeau rouge into
the English phrase the red hat. Predictive modeling may also be used to make infer-
ences about the present, such as predicting if a student is bored during an activity. Or
it could be used to evaluate a design, such as predicting what scores a set of learners
might get on a post-test based on various design options.

   ► Regression Prediction: A model that predicts a number is called a “regressor”
      in data mining. The overall task is called regression. To build a regression, start
      with a data set where you already know the answer—called the training label
      because it’s used to train the model. Associated with each training label record
      are other variables, called features in data mining. The goal of regression is to
      find which features, in what combination, can predict the label’s value.
   ► Classification is used to predict things that aren’t numbers, such as the
      answer to a yes-or-no question. Instead of a number, the label is one of a set
      of categories (a classifier). This is the same as regression prediction except
      we add one or more thresholds; for example, we could say the answer is “yes”
      when a given score is greater than 0.5 and “no” when the variable is less than
      or equal to 0.5.


INFERENCE MODELING

The goal of inference modeling is to estimate an association between an outcome
variable and a predictor variable. In other words, it’s used to understand the relation-
ship between x and y.

   ► Knowledge Inference: The goal of knowledge inference is to estimate a
      person or group of people’s knowledge, skills, or abilities to give feedback
      to learners, instructors, and other stakeholders—including the learning
      engineering team—so they can make the learning conditions better. It’s also
      called latent knowledge estimation, where latent means that something isn’t
      directly measurable (such as confusion or boredom).
   ► Behavior Detection: Automated models can use log data (such as clickstream
      data from an online activity) to infer a learner’s behavior. This can help us
      understand how different behaviors affect learning, which is useful for iterative
      improvement as well as for adaptive systems that respond to behaviors to
      improve learner success.
370 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     MINING

     The goal of mining is to discover features, patterns, correlations, or anomalies
     of a data set that are useful for decision-making or further analysis, for example,
     which features of an instructional design correlate to desired outcomes. (The word
     “discover” implies that mining is more of a method for science than engineering, but
     especially when combined with other techniques these methods can be a valuable
     part of making data sets useful for answering specific engineering questions.)
     Mining techniques include, but aren’t limited to, the following:

        ► Relationship Mining: The goal of relationship mining is to discover variables
           that are related to one another within sets of many variables. Relationship
           mining is also key to big data reduction, that is, reducing the number of
           variables and size of the data set while keeping the relevant information
           needed to answer the question.
        ► Correlation Mining is used to find substantial linear correlations between
           variables. Remember, though: Correlation doesn’t mean causation!
        ► Causal Mining is used to infer causality, as in x causes y.
        ► Association Rule Mining is used to find simple if / then rules in the data set.
           For example, if a learner does x, they’re likely do y. This is useful for finding
           unexpected connections and generating hypotheses.
        ► Sequential Pattern Mining is used for finding patterns over time. For
           example, if a person reads a book on learning engineering now, how likely is
           it that person will take a class on education data mining later?
        ► Network Analysis is used for finding connections and their relative strengths,
           such as in social networks.


     How good is my model?
     For prediction and inference modeling the next step in the process is to test how good
     the model is using different data sets than those used to create the model. Machine
     learning experts call this “model goodness” and recommend using techniques that
     test confidence and validity of a model.
                                                               Data Analysis Tools | 371



CONFIDENCE

Confidence is about how much you can trust that a prediction will be accurate. A
learning engineering team might use classification as described above to predict yes,
a design decision is likely to improve learning, or no, it likely won’t. But a yes with
only a 50.1% confidence is not as good as a yes with a 99% confidence. A learning
engineering team might decide to adopt a design decision that’s predicted to work
only if the confidence is over a certain percentage.
       Design decisions or interventions can have potential benefits and potential
risks. So decisions about whether to act can be made using a cost-benefit analysis.
It’s important to consider, for example, “What’s the cost of an incorrectly applied
intervention or design decision?” along with “What’s the potential benefit of a
correctly applied intervention?” We might be less likely to apply a design decision
that could do a lot of good if there’s also a good chance that it might do harm if
incorrectly applied or applied under certain conditions. In this case, analysis can
suggest when it’s safe to apply a stronger but riskier approach and when to revert to
a weaker but safer one.


VALIDITY

Validity refers to the extent to which a model reflects what you meant it to model.
For example, if a model is meant to analyze a learner’s level of understanding, but
instead it inadvertently measures (or is affected by) their engagement level, then
that model wouldn’t be particularly valid for its intended use. There are multiple
dimensions to consider when testing for validity, including the following:

   ► Generalizability: Does your model (developed with data from one set of
      conditions) transfer to a new context in which you want to use it?
   ► Cross-Learner Validity: If you developed a model to detect learner frustration
      using data from three learners, will that model still work for other learners in
      your target population?
   ► Ecological Validity: If you built a detector in a lab setting will it still work in
      real-life education and training environments?
   ► Construct Validity: Does the model behave in a way consistent with
      theoretical hypotheses?
   ► Predictive Validity: Does the model accurately fit existing data as well
      as future data? For example, if you built a linear model that predicts the
372 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           relationship between hours studied (from 0 to 10 hours) and post-test scores,
           does the model accurately predict the outcomes of new data, such as the post-
           tests scores of learners who studied for 15 hours?
        ► Content Validity: Does the model cover the full intended scope, such as the
           full range of predicted behaviors or indicators of learner states? Are there
           other indicators that should be considered?
        ► Conclusion Validity: Are your conclusions justified based on the evidence?


     Will it be helpful to visualize the outputs?
     There are times when visualizing data is the best way to spot features for decision-
     making. For example, the learning curve analysis discussed below shows an example
     where we expect to see the error rate go down over time as students learn, but a
     visualization of the data shows a point in time where the error rate jumped. That’s a
     case where the anomaly is much easier to see on a graph than by looking at numbers.




     OpenSimon Toolkit
     Carnegie Mellon University (CMU) has packaged its educational technologies
     developed through decades of educational research into a toolkit for conducting
     learning engineering. These tools support learning design through analysis and
     iterative improvement. Two of these tools support data analytics.
            DataShop 4 is first an open online repository of over 3,000 EdTech data sets.
     It’s also an online tool for analysis of log data from learning activities. It can be
     used to perform various analyses for visualization and modeling of learning data
     like the learning curve analysis shown in Chapter 6 where CMU could visualize the
     learning, or lack thereof, taking place in its first-year math course. These analyses
     also provided key information about which skills students needed more practice
     with, as well as where there were holes in the analytics being collected.
            LearnSphere 5 provides a learning analytics infrastructure within the Open-
     Simon Toolkit. This infrastructure provides the ability to perform repeatable custom
     analysis methods, and stores learning analytics data in a standard format that can
     be analyzed using DataShop. LearnSphere was designed for educational research
     and includes access to anonymized research data sets from multiple platforms via
     its portal.
                                                              Data Analysis Tools | 373



Learning Curve Analysis
ITERATIVE IMPROVEMENT USING OPEN LEARNING INITIATIVE
INSTRUMENTED COURSEWARE


Learning curve analysis can be used to infer whether a student is
receiving sufficient practice for a skill or if there are potential problems
with the instructional or assessment content tied to a specific skill.



The Open Learning Initiative (OLI) at CMU offers courseware with content that
is instrumented to collect learner data, along with its semantic context. Rather than
analyzing page views and time on task, which are proxies for information about
how the students are learning, OLI collects data on user selections to answer
questions and respond to activity interactions that are carefully designed and tagged
to the learning objectives and skills (also known as sub-objectives or knowledge
components) for the purpose of measuring learner mastery toward the stated learning
outcomes. These data drive important feedback loops, delivering data back to the
students, the instructors, learning designers, and the learning science community
(as this anonymized data are collected in large data repositories such as DataShop
and LearnSphere). Instructors use these data, reporting to a dashboard in the OLI
interface, to drive classroom discussions based on which skills might be difficult to
learn for the majority of the students.
       The skill tagging later helps the course developers, in the aggregate, gain more
insight into the learning design of the course. After the course has been completed by
students in any given course cohort, OLI can export the data to DataShop, the world’s
largest repository of learning interaction data. DataShop can provide a visualization
of the data, providing the simplest form of analysis with learning curves.
       Learning curves represent the amount of mastery over each skill. Learning
curve analysis is a kind of knowledge inference. When viewing a learning curve, the
x-axis represents the number of opportunities against a given skill, and the y-axis
represents the aggregated error rate for all the students of that data set. Ideally the
error rate should decrease with each opportunity, which means that the curve always
should slope downward. It’s expected that the more that students practice with a
given outcome, the more adept they’ll become at that skill, and therefore have fewer
errors on problems targeting that skill.
374 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                    GOOD                                          PROBLEMATIC
                dist_when_2_var_r_adj_or_not (Category: Good)         categorize_var_on_path_as_collider_non_collider



                                       STUDENT ERROR RATE
                                        (SOLID)
  Error Rate




                              Questions Over Time                            Questions Over Time
                                                    E
                                  LEARNING CURV
                                       (DOTTED)
               FIGURE 18.2. Example learning curves, illustrating a good and a problematic
               skill in a graphical causal modeling course delivered by OLI courseware
               CC-BY Carnegie Mellon University
                 Error Rate




                                                         categorize_var_on_path_as_collider_non_collider

                                                  Questions Over Time

               FIGURE 18.3. Example learning curve representing a problematic skill in
               a graphical causal modeling course delivered by OLI courseware
               CC-BY Carnegie Mellon University
                                                              Data Analysis Tools | 375


                                            X1




                                                         X2




                                            X3




                                            X4

 Categorize each variable on the path X1→X2→X3→X4 as a
 collider or non-collider. Select collider or non-collider.
 X1      !"#$%"&&'()*

 X2      !"#$%"&&'()*

 X3      +"&&'()*

 X4      +"&&'()*

FIGURE 18.4. Viewing a problematic question as part of learning
curve analysis for instructional/assessment content
CC-BY Carnegie Mellon University



       Learning curve analysis can tell us about how students are doing and can be
used to uncover problems with instructional content. For a particular skill, if the
learning curve doesn’t slope downward or shows upward spikes, it might represent
some problem within the learning design for that skill.
       Figure 18.2 shows an example from a course on graphical causal modeling.
In this example, the curve on the left is an example of a good learning curve, and
the one on the right is a problematic learning curve. As seen in Figure 18.3, the
problematic learning curve from the right side is for the skill with the identifier
“categorize_var_on_path_as_collider_non_collider.” From looking at the learning
curve graph, it appears there are a total of six questions in the courseware providing
practice and assessment against this one skill.
376 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            DataShop offers the ability to explore further and expose more of the data for
     any given point in the graph, offering functionality for the user to click on the point
     at the spike in the learning curve, taking the user directly to the question where the
     spike occurred.
            In the example provided, Figure 18.4 shows the question related to the spike
     shown in the problematic learning curve above. As you can see, the issue is caused
     by the third item in this set of questions that asks students to categorize X3 as a
     “collider” or a “non-collider.” We can try to understand where students specifically
     encountered an obstacle by further viewing a table from DataShop, as shown in
     Figure 18.5. It summarizes the answer options and the numbers of students who
     selected each as well as the feedback associated with each option.
            Upon further examination of the feedback for X3, as shown in Figure 18.6,
     there is mention about a “mediator,” which isn’t mentioned at all in the list of skills
     being analyzed. This observation could lead one to conclude that “identifying a col-
     lider” and “identifying a mediator” are potentially two different skills.
            A learning engineering data-driven decision from this analysis might be to de-
     fine a separate sub-skill “identify the mediator” as a separate knowledge component
     to be practiced and assessed. Practice problems for this separate skill would then be
     developed and included in the online course model.
            In this case, the course developers added “Identify the mediator” as a skill in
     the course and added practice questions for identifying mediators to the course as
     scaffolding toward mastery of the “categorize_var_on_path_as_collider_non_col-
     lider” skill. In the subsequent implementation of the course, the learning curve anal-
     yses for the “categorize_var_on_path_as_collider_non_collider” skill were found to
     be “good” learning curves, and the learning curve analyses related to the new skill
     “identify_mediators” also were found to be “good” learning curves.
            In another case, Carnegie Learning used learning curve analysis to discover
     ways that students think about solving linear equations. Their MATHia software
     teaches students to solve linear equations, including those of the form !"#$#% (such
     as &"#$#'). A learning curve shows that students make fewer errors as they gain ex-
     perience with such equations. But a close analysis of the learning curves may show
     “blips.” The blips may represent cases where equations that might seem similar to
     an expert are seen as different by students.
            For example, suppose the software gave students a set of equations that solve
     to an integer, such as ("#$#), &"#$#*(, +"#$#,, and so on. Students will likely get better
     at solving such equations, showing a learning curve where errors are reduced over
     time. If these students next encountered an equation that’s superficially similar but
                                                                         Data Analysis Tools | 377



 Evaluation    Number of    Answer                 Feedback/Classification
               Observations
 Correct       9 (81.82%)          <material>      <material>Correct. X1 is not a
                                   Non-Collider    collider because it only has one
                                   </material>     edge connected to it.</material>
 Incorrect     2 (18.18%)          <material>      <material>Incorrect. X1 cannot be a collider
                                   Collider        because X1 only has one edge connected
                                   </material>     to it. Remember, a variable is a collider
                                                   if two edges point into it.</material>
 Evaluation    Number of    Answer                 Feedback/Classification
               Observations
 Correct       4 (36.36%)          <material>      <material>Correct. X3 is not a collider
                                   Non-Collider    since it is a mediator, and all mediators
                                   </material>     are non-colliders.</material>
 Incorrect     7 (63.64%)          <material>      <material>Incorrect. X3 cannot be a collider
                                   Collider        since it is a mediator, and all mediators are
                                   </material>     non-colliders. Remember, a variable is a
                                                   collider if two edges point into it.</material>

FIGURE 18.5. Answers and student responses from the learning curve analysis
CC-BY Carnegie Mellon University
                                                  X1




                                                           X2




                                                  X3




                                                  X4

             Categorize each variable on the path X1→X2→X3→X4 as a
             collider or non-collider. Select collider or non-collider.
     !"##$%&'()*(+,(-"&(.(%"//+0$#(1$%.2,$(+&("-/3(4.,("-$($05$(%"--$%&$0(&"(+&'

     !"##$%&'()6(+,(-"&(.(%"//+0$#(,+-%$(+&(+,(.(7$0+.&"#8(.-0(.//(7$0+.&"#,(.#$(-"-9%"//+0$#,'

     :-%"##$%&'()A(%.--"&(1$(.(%"//+0$#(,+-%$(+&(+,(.(7$0+.&"#8(.-0(.//(7$0+.&"#,(.#$
     -"-9%"//+0$#,'(<$7$71$#8(.(=.#+.1/$(+,(.(%"//+0$#(+>(&?"($05$,(@"+-&(+-&"(+&'

     :-%"##$%&'();(%.--"&(1$(.(%"//+0$#(1$%.2,$();("-/3(4.,("-$($05$(%"--$%&$0(&"(+&'(
     <$7$71$#8(.(=.#+.1/$(+,(.(%"//+0$#(+>(&?"($05$,(@"+-&(+-&"(+&'

FIGURE 18.6. Viewing answer options and feedback as part of
learning curve analysis for instructional/assessment content
CC-BY from Carnegie Mellon University
378 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     whose answer isn’t an integer (such as ("#$#- or &"#$#,), the learning curve is likely
     to show an increase in the error rate for those equations. The reason is that students
     can solve an equation like ("#$#) by thinking “two times what number is six”? But
     few students can apply this thought process to an equation like ("#$#- (“two times
     what number is seven?”). In learning science terms, the knowledge required to solve
     !"#$#% equations where the answer is an integer doesn’t transfer to the case where
     the answer isn’t an integer. Paying attention to learning curves can reveal subtle
     ways that students think about problem solving.

     1 CC-BY Jim Goodell and Steve Ritter. Informed by:

          www.datasciencecentral.com/profiles/blogs/inference-vs-prediction-in-one-picture
        • Baker, Ryan S. Big Data and Education. 6th ed. Philadelphia, PA: University of Pennsylvania,
          2020. www.upenn.edu/learninganalytics/MOOT/bigdataeducation.html
        • Fayyad, Usama, Gregory Piatetsky-Shapiro, and Padhraic Smyth. 1996. “From Data Mining
          to Knowledge Discovery in Databases.”AI Magazine 17, no. 3: 37–54. https://doi.org/10.1609/
          aimag.v17i3.1230

     3 Hubbard, Douglas W. How to Measure Anything: Finding the Value of Intangibles in Business.
       Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, 2014.
