  VISION AND
COMMENTARY
A SHORT STORY ABOUT LEARNING ENGINEERS IN THE FUTURE
CHAPTER 19


The Future World
with Learning
Engineering: A Story
by Sae Schatz and JJ Walcutt



This chapter weaves together five stories from a not-too-distant future
where learning engineering has become the norm. This is a fictional
narrative of what might become—created for our shared insight and,
hopefully, enjoyment!


Imagine sometime in the future. Technology and globalization have driven disruptive
change across the world. The intelligence augmentation economy is in full swing,
and just like the industrial and service economies before it, this new paradigm has
fundamentally transformed the nature of work, life, and learning.
       Information has become ubiquitous. Jobs that once required specialized
subject-matter expertise are now being filled by less expert—but highly adaptable—
people augmented with high-performing intelligent agents. The value of lower-order
knowledge and skills has diminished, replaced by an increased emphasis on more
transcendent capabilities such as continuous learning, systems thinking, creativity,
distributed teamwork, and a breadth of knowledge literacy.
       Schools are no longer the primary gatekeepers of knowledge. Learning and
working happens anywhere and everywhere—in virtual and physical spaces and
across a shifting landscape of organizations, platforms, and social constructs. The
lines have blurred between education, training, working, and participating in society.
Words like “curriculum” and “teacher” have taken on different meanings, given the
range of autonomous, application-oriented, and adaptive learning approaches now
regularly used. Similarly, the purpose of primary and secondary schools has shifted
away from an outmoded information-centric model to one focused on preparing



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-25
382 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     students for life-long learning, global citizenship, and human-machine collaboration.
     Schools try to help students develop a working familiarity of as many areas as
     possible, along with the mental models needed to connect concepts across disciplines,
     adeptly address novel challenges, navigate an interconnected information-dense
     environment, and participate as ethical contributors within society.
            Alternative forms of learning and development, including self-directed,
     experiential, and just-in-time learning in the flow of work have also become
     commonplace. Across this tapestry of experiences, individuals demonstrate their
     mastery through verified credentials aligned to competencies. Competencies have
     become a currency of sorts. They’re held in individuals’ digital wallets and used for
     a variety of learning and working purposes such as discovering relevant educational
     opportunities, connecting potential employees to employers, and evaluating
     organizational talent management needs.
            In this new world, global learning engineering teams work together with
     intelligent agents, communities of learners and other stakeholders, human and AI
     learning coaches, implementation teams, and big data analysts to co-create the
     tools and conditions for optimal life-long learning. This chapter follows the story
     of five team members from different functional domains and geographical regions
     as they collaborate on learning engineering tasks. These stories aren’t meant to
     comprehensively depict learning engineering in the future. They’re just one slice of
     life from one particular team that might someday exist.


                                            !!"
     Arun’s Story
     Arun looked up from his bank of monitors. Above them, he could see rain pelting
     his office window. Normally, it showed an impressive view of Bengaluru’s bustling
     National Highway 44, but today Arun could only see the gray smudges of an after-
     noon downpour. He sighed and turned back to his work. It was nearly 5 p.m., but the
     thought of commuting home in that weather slowed his feet. Well, he thought, may
     as well stay a bit later and hope for a break in the storm.
            Arun worked in the analysis division at Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek. His team
     specifically focused on global trends and insights reporting. Businesses paid good
     money for their forecasts. The finance and entertainment reports were most popular,
     but their reports on emerging learning and development targets also had a dedicated
     following. Years ago he would have expected only schools and universities to be
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 383



interested, but in this age of life-long learning, businesses and NGOs accounted for
the report’s largest customer base.
       A dialog box on Arun’s console was flashing, bringing his attention back to
the screen. The blinking indicator meant the data reduction algorithm had finished
processing. He’d planned to finish his data wrangling work tomorrow, but with the
weather looking like a monsoon outside, he may as well handle it now.
       Arun clicked the confirmation button to proceed, and a visualization filled his
screen. On the left, ranked-ordered lists of popular topics appeared. Icons next to
each item showed the topic’s popularity over time along with a summary of the typ-
ical sentiment felt toward it. On the dashboard’s right side, a world map glowed with
colorful overlays illustrating regional differences, and with a few clicks, he could
explore other filters, like demographic or temporal overlays.
       Arun moved his cursor down the list as he reviewed it. Immediately, his eye
was drawn to the line, “First successful Mars walk.” Like many around the world,
Arun was captivated by the inaugural human spaceflight to Mars. He’d followed
the astronauts’ vlog and had tuned into the live (or, rather, three and a half hours
delayed) stream of the crew’s first steps on the red planet. The world’s interest in
the Martian mission wasn’t surprising, although he hadn’t expected the topic to be
trending so vigorously after this many weeks. He clicked through to examine it
in more detail. Its emotionality quotient looked good—broadly positive and with a
moderately high excitement level across demographic groups. This trend seemed to
have across-the-board staying power. Closing out that dialog box, Arun confirmed
the trend’s inclusion, formally adding it to his report.
       Returning to the master list, Arun clicked through several more items,
reprioritizing or eliminating entries all together—like the new K-pop sensation
W1$H Girls Magic. Among certain demographics, that K-pop band was far and
away the most popular trend, but its sentiment was all wrong for this particular
report. The emotionality magnitude was running near the ceiling, and Arun knew
from experience that pop-culture fads with that sentiment profile tended to create
distractions rather than hooks when it came to instructional settings.
       After finishing the popular trends list, Arun turned to the scholarly trends
window. The algorithm sifted through the previous month’s academic publications
to create a summary of the most popular topics, latest breakthroughs, or leading
indicators of new science and engineering findings. He brought up a different view,
a dashboard showing a dense network diagram with clusters of interconnected dots
representing the relationships and frequencies of different topics. Working with his
AI decision-support bot, Arun sorted through the trends, adding the most relevant
384 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     ones to this month’s report and culling those he deemed less viable.
            A couple of hours later, Arun was done. He’d sorted through the popular and
     scholarly trends and was ready to add the crowdsourced Experts Pool™ opinions, a
     unique selling point his company offered. The expert panel for this report included a
     group of verified learning engineers who’d had the opportunity to review and com-
     ment on trends using Delphi-style techniques and a private (human-with-AI mod-
     erated) online discussion board. They received full access to the trends data at no
     cost, and in turn, Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek benefited from their human-centered,
     context-specific, and data-informed practices and expertise.
            But the real secret sauce in the Experts Pool™ came from the company’s
     integrated testing platform. Thousands of end-user learners received early access
     to the (sometimes experimental) learning experiences built from the report, and in
     return they shared their own backgrounds and experiences. Using natural language
     processing, Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek was able to ingest their comments and use data-
     mining algorithms to uncover learner-centric insights. In recent years, the company
     had even established a dedicated user-testing and participatory design division based
     on this capability, and now that division supported learning engineering teams from
     around the world in A/B testing and iterative curriculum development.
            Arun spent another hour scrolling through the experts’ commentary online,
     while checking the summaries and recommendations supplied by the corresponding
     AI filtering app with the integrated end-user model. As he reviewed the material,
     Arun continued to nudge the parameters, causing his AI assistant to optimize the
     master trends list until it was up to Arun’s standard. By incorporating the expert
     and learner opinions, along with AI-supported learning engineers like Arun,
     Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek Learning and Development (L&D) Trends Targets Report
     was nearly 15 percent more accurate than their next successful competitor.
            Human-algorithm teams for the win, Arun mused.
            Outside, the sky had darkened from pale gray to a deep coal black. Night had
     fallen, and the rain hadn’t slacked. Arun rang for a runner to bring him some food as
     he settled in for a long night. No use in stopping now, he thought. If I get the report
     packaged tonight, then I can just take tomorrow off.
            As the last step, Arun checked the information quality of the aggregate list.
     The AI agent had already applied the Mägi-Yang filter, an algorithm named after
     two AI researchers who’d won a Turing Award for their work—their Trust DApp
     algorithm. It used a combination of explainable neural networks and distributed led-
     gers to provide a reliable estimation of an information source’s validity and bias. It
     worked best when the source data came from stable organizations or people with
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 385



patterns of trusted micro-transitions over time. It also worked well on high-volume
data sets, like the one Arun was working with. The M-Y Trust DApp flagged several
trends as possible sources of misinformation, incomplete, or misleading materials.
Arun reviewed each warning, particularly the high-risk items. He also published the
risk-by-topic list to the expert discussion board for their future feedback.
       Like the information sources themselves, each of the reviewers in the Experts
Pool™ also developed Trust Factors over time, based on the fairness and accuracy
of their contributions to the group. Arun couldn’t have mentally kept up with the
different layers of reliability across topics, sources, and individual peer reviewers.
Fortunately, the system calculated those weights for him, and the result was that his
company’s L&D Trends Targets Report was regarded as one of the most valid and
reliable publications in its sector.
       Finally, the data set was nearly complete. Arun double-checked its metadata.
His software automatically tagged the package with the standard digital signatures—
date, time, contributor codes, and so on. This included identifiers for the contributing
algorithms, along with the aggregate MYTCO score (the Mägi-Yang Trust
Coefficient) for the package. The metadata also included machine-readable pointers
to the original data sources to show the lineage of the information used in the report,
and it had human-readable tags for the topics included in the data set. These topical
tags, like “astrophysics” or “game studies,” referenced the Worldwide Wikiword, a
community-maintained linked data set for disambiguating terms. Over time, Arun
had discovered that he always needed to check those tags; invariably, he’d find one
that was off. And it was just that case today. He chuckled to himself as he fixed the
link to “Mars” away from “a caramel and nougat candy bar” to “fourth planet from
the sun.” It was times like this when Arun was reminded that he would always have
a job, no matter how sophisticated his AI teammates became.
       Finally finished, Arun pressed “publish,” and smiled as the processing bar ap-
peared on his screen. Pleased with his long day of work, Arun stretched and turned
off his monitor. Outside, the rain had slowed, and miraculously, traffic on the NH-44
was light. He’d be able to make it home in half an hour—and he’d finished his work
for the rest of the week too. It had been a good day.


                                        !""
Dahlia’s Story
Dahlia nodded politely at her colleagues as she passed them in the hall. In one hand,
386 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     she carried a steaming espresso, and in her other, she clutched her bulky coat. Dahlia
     always struggled this time of year, when the sun didn’t rise until late in the morning.
     Blearily, she found her desk, situated near a window that looked onto one of Helsin-
     ki’s many cobblestone streets.
            Dahlia settled in, turning on her monitor and donning the oversized noise-
     canceling headphones she preferred. Her console came to life, and she logged in.
     Taking a sip from her mug, she clicked the icon for her Command Station app. It
     aggregated her work-related feeds into a single dashboard, including her email,
     assigned tasks, file update notifications, and filtered newsfeeds. Dahlia immediately
     noticed her team’s thread for the national curriculum project. It showed several
     new file commits. Presumably, that meant they’d met yesterday’s milestone gates.
     The project was the company’s largest and most important contract, financed by
     the Finnish National Agency for Education. The curriculum focused on adaptive,
     mixed-mode skills development in a collaborative problem-solving context that
     required students to demonstrate cross-domain knowledge literacy. Before she could
     explore the updates more fully, though, a priority alert caught her attention. One of
     her automatic reminders had flagged “L&D Trends Targets Report update” at the
     top of her daily checklist. Clicking the alert launched a visualization app where she
     could explore the latest L&D Trends Targets data set.
            Dahlia worked at a learning technology—or LTech—firm, one of several sim-
     ilar businesses in that burgeoning sector. Her own company was relatively small,
     just a few dozen employees. They balanced their size with a reputation for product
     excellence and through a network of worldwide partners, including the analytics
     firm Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek, who’d produced this trends report. Through such
     partnerships, the learning engineering team for any given product effectively includ-
     ed members from multiple companies, backgrounds, and capability areas.
            A few parts of the report immediately stood out to Dahlia. The excitement of
     the Mars mission continued to be high across all demographics. That was good. The
     national curriculum project team had already integrated those themes into several
     prototype lessons that were being evaluated through Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek’s
     user-testing platform, and she was pleased to see interest in the topic wasn’t waning.
     Dahlia also noted some developing topics in bioengineering, health sciences,
     corporate finance, and popular arts. The forthcoming release of the e-Mersion 360°,
     the next-generation consumer-grade VR platform with high-fidelity onboard haptics
     and proprioception sensors, also topped the list. People were calling it “gadget of the
     year” and the “herald of a new reality.” Whether that hype panned out, the system’s
     popularity had catalyzed substantial interest in new media creation, and as a result,
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 387



experiential immersive design had re-emerged as a hot topic. That was particularly
great for Dahlia, because she could easily add elements of new media design into
nearly every curricular package she created.
        Dahlia specialized in designing packages for problem-based learning, or PBL
for short, that integrated multiple topics from across different competency areas. For
instance, her most recent deliverable bundled sustainable agriculture, introduction to
the scientific method, public policy, and personal health into a package for secondary
schools. Dahlia also had several L&D projects under contract for business-to-
business (B2B) clients. Although they generally focused on workforce development,
they still used PBL and other pedagogical approaches similar to the modules she
built for schools and universities. Remembering this, Dahlia selected some of the
demographic filters on her visualization app, examining the latest employee up-
skilling developments in the L&D Trends Targets Report.
       The right-to-repair movement had become popular across the commercial
sector, and today, it was becoming common for large businesses to train their em-
ployees in basic maintenance skills. With that foundation, employees—assisted by
their AI agents—could fix just about anything. Several industry reports showed that
“troubleshooting literacy” helped businesses sustain software and equipment lon-
ger, and it increased productivity by shortening downtimes. As a result, just-in-time
learning modules on various repair topics were flying off the digital shelves, and the
PBL packages Dahlia designed were also popular. Her introductory modules helped
workers build basic troubleshooting competences, creating a baseline that let them
more effectively use the specific just-in-time packages when they needed them.
       The firm where Dahlia worked, Oivallus, also had a team focused on micro-
learning curation. Of course, since the mid-2020s, microlearning businesses had be-
come a dime a dozen. Anyone could search the web for whatever topic they wanted
and find hundreds of automated websites offering microlearning “courses.” In prac-
tice, typically, these were just strings of automatically assembled multimedia with
computer-generated quizzes tacked on. Initially hailed as a revolution in learning,
these microlearning aggregators had soon become jumbles of noisy content.
       Development of the MYTCO score helped. As its use grew in popularity,
it helped individuals find the most trustworthy resources. But just because an
instructional video or how-to guide isn’t biased or misleading, doesn’t mean it’s
pedagogically sound or appropriate for all contexts. As a result, in the old days of
crowdsourced microlearning, too many of these Frankenstein just-in-time-training
packages were created. And even though they included all of the de facto concepts,
the pieces were often too difficult to follow—like reading a book where different
388 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     authors wrote each page without consulting with one another. This was especially
     problematic for schools developing knowledge literacies, which require coherent
     learning experiences—particularly at the introductory stages. And after several
     large-scale state-sponsored studies, it became clear that the low-effort microlearning
     platforms weren’t really effective, at least from a learning outcomes perspective.
            That’s how Oivallus got its start. Their first successful product was a micro-
     learning curation service that used human-supervised AI to verify and tag instructional
     resources with contextual relevance and pedagogical metrics, making it possible for
     both educational software and human teachers to assemble and sequence the content
     into semantically coherent curricular arcs. The algorithm Oivallus developed also
     automatically identified topical, contextual, and pedagogical similarity so that
     assembled strings of learning resources would function as integrated lessons rather
     than disjoint factoids.
            Oivallus soon evolved from a software-centric startup to a learning engineering
     firm. They hired learning designers, including Dahlia, and found their niche in the
     intersection of applied AI and learning theory. Dahlia specialized in cognition and
     education. She worked closely with her more tech-savvy coworkers, and she relied
     on data sets, such as the L&D Trends Targets Report, and advanced software, like the
     Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek user-testing and participatory design platform. With these
     tools, combined with iterative cycles of testing and analysis, Dahlia and her fellow
     Oivallus learning engineers were able to substantially enhance the effectiveness of
     the learning experiences they created, even across different contexts and for diverse
     learner populations.
            It was time for Dahlia to get to work. She opened her latest assignment, a
     vocational project called Practical Piping, contracted by an industry group. It focused
     on new smart plumbing technologies. (Who knew that was a thing? Dahlia mused.)
     The learning engineering challenge this time was to train an entire industry on a
     brand-new plumbing technology. The project also incorporated several twenty-first
     century competencies, specifically metacognition and self-directed connectivism
     skills, and it included practical exercises organized around a space exploration
     theme. Oivallus had been getting mileage out of the popularity of that Mars mission!
            Several hours passed. Dahlia lost herself in her work, until she was interrupted
     by a gentle tapping on her desk. Elliot stood there, with two other coworkers loitering
     expectantly near the door. They were heading down the café for a midmorning
     coffee. Already?! The morning had been a blur! she thought. With a start, Dahlia
     checked the time. It was already ten o’clock. That meant the workday would be
     ending soon in Singapore. Declining her team’s invitation, she instead logged into
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 389



the extranet chat and found her Pac-World Informatica contact. After a few polite
texts to confirm the call, Dahlia joined a video-teleconference with Ehuang, her
performance analytics point of contact.
       Ehuang was a wiz at data science, and even better, she made the technobabble
intelligible.
       “This latest package we’re making,” Dahlia began.
       “The plumbing one . . . ?”
       “Yes, Practical Piping for Plumbing Professionals,” Dahlia smiled at the
ostentatious module name. “It’s almost done, but I need to add in the instrumentation
data hooks you created.”
       The company where Ehuang worked, a massive analytics firm based in the
heart of Singapore’s tech sector, had an agreement with Oivallus to collect and analyze
data on the performance of its learning products. Every module Oivallus created
included—what Ehuang called—paradata. It provided general inference tracking
based on the module’s access, use, and ratings. The higher-end products, like the
PBL packages Dahlia designed, also included tailored impact metrics that tied into
Pac-World Informatica’s global network of data systems. These additional measures
went over and above the learning effectiveness evaluations that Dahlia included in
the curriculum. For example, Ehuang explained how some of the measures were
included in the current package to tease out the profitability impacts of the training.
       Dahlia always found it difficult to envision how such distal impacts could be
correlated with specific interventions, like a single training program, but the special-
ists at Pac-World Informatica were masters of that kind of big data analysis as well
as the instrumentation that made it possible. Ehuang would sometimes joke that their
secret was “data farming” instead of “data mining.”
       “You have to plant the data seeds if you want the data fruits, lah!”
       Over the next hour, Ehuang walked Dahlia through the package that Pac-
World Informatica had sent over for the new plumbing curriculum. By the time their
call ended, Dahlia was ready to do the project’s pre-flight review.
       The Practical Piping curriculum covered a set of three primary and five
secondary competencies. It included three interrelated PBL activities (with the
embedded impact measures from Ehuang) as well as integrated formative and
summative assessments and AI-enabled student, mentor, and evaluator guidance,
such as scaffolding recommendations, just-in-time nudges, and feedback templates.
The package also included alternative pedagogical recommendations, adaptation
pathways, and different presentation modes depending on whether it was used in a
formal learning context (like a vo-tech school), a technology-aided blended one, or
390 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     a self-directed learning situation.
            Once the files were packaged, Dahlia clicked the “pre-flight check” button.
     Her mouse icon became a spinning animation, indicating the system was processing,
     and a few moments later, a dialog window appeared with a list of errors and warn-
     ings about the curriculum package. Dahlia reviewed them, accepting or fixing each
     as appropriate. Once satisfied, Dahlia clicked “approve” and then committed the
     Practical Piping package to her team’s repository. Elliot would do the final quality
     control on it—including the crowdsourced user-testing facilitated by their partner
     Chakrabarti-Dhav Infotek—and then he’d send it off for the customer’s review. Once
     they gave the greenlight, it would be disseminated across their partner organizations
     and vocational schools.
            Dahlia smiled. That was one task done for the day. After lunch, she thought,
     I’ll review the team’s update on our national curriculum project.


                                              !#"
     Dominic’s Story
     Dominic tugged at his collar. He hated wearing a tie, especially when it was so hot
     out. It was nearly winter, but Austin still felt like a sauna. He fidgeted unconsciously.
     Normally, his in-office days at the CrownSoft Learning Systems Corporate HQ were
     the highlight of his workweek. He looked forward to the breakout groups and good-
     natured camaraderie among the Scrum teams—and the less formal dress code. But
     today wasn’t business as usual; instead, Dominic was leading a special in-progress
     review with their important (VIP) clients from the Faang Academy.
             He checked his watch again. Logically, he knew there was plenty of time,
     but these VIP ceremonies always made him inexplicably nervous. He took a breath
     and closed his eyes. Dominic considered his day; walking through his mental to-
     do list helped settle his jitters. First, he’d check in with Marko and Liz to make
     sure they were ready to show off the new content streaming server. It leveraged
     widespread interoperability standards to enable CrownSoft’s curricular packages to
     flow seamlessly across different learning platforms, whether web browsers, mixed
     reality gear, pedagogical assistance apps, or some other emerging tech. CrownSoft
     had effectively abstracted the content from the hardware and software, a major coup
     in terms of efficiency and flexibility.
             Next, he’d talk with Imani about her adaptive VR demo. CrownSoft had
     scored pre-release copies of the new e-Mersion 360° system, and Imani’s team had
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 391



wasted no time assembling an adaptive learning module for it. The lesson was built
on a PBL package they’d gotten from their Finnish vendor. Dominic had tested it
last week. And what a marvel! The system transported him to different “locations”
around the world where he could work with people from other countries on problem-
solving challenges, and it doled out in-game rewards for his actions. It had felt like
a game and a vacation all at once, and beyond that enjoyment, he’d also picked up a
new appreciation for cross-cultural skills and international affairs.




Later that morning, Dominic found himself in the CrownSoft development team
conference room. He’d completed his to-do list hours before, and Mackenzie, the
team’s project coordinator, had the meeting logistics well in hand. She was just
connecting the video feed to their colleagues in Helsinki when the VIP customers
arrived. Through the etched-glass walls of the conference room, Dominic could see
them in the atrium. He waved, and Kevin Tanaka made a friendly gesture in reply,
as he and his companions turned toward the meeting room.
       Mr. Tanaka was one of the executives at the Faang Academy, a private nonprofit
school system that had spun out of a Silicon Valley conglomerate. The Academy
began as a postsecondary vocational program, a bridge meant to prepare prospective
workers to fill the high-tech roles those companies needed. Soon it expanded into
a magnet school for secondary students. Over time, it grew even more: adding
adult education for ongoing workforce development and incorporating younger and
younger preparation programs. Eventually, the Academy became a fully integrated
learning pipeline, stretching from primary school to workforce development. It also
built public-private partnerships, and in coordination with government programs,
was soon recognized as the first public-private K-to-Gray (kindergarten through
life) school system.
       The Academy’s success could be attributed, at least in part, to its adoption of
learning engineering principles. From its first days, the Academy focused on high-
yield learning approaches. It championed proven learning science theories, employed
iterative human-centered engineering design methodologies, and verified their
impacts through ongoing performance assessments. Using the wealth of data science
knowledge its parent companies had refined through e-commerce or digital media,
the Academy also incorporated advanced learning analytics and constantly improved
its offerings through experimentation and A/B testing. They also leveraged those
data to support highly adaptive learning—what the Academy touted as educational
392 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     “mass customization,” a term borrowed from its commercial roots. Regardless of
     the nomenclature, the results were powerful. Academy programs showed effect sizes
     over 3.0, as compared to more traditional educational offerings.1
            What’s more, because the Academy began as a vocational program, it hadn’t
     been constrained by the classic template of a multiyear degree. Instead, individual
     learners could identify the jobs or tasks they wanted to accomplish, then complete
     curricular blocks to build those competencies and earn associated microcredentials
     along the way. Over time, as the Academy expanded into K-12 educational offerings,
     including foundational requirements such as algebra, English, and a broad range of
     cross-domain knowledge literacy competencies.
            Across sectors, Faang’s competency-based approach untethered education and
     training from the time-in-seat model. It was also a key factor in personalizing each
     person’s learning trajectory, making the Academy widely popular—from individuals
     and parents to companies and communities.




     “ . . . and with these inference engines, we fuse the different assessment pieces and
     parts to give us the holistic profile for the learner,” Elliot Mäkinen was explain-
     ing through the video conference. The meeting was well into its third hour, albeit
     with a lunch break in the middle where Mackenzie had served up platters of street
     tacos—much to the delight of their out-of-town clients. Bellies sated and in high
     spirits after the morning’s successful tech demos, the second phase of the meeting
     was progressing steadily. It featured CrownSoft’s data engineering team and their
     partners at Oivallus, a boutique Finnish company expert in cognition and learning
     with advanced technologies.
              This discussion was the main feature of the meeting, and despite himself,
     Dominic felt that familiar nervous energy creeping into his mind. For reassurance,
     he glanced at Mr. Tanaka, sitting at the head of the table. The VIP client was deep
     in discussion with Elliot and seemed genuinely absorbed in the conversation. In his
     earlier days, Mr. Tanaka had been an accomplished software engineer, and Dominic
     could tell the older man still enjoyed diving into the technical details. That was good.
     It boded well for this part of the meeting where Dominic’s team planned to unveil the
     beta release of their Autodex 3.0 software.
              Autodex had become the industry leader for 3E records—those digital
     wallets that individuals and organizations use to store, validate, and exchange data
     on their education, employment, and experience (the so-called 3Es). Autodex used
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 393



distributed ledger technologies and standards-based interfaces to create a secure
and trustworthy data exchange system. By a large margin, Autodex was the most
popular application for this, but it wasn’t the only app available. The data feeding
into the Autodex was considered a public utility, so anyone could (theoretically)
build applications and data exchanges on top of that stream. Several different 3E
exchanges, sort of like different credit card companies and credit monitoring bureaus,
were popular across the learning and development sector, and associated application
programming interfaces (APIs) were standard on most devices.
       Dominic’s thoughts refocused as Mackenzie made a drum-rolling sound on the
conference table. “And now, ladies and gentlemen,” she said with a verbal flourish,
“the thing you’ve all been waiting for: It’s time for Dominic Morales to truly geek-
out over data.” A couple of the clients smiled, and Mr. Tanaka looked eager. Dominic
got to his feet and gestured at the oversized display screen. On it, a video of the new
Autodex upgrade had begun to play.
       “Thanks, Mac, for that . . . flattering introduction.” Dominic said with
good-natured sarcasm. “And thanks to Elliot and our friends at Oivallus. With their
latest set of composite assessments, we’re getting a remarkable level of validity from
today’s L&D performance data. We’re up to 92 percent reliability for identifying
someone’s digital signature, just from their routine clickstream interactions. That’s
almost eliminated K-16 cheating in Faang schools and apps. Also, the results of
last quarter’s testing in the Midwest vo-tech sector found a better than 80 percent
correlation—consistently—between assessments embedded in our curriculum and
downstream training transfer tasks.”
       The clients nodded politely. No doubt, they’d heard these statistics before. He
was simply leading up to the big reveal, so Dominic continued.
       “These are all incremental improvements. Important, meat-and-potatoes im-
provements, but not really new, you know? Schools and students have come to ex-
pect that level of rigor with our products. So where are their pain points?”
       The video playing on the conference screen shifted to a set of graphs showing
the results of CrownSoft’s most recent consumer studies. Dominic touched the first
graph, and it expanded to fill the monitor.
       “For the third year in a row, businesses want more consumer confidence. A
lot of workplaces are still hesitant to cut the cord. They’re requiring applicants and
workers to use an Autodex app, but still verifying personnel through legacy, manual
means. This is a particular problem in these two factors, here.” Dominic pointed.
“First, third-party credentials. Although Faang Academy and a lot of other classic
school systems have good name-recognition, which has built trust in our programs,
394 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     our smaller partners don’t benefit from that celebrity status.”
            “And institutional name-recognition isn’t a pedagogical technique!” Dahlia
     quipped from the video feed, as she and Dominic had practiced in advance.
            “The other issue,” Dominic continued after pausing for some polite chuckling,
     “is the public competency sets Autodex uses.” The Autodex software, like the soft-
     ware used by their industry competitors, leveraged open ontologies that were created
     from common definitions of competencies, accessible via linked data from APIs.
            “The governance process for those public data sets is slow. And I mean
     sloooow—like Austin traffic before a three-day weekend. Which means we’re
     starting out with stale or incomplete competency data. It’s no surprise our consumers
     are skeptical of it. But we’ve fixed both of those issues in the latest Autodex release.
     Here’s how . . . ”
            Dominic then launched into his well-rehearsed summary of the new features
     meant to improve consumer trust in the system’s outputs. He walked through its
     updated data-lineage tagging and multitiered trust algorithms, along with the new
     user-experience features meant to better convey those trust quotients to end users.
     He also highlighted a new human-AI filtering algorithm meant to improve the cur-
     rency and reliability of baseline competency data.
            “We expect these enhancements to appeal to our employer end users and to
     help smaller schools or EdTech businesses. But what about individuals—the students
     and employees? We surveyed them too, and pretty universally, they want better self-
     directed learning.”
            Dahlia chimed in on cue. “Schools and businesses want this feature too. Schools
     are always eager to supplement their formal curriculum, and employers want their
     workforces up-skilled to the latest knowledge and skills. Meanwhile, individuals
     have a whole spectrum of reasons for wanting to learn.” Remotely, Dahlia clicked
     through to the next graph as she described the different archetypes of self-directed
     learners.
            “On the surface,” Dahlia continued as she advanced the presentation to a
     new screen, “it seems like our main challenges are identification (of the desired
     competencies), access (on the right platform), and motivation (to make the activity
     intrinsically or extrinsically appealing). These are all real issues, but there’s another
     factor: Learning skill—how effectively a student can teach themselves.”
            “The new algorithms in Autodex 3.0 include user-support for . . . ” Dominic
     fumbled at the word and checked his notes, while Dahlia chimed in to help.
            “ . . . for metacognitive support. Formally, we call it self-regulated learning
     scaffolding.”
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 395



       “Yeah, thanks! Dahlia’s talking about our new embedded pedagogical assis-
tance algorithm. At CrownSoft, we’ve just been calling it ‘Mentor in a Box.’”
       The meeting continued on for a couple more hours, broken up by demos and
coffee breaks. By the end of the day, Dominic felt exhausted but accomplished. He’d
rather spend twelve hours coding than half a day in meetings. But it had been worth
it. Mr. Tanaka and his team loved the new immersive learning modules, and they
seemed to appreciate the Autodex 3.0 upgrades. In fact, Mr. Tanaka had already
scheduled time for Dominic to brief the Faang Chief Learning Officer Council so
they could begin planning its roll-out.
       Ugh, another corporate meeting, Dominic mused. Well, I suppose that’s just
the price of success!


                                        !$"
Ishimwe’s Story
Ishimwe squinted at the midday sun beating down on her and the small group fol-
lowing behind her. She was leading them on a tour of the new Icyizere Hybrid School
campus in Rwanda’s Ngoma District. They were a curious sight strolling along the
town’s red-clay streets, each wearing business attire topped with fluorescent safety
vests and hardhats. Ishimwe paused and gestured to a half-constructed building.
       “This will be one of the multipurpose seminar buildings, and it’s one of the
hubs in the sensor network we discussed.”
       “Is this another community-built structure?” Shema Mugisha asked. The
white-haired man walked with a cane, and he was the most elderly representative
on the board of advisors. Despite the heat, he wore a button-up shirt and black suit
jacket, adorned with a silver pin from his favorite philanthropy. It reflected the light
like a miniature sun on his lapel.
       “Yes!” Ishimwe replied. “Through our program, we supply the autonomous
construction system that essentially prints the components, but we work closely with
the community to assemble the actual buildings and design the campus layout. We
also supply the smart sensors, so that the campus and portions of the city can be
added to the IoT network. But again, we work with the community to design the
specific features of each network. That means each region implements a different
version of it.”
       “Most of the sensors are self-powered, a majority through solar energy here
in Ngoma, and their connectivity is almost entirely wireless,” Ishimwe continued,
396 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     as she carefully stepped over a bundle of newly unfurled cables stretched out on the
     ground. “But we’re also installing some high-performance structured cabling under
     the campus.”
            “Come, let me show you one of the completed seminar buildings, and we can
     see how the citywide sensors are supporting educational outreach.” Ishimwe led the
     group to one of the nearby buildings.
            Inside, much-needed shade and water bottles awaited. The group sat down at
     a table, which had been arranged for them in a partially completed classroom. All
     around, electrical wires and monitor mounts hung from bare walls, clearly awaiting
     the addition of video screens and other technologies. Ishimwe continued chatting
     with the board members, and after a few minutes, she described the various tech-
     nologies that would soon be installed in the educational space. She also highlighted
     the back-end technology, including the underlying data fabric that would connect the
     various learning applications into an interoperable, holistic system of systems—a
     learning ecosystem. That ecosystem could incorporate a range of technologies, from
     common computers to mixed reality devices, mobile learning tools, and even em-
     bedded sensors. Together, these interoperable delivery platforms created flexibility,
     and they offered learners many different learning pathways, depending on their
     unique needs.
            She continued, “We’ve found it’s important to situate learning within the real
     lives of the students. For example, in this region, there’s still a lot of traditional
     agriculture, and we use that in some lessons. The district-wide sensor grid monitors
     weather conditions, and then we use those data in modules related to climate,
     weather, and even students’ own farming projects.”
            “This goes both ways too. We can bring the outside data into structured
     lessons, making those lessons more relevant to students. Conversely, we can take
     lessons out into the students’ world. Thanks to our network upgrades, students have
     6G connections anywhere in the district.”
            Ishimwe pulled a pair of augmented reality glasses from her bag and then
     showed how their embedded location sensors and computer vision could help situate
     lessons. She loaded a postsecondary biology module that her group had recently
     acquired from CrownSoft Learning Systems. Then, using the software’s developer
     mode to spoof some of the context data, she showed how the glasses could detect
     different animals and plants, such as the slightly wilted eucalyptus leaf she produced
     for demonstration purposes. Once detected, the glasses could deliver anatomy or
     phytotomy in microlessons that were overlaid in the wearer’s audio and visual
     channels. The software came as a part of a gamified package. It encouraged students
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 397



to earn points by seeking out different species and then proving their biological
knowledge of them. Microlessons in the package also accounted for the local
environment, for example, incorporating geo-specific modules on invasive species
if students happen to find non-native flora at a particular location.
       The board of advisors seemed impressed, and their appreciation grew as
Ishimwe reviewed the financial details. The ruggedized AR hardware had become
cost-effective enough to provide up to two pairs for every student covered by their
program.
       “That’s all quite good,” Shema Mugisha said slowly. “You’ve made great prog-
ress in expanding educational access. You should be proud of this work, Doctor Ma-
horo,” the elderly man continued, using Ishimwe’s surname. “But I still worry about
the digital education divide. Yes, we’re rapidly closing the access gap, but I have
seen many educational programs and many, many new educational tools. So, I know!
I know there is more to the divide than technology. Simply increasing access to com-
puters, networks, and”—he gestured toward the glasses—“other gadgets, that is not
enough. Technology, alone, is not learning, and information, alone, is not education.”
       Ishimwe nodded and began to reply, but Mugisha kept talking over her. Clear-
ly, he’d been mulling over this point for some time, and it was bursting to escape.
       “I am no Luddite. I see the potential, but we need to also ensure the lessons are
well made and well curated. We need semantic coherence in our curricula. All of the
individual pieces need to come together so that they create quality learning. It is our
job to produce students who have knowledge and learning literacy, who can think,
and who can transfer that foundational knowledge out into the world. Ultimately, I
worry about the hidden digital divide. I worry that some students will have unequal
access to coherent and high-quality educational curricula—even as they get access
to new electronics and libraries worth of information.” Mugisha continued on his
soapbox a bit longer, even referencing some graphs he’d prepared on his tablet.
       “Mwami Mugisha,” Ishimwe finally interjected, using the loquacious man’s
honorific title. “Thank you for your concern. It’s clear you understand the depths
of the challenge we face. I can assure you that we’ve learned from the past. Today’s
modernization efforts extend beyond a mere moment in time.”
       Mugisha nodded for Ishimwe to explain.
       “One of the keys to our success is community outreach. We don’t simply ar-
rive with premade solutions from Kigali, Addis Ababa, or Geneva. The first phase of
any development project starts months before even a single meter of dirt is moved.
We work hand-in-hand with the local teachers and leaders. It’s a human-centered
design process. Together, we identify their city’s needs and tailor the curriculum,
398 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     technology grid, and construction to them. We also take special care to find com-
     munity groups, like the Zaza Women’s Center, so that we can make sure different
     voices are heard.”
            “Especially, we collaborate closely with educational professionals. For
     example, here in Ngoma, we’ve just completed the third cohort of teacher training.
     The training helps them learn to use new educational technologies, including the
     assistive AI coaches and all of the associated pedagogies,” Ishimwe explained with
     a pointed tilt of her head toward Mugisha. “Once these systems reach an initial
     operational capacity, we leave a smaller team behind. They continue working with
     the community and maturing their processes iteratively. They also collect efficacy
     data, like what you’re looking at here in the presentation.”
            Mugisha smiled. “Our investors from the World Bank and United Nations will
     be pleased.”
            “Yes, agreed . . . ” another member of the board, Adola Abdullahi, interjected,
     her tone implying a forthcoming “but” at the end of her words. The shrewd business-
     woman continued, “But what about the ‘private’ side of our public-private partner-
     ship? We can all agree that expanding educational access is admirable, but we also
     need to consider our private sector investors. Doctor Mahoro, would you talk to us
     about the business case?”
            “If you’ll turn to page twelve on your tablet,” Ishimwe responded, “you’ll
     see initial results from our newest commercialization initiative, called the Pathways
     Network. It’s still early, but we’ve established a network of schools and businesses
     that create sponsored educational opportunities as well as a business pipeline for
     both employers and students (or, as we like to say, ‘future employees’).”
            “This says businesses have access to freemium subscriptions?” Abdullahi
     questioned as she examined the presentation on her tablet.
            “That’s correct. Businesses or business groups, like the International Cham-
     ber of Commerce, purchase licenses to join Pathways. Among other things, those
     subscriptions give them unique access to certified talent in places like rural Ngoma.
     This gives businesses the high-quality employees they desperately need and at a
     competitive rate in the global context.”
            “But what does freemium mean, here?”
            “The license costs are on a sliding scale, depending on the other contribu-
     tions an organization provides. For instance, some businesses sign up for our mentor
     program. They dedicate a set number of expert hours for group teaching and indi-
     vidual coaching. This actually serves our needs just as well as, if not better than,
     financial payments. It gives our students access to professionals who can help them
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 399



understand how different lessons translate to workplace settings. And, as Mwami
Mugisha will appreciate, the small-group sessions and personal mentorship prove
extraordinarily valuable to the students. This is just one example. Businesses can
contribute in several other ways, and ultimately, those that choose a full level of
commitment pay nothing for their Pathways membership, at least monetarily.”
       “You said ‘high-quality employees,’” Eric Kubwimana, one of the newest ad-
visory board members, noted. “How are you determining that?”
       “One of the options is to participate in our validation and accreditation pro-
gram. Members assist with continuous content reviews and certifications as well as
some individual assessments and credentialing work.”
       “The NGOs also support those programs,” the venerable Mugisha observed.
       “Can you tell us more about this?” Abdullahi asked, pointing at a new page
on her table. Ishimwe noted where the woman was looking and then launched into
another topic.
       “You mean our AI-recommender system. It’s the cornerstone of our learning
ecosystem. It accounts for a variety of factors, from individuals’ personal profiles in
their Autodex to their physical context, teacher and mentor suggestions, and even the
time of day or local weather. Students can access their personalized recommendations
through an interface on either their AR glasses or personal computers, tablets, or
phones. Teachers and educational cohorts have similar user interfaces, and if students
lack the proper gear, they can go to one of these classroom huts, located around the
region, which all have connectivity and public screens.”
       Ishimwe stood from the table, motioning for the others to do the same. “Actu-
ally, this is good timing. I want to show you how the smart interface on this system
works in context. I’ve planned a little demonstration at the nearby irrigation station.
When you get there, put on your AR glasses and enter this code. It will make the
system think that you’re Jan Alleman, a young man in the tenth grade who wants
to be a civil engineer. When you arrive at the pumping station, you’ll see how the
AI-recommender suggests a variety of lessons, including some hybrid activities that
incorporate multiple competency sets. Is everyone rested and ready? Good, let’s
head out.”


                                        !%"
Ehuang’s Story
“ . . . but in two years, our forecast predicts a shortage of professionals across your
400 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     medical sector,” Ehuang explained through the video conference to her audience
     on the other side of the world. She was one of the senior scientists at Pac-World
     Informatica (PWI), a world leader in data engineering and analytics. Her specialty
     was education and workforce data, and today, she was briefing the results of her
     most recent project. A consortium of civic leaders had commissioned PWI to gauge
     the health of their education, training, and employment indicators and to help them
     predict potential risks and opportunities.
            “This result, which I report at a 90 percent confidence interval, shows a short-
     fall of 18 to 23 percent. Fortunately, we have recommendations for you.” Ehuang
     pressed her screen, advancing the presentation to the next page. There, she drilled
     into several courses of action her team had prepared. These included proposals for
     educational apprenticeships and new vocational modules to help mitigate some high
     attrition rates they’d uncovered. Ehuang walked through several more projections.
     One showed how certain improvements to science programs in secondary schools
     would predictably improve their medical sector workforce along with jobs in other
     bio-related fields. They also discussed some mature vo-tech programs that the com-
     munities could implement.
            “These vocational curricula are produced by a Finnish company called Oi-
     vallus. They partner with us, and we can help arrange for them to work with your
     school system, if you’d like. Also, a notable feature of the curricula is their embed-
     ded assessments, courtesy of PWI. These include the typical tests and evaluative
     simulations but also many stealth assessments, which have a significantly greater
     reliability and validity when used over time.”
            Ehuang also pitched a creative solution available from another one of PWI’s
     partners. “It’s called the Pathways Network. You purchase subscriptions, with a high
     majority of those proceeds directly supporting the program’s operation, or you pro-
     vide in-kind resources like some faculty service. Participation gives direct access
     to entry-level employees who matriculate through a rigorous program and receive
     certifications from a well-accredited board. This won’t completely solve your worker
     shortage (because the graduates don’t typically emigrate), but a portion of your at-
     risk jobs can be filled through telehealth. This means Pathway could be an ideal way
     to augment your local communities while also growing global connections.”
            Ehuang continued through the presentation, next reviewing several indicators
     tied to arts and humanities. She also shared some interesting correlations her team
     had uncovered about population wellbeing, worker sick days, and various arts
     programs. As she began to flip to the next digital page, one of the viewers, a woman
     whose screen name read Mme Renée Bouchard interrupted.
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 401



       “Pardon me, but before we continue, could you address the gray section on
that chart?”
       The woman’s eye had been drawn to a voided section of the visualization.
       “This place has unavailable data. Although we can collect this data, it hasn’t
been integrated into the analysis because of privacy policies. This data could be
useful, as it gives a more complete picture of primary education factors,” Ehuang
replied. “Actually, this is a good time to explain. You can see the ‘more details’ box
near the bottom of your screens, meh? Click it and I will highlight something.”
        Ehuang pressed a spot on her display, making the presentation jump to the
appendix. It showed a chart with cost figures and copious fine print. “This is the
work-breakdown cost of our project. The green-colored portion, here, is overhead
cost. The next section is the labor-hour cost, mostly for analysis and reporting. Look
at the portions highlighted in blue. Those indicate the data usage fee. Around half
of the report cost is used for microtransactions to the data owners. This fee doesn’t
apply to all data. Some of it comes at no cost from sensors in your own public
infrastructure. Some other data was provided by your schools, at no cost to PWI. But
individual data points—particularly those collected by PWI or its partners—each
connect to a distributed ledger that records third-party uses of it. In return, PWI
(or whomever uses that data) sends a very small payment back to the person who
produced it—the student or worker, in this case.”
       “So, to answer Ms. Bouchard’s question more fully: The missing data on the
earlier chart is governed by privacy regulations that prevent us from collecting or
analyzing it. But, if you wanted to, we could ‘turn on’ that data collection for stu-
dents. You would need to gain their permissions and those of their legal guardian.
And we’d need to provide micropayments to all who consent. Make sense, meh?”
       “That does make sense,” a man with a graying mustache remarked. His screen
name read Lucas Russell. “How does this work with the Autodex service that we
recently purchased for the public schools? Should we anticipate escalating costs as
the program’s implementation expands?”
       “No,” Ehuang said. “The data in Autodexes (or any brand of education, em-
ployment, and experience record) belongs to the individual owning that wallet, lah.
So, there are no privacy restrictions or microtransactions for their own use of their
own data. This has been codified in various self-sovereignty regulations, including
several endorsed by your own nation. People must be allowed unfettered access to
their own data.”
       “How does that work with schools and businesses? If someone is, let’s say, a
nurse at a regional hospital, does the hospital or the medical system have access to
402 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     their records?”
            Inwardly, Ehuang counted her blessings that Tan (their unit’s legal and
     ethics officer) had required her to earn that credential in regional data regulations.
     Originally, she’d groused about the compliance training. She was a data scientist—
     not an attorney after all! But she had to admit, the training had paid dividends,
     especially in meetings like this.
            “Yes and no,” she replied to Mr. Russell. “Workers, for example, may freely
     provide data such as their earned credentials or their competency mosaic to their em-
     ployer. But organizations may only access these freely for the benefit of that worker,
     such as hiring or development. And they may not store the employee’s information
     outside of their Autodex without permission. But a hospital or health care system
     may access those data for, say, workforce planning or even for broader goals, such
     as scientific studies, if the data owner has set their privacy permissions accordingly
     and microtransitions are paid.”
            “So, if we’d like to identify high-potential junior medical workers, or others
     who we could up-skill into lower-density health care roles, would that be possible?”
     a different woman, marked as Councillor Namid, asked.
            “In general, yes.” Ehuang responded. “In fact, that’s one of the most effica-
     cious uses of such data, and it leads me back to some recommendations we had
     prepared. Shall we return to the main presentation?”




     By the time the briefing had ended, Ehuang felt wrung out. It had only taken a few
     hours, but briefing clients across a video conference—at five o’clock in the morning,
     no less—was exhausting! Fortunately, she’d planned her workday wisely, blocking
     the rest of it for quiet data science tasks. Psychometrics and statistical computing
     were just the thing to soothe Ehuang. She stretched and flipped on the electric kettle,
     which sat on the other side of her small home office. Her dog, Ginger Bean, stirred
     on his little bed as she passed, exposing his furry belly in hopes of some pets, which
     Ehuang dutifully provided as she waited for the water to boil.
           With a steaming tea in hand, she returned to her workstation and clicked the
     Command Station app. It brought up her assignments, reports, and team chatter. She
     had two tasks for the day, in addition to that early morning briefing. Her team had
     received a new job from a major athletic brand who wanted to create data-driven
     credentials for lifestyle activities, like mountain climbing or snowboarding. She and
     her team needed to identify the stealth assessment factors. She would create those
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 403



classifiers by analyzing the data streams from the brand’s wearable sensors. The
company wanted to issue validated digital badges to people who wore their gear
while accomplishing certain athletic feats, as logged by the smart apparel. It seemed
like a complex task—but incredibly exciting! Ehuang couldn’t wait to dig into it. But
first, she had better clear out some of her backlog.
        She owed one of her colleagues at Oivallus a follow-up. Ehuang had worked
with Dahlia for years, and now counted the learning scientist among her friends,
even though they had never met in person. Dahlia wanted to coordinate on the
embedded assessments in their Finnish National Agency for Education project. It
had something to do with connecting learning analytics from the K-12 curriculum
to improved feedback for teachers and mentors. If it had been up to Ehuang, she’d
have simply shown the faculty the educational data, but Dahlia insisted on creating
something she called “scaffolded support.” It tied into a decision support system
Oivallus helped to maintain, and although Ehuang didn’t understand the details,
she could appreciate the need to validate the interactions between the data streams,
teacher recommendations, and performance outcomes.
        Ehuang checked the time on her computer. It was barely past 8:00 a.m., too
early for Dahlia in Finland. That meant Ehuang could dive into that athletic sensor
data, after all, at least for a few hours. This was going to be a fun day!


                                        !&"
Conclusion
This chapter was written as a story to illustrate several themes. In Arun’s section,
we incorporated notions of data-driven learning analyses, human-AI teaming, social
learning, crowdsourcing from both experts and learners, A/B testing, and trust. In
Dahlia’s section, we emphasized the critical role learning theory plays in building
quality education and training interventions, and we touched on nonlinear learning
concepts, competency-based learning, twenty-first century competencies, social and
problem-based learning, semantic coherence, and the integration of multiple sub-
jects into a single curriculum.
       Dominic’s section highlights the collaboration between pedagogical and
technological experts as well as concepts such as credentials, AI mentors, personalized
learning, and the integration of schooling and employment sectors. Perhaps most
notably, this section also introduced the Autodex, a persistent record of someone’s
education, employment, and experience. Ishimwe’s section builds on several learning
404 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     technology topics, including augmented reality, network connectivity, AI-driven
     personalization, and the internet of things. Her section also extends the theme of
     blurred boundaries across educational, commercial, and geographical borders, and
     importantly, it highlights educational access and equity issues. Finally, Ehuang’s
     section focuses on assessment, for both formal and informal learning contexts, and it
     references learning analytics, stealth assessment, data privacy, and self-sovereignty.
            Taken together, these five stories emphasize the diverse, interdisciplinary
     teamwork needed for learning engineering. In the future, we imagine learning
     theorists working closely with technology developers, psychometricians, data
     scientists, business leaders, and community advocates—among many others.
     Similarly, we foresee an evolution in learning and employment, with both shifting
     toward anytime, anywhere modalities and moving away from artificial constraints
     such as age, location, and time.
            Humans, by our nature, are always learning, but in the future, we imagine there
     will be mechanisms that allow individuals to earn credit for ubiquitous learning—
     even experiences like climbing a mountain. Informal learning will be captured
     through various data streams, possibly through stealth assessments running in the
     background, making it more measurable and accreditable. And when enough evidence
     has been demonstrated, someone may be able to earn inferred ranks in a competency
     or a verified credential, like a digital badge. Similarly, we would expect that many of
     these learning experiences would help reveal traditionally underrepresented factors,
     such as social or emotional capabilities, and that such valuable competencies will
     become more explicitly recognized in individuals’ personal profiles.
            Changing how education, employment, and experiences interact and are
     credited will, necessarily, also have large effects on today’s formal learning
     institutions. For example, there will be a significant power transfer from teachers
     (today’s classroom leaders) to students, who will be more empowered to pursue
     their own learning goals and definitions of success. In the intelligence augmentation
     economy, where new knowledge is created faster than it can be consumed, this
     learning-to-learn skill will be essential, along with other novel or reimagined twenty-
     first century competencies, such as collaboration, empathy, and creativity. Similarly,
     self-regulation and other metacognitive skills will grow in importance, as success in
     this self-driven, nonlinear, AI-supported life-long learning environment will depend
     so heavily on them.
            Because this chapter focuses on the learning engineering process, the
     involvement of teachers and trainers was muted in the narrative. However, we
     anticipate that learning facilitators (of all flavors) will continue to fill important,
                       The Future World with Learning Engineering: A Story | 405



albeit different, roles. Teaching methods, subject areas, technologies, and data-driven
algorithms will change the character of their jobs. Their roles will evolve from “sage
on the stage” (a more passive approach focused on information transfer) to “guide on
the side” (an active mentor who helps scaffold knowledge and offer coaching across
mental, social, emotional, and physical domains).
       In addition to learning technology and technique, significant portions of our
story focused on data. The big data revolution promises to have massive, paradigm-
changing impacts on education and employment. This chapter paints an optimistic
picture, but we’ve also hinted at the darker side of what’s possible. For example,
data-rich and AI-enabled contexts present opportunities to spread disinformation,
create inequitable access to quality information, or misuse personal data. While we
mustn’t let these risks derail progress, we do have an imperative to address them.
For example, what safeguards can the learning engineering community establish to
ensure educational access and build internal safeguards against corrupt or degraded
information? How can we quantify and validate trust in educational resources or
manage the veracity and potential bias of data sources and analyses? Privacy and
protection of data are other areas of concern, along with issues of self-sovereignty
of one’s own data. We will need to invest in intentional planning, policies, and
governance to prepare for this dynamic worldwide system of systems.
       We believe the capabilities described in this chapter are within reach. Each of
the techniques and technologies exists in some form today (if not yet fully realized or
deployed). Similarly, there is a real desire for quality formal and informal learning,
improved measurement, data-driven analyses, and broad integration across global
education and employment practices. So, the question isn’t technical feasibility or
motivation, rather the question is whether we can organize a practical, equitable,
and sustainable learning engineering system. This is the challenge that we, the
burgeoning learning engineering community, face. Will we rise to the challenge,
navigating complex integrated systems, ethical conundrums, and wise governance?
We have the tools if we’re willing to commit to its success. The future of learning is
our story to write.
406 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Endnote
     1 An effect size of 3.0 means that 99.9% of the experimental group outperformed the control group.
       In practice, it’s essentially the highest effect size. Today, the best designed intelligent tutors can
       produce this effect size as compared to traditional training and education modalities. For exam-
       ple, see Kulik, James A., and J. Dexter Fletcher. “Effectiveness of intelligent tutoring systems:
       a meta-analytic review.” Review of Educational Research 86, no. 1 (2016): 42–78. https://doi.
       org/10.3102/0034654315581420

About the Authors
Avron Barr
                        Avron Barr edited the seminal Handbook of Artificial Intel-
                        ligence while at Stanford University and then co-founded
                        Teknowledge, an early AI startup in Silicon Valley. As a
                        consultant, he helps people understand, explain, and market
                        cutting-edge software. Avron is an expert on the infrastruc-
                        ture needed to support AI-assisted learning. He chaired the
                        IEEE Learning Technology Standards Committee and cur-
rently serves on the IEEE Educational Activities Board. He’s had a life-long interest
in how AI will change the way we know, the way we learn, and the way we teach.


Michelle Barrett
                          Dr. Michelle D. Barrett is Vice President of Research and
                          Learning Engineering for Edmentum, where she leads
                          initiatives in learning engineering, efficacy research,
                          psychometrics and assessment design. After starting
                          her career in education teaching middle and high school
                          mathematics, Dr. Barrett has since held research, technical,
                          and leadership roles at the Colorado Commission on Higher
Education, the Colorado Department of Education, CTB / McGraw-Hill, Pacific
Metrics, and ACT. She is published in the Journal of Educational and Behavioral
Statistics, the Journal of Educational Measurement, the International Journal of
Artificial Intelligence in Education, and Psychometrika. Dr. Barrett serves on the
Board of Directors for the nonprofit organizations AIS Consortium and We Can
Learn and chaired the IEEE Industry Consortium on Learning Engineering SIG
on AI and Adaptive Technology from 2018 to 2019. Dr. Barrett earned her BS from
Stanford University, her MEd from Harvard University, a graduate certificate in
large-scale assessment from the University of Maryland, and her PhD from the
University of Twente, Netherlands. She also holds certifications in Scaled Agile,
Lean Six Sigma, and Pragmatic Marketing.
408 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Erin S. Barry
                              Erin S. Barry, MS, is Assistant Professor in the Department
                              of Military and Emergency Medicine at the Uniformed
                              Services University (USU). She is a biomedical and
                              biobehavioral scientist with expertise in research design,
                              data management, statistical analyses, and assessment.
                              Her role is to work on all aspects of leadership curriculum,
                              assessment, and research and development to translate
     scientific materials and evidence to be effectively taught and disseminated. She
     serves as Principal Researcher for several studies of leadership that focus on
     individual characteristics, interpersonal dynamics, team building, and academic
     performance as predictors of leadership competence. Ms. Barry earned a BE in
     Biomedical Engineering from Vanderbilt University. She earned an MS in Biomedical
     Engineering from the University of Texas at Arlington. She has worked at USU
     since 2010 where her research has focused on traumatic brain injury, post-traumatic
     stress disorder, and leadership. In addition, she helps to mentor graduate and medical
     students with regard to research activities and is a leadership coach for faculty, staff,
     and students. She is currently working on her PhD in Health Professions Education
     focusing on leadership and followership within healthcare teams.


     Laura Casey
                              Dr. Laura Casey is a professor in the Applied Behavior
                              Analysis (ABA)/Special Education program at the
                              University of Memphis. She earned her doctoral degree
                              in Educational Psychology, a Master of Science in School
                              Psychology, a Master of Arts in Community Counseling,
                              and a Bachelor’s in Psychology. She completed her doctoral
                              intern at the May Institute with an emphasis in ABA and
     Positive Behavior Supports in the school. She is a board certified behavior analyst
     (BCBA), a nationally certified counselor (NCC), and a nationally certified school
     psychologist (NCSP). She has been the PI and Co-PI on multiple grants totaling over
     6 million dollars. She has over 65 peer-reviewed publications and a leading book in
     ABA and early childhood. Dr. Casey teaches undergraduate and graduate courses
     in behavior analysis and autism, specializing in evidence-based practice, ethics, and
     assessment.

                         tems Engineering at Arizona State University and Director
                         of the DoD Advanced Distributed Learning Initiative Part-
                         nership Laboratory at Arizona State University. He has a
                         dual affiliation with the Ira A. Fulton Schools of Engineer-
                         ing and the Mary Lou Fulton Teachers College. Dr. Craig
                         has expertise at the intersection of cognitive psychology,
usability, and the science of learning (specifically learning technology). He has pub-
lished widely in the intersecting areas of psychology, education, and technology. Dr.
Craig’s previously funded projects include grants and contracts from the Institute of
Educational Sciences, Office of Naval Research, and several organizations within
the US Department of Defense. He has served on the program committee for AIED,
iFEST, and Cognitive Science and the editorial board for Journal of Educational
Psychology, Computers & Education (open), International Journal of STEM Edu-
cation, and the journal Technology, Mind, & Behavior. He has also guest edited vol-

STEM Education, and Journal of Research on Technology in Education and edited
three books: End-User Considerations in Educational Technology Design, Tutoring
and Intelligent Tutoring Systems, and Readings in Training and Simulations Vol 2.

     Brandt Dargue
                              Brandt Dargue is a Sr. Systems Engineer and Boeing Asso-
                              ciate Technical Fellow specializing in adaptive, personal-
                              ized, competency-based learning for Boeing Training Solu-
                              tions. Brandt has spent his 33 years at Boeing researching,
                              designing, developing, integrating, and testing training and
                              performance support solutions for Boeing customers and
                              employees. Brandt’s main focus is in automated curation of
     learning and operational experiences. He is inventor and co-inventor of several pat-
     ents for adaptive training curation technologies that enable automated performance
     assessment, adaptive scenarios, intelligent tutoring, and adaptive instruction. These
     technologies have been applied to several learning experience modalities, including
     e-learning, classroom instruction, simulations, virtual / augmented environments,
     and live aircraft. Brandt has served as technical lead, principal investigator, or pro-
     gram manager on numerous government contracts to create adaptive training solu-
     tions and to develop / perform the empirical studies to measure their effectiveness.
