Chris Dede
                        Chris Dede is the Timothy E. Wirth Professor in Learning
                        Technologies at Harvard’s Graduate School of Education.
                        His fields of scholarship include emerging technologies,
                        policy, and leadership. In 2007, he was honored by Harvard
                        University as an outstanding teacher, and in 2011, he was
                        named a Fellow of the American Educational Research
                        Association. He is currently a member of the OECD 2030
Scientific Committee and an Advisor to the Alliance for the Future of Digital
Learning, sponsored by the Mohammed bin Rashid Global Initiative (MBRGI). His
co-edited books include: Scaling Up Success: Lessons Learned from Technology-

Classroom Learning for Each Student; Teacher Learning in the Digital Age: Online
Professional Development in STEM Education; Virtual, Augmented, and Mixed
Realities in Education; Learning Engineering for Online Education: Theoretical
Contexts and Design-Based Examples; and The 60-Year Curriculum: New Models
for Lifelong Learning in the Digital Economy.

                         Diana Delgado is an Assistant Professor in the Applied
                         Behavior Analysis Program at the University of Memphis
                         and a Board Certified Behavior Analyst. Dr. Delgado has
                         provided behavioral services to individuals with develop-
                         mental disabilities and ASD, in home settings, schools and
                         clinics, and she supervises students who are in training to
                         obtain certification. Dr. Delgado has been a professor of be-
havior analysis at the graduate level for 11 years and is an active contributor to the
field through her research and service activities. She has published her research in
Behavior Analysis in Practice, The Psychological Record, Children and Youth Ser-
vices Review, The Behavior Analyst Today, Mexican Journal of Behavior Analysis,
and also in Latin American journals of psychology including Conductual, Suma
Psicológica, and Revista Latinoamericana de Psicología. Her research focuses in
the areas of learning processes in humans, problem solving, and in the application of
behavior analysis to problems of social significance.

                          Scott W. Greenwald, PhD, is President and CEO of Three
                          Space Lab, a spatial computing company focusing on learn-
                          ing, collaboration, and communication. He’s a PhD alum
                          of the MIT Media Lab, where he wrote a thesis titled The
                          Equipped Explorer: VR as a Medium for Learning in 2017
                          with Prof. Pattie Maes. He’s a former Research Scientist at
                          MIT’s Materials Research Lab, where he pursued explor-
atory learning and interactive visualization for crystallography with Prof. Joseph
Checkelsky. He’s co-founder and chair of the nonprofit Reality Hack, Inc., which
co-produces an annual VR / AR hackathon event at MIT. He has co-created numer-
ous VR installations and several live performances using AR and VR technology,
where his past and present projects have involved the neuroscience of the retina, im-
prov comedy, immersive narrative for children, crystallography, simulating electro-
statics, 3D capture of a string quartet, stories of flight from East Berlin, and contem-
porary circus. He holds degrees from Northwestern University, the Free University
of Berlin, and MIT, and has recently been appointed a Visiting Professor in Design
and Computation, a joint program between TU Berlin and UdK Berlin. In his spare
time, he practices dance improvisation, contact staff juggling, guitar, and singing.

eral psychology, writing for psychology, research methods, psychology of film, per-
sonality psychology, and advanced statistics. Research interests include technologi-
cally mediated communication, psycholinguistics, adaptive educational technology,
artificial intelligence, and the ethical implications of AI.

     in-service educators as an assistant professor of educational technology. At the core
     of all his work is supporting the development of instructional opportunities that
     provide learners the chance to engage in building deep conceptual understanding
     of content.
                                                             About the Authors | 415



Janet Kolodner
                        Janet Kolodner is Regents’ Professor Emerita of Computing
                        and Cognitive Science in Georgia Institute of Technology’s
                        College of Computing and Professor of the Practice at
                        Boston College’s Lynch School of Education and Human
                        Development. Her research has addressed issues in
                        learning, memory, and problem solving, both in computers
                        and in people. She pioneered the machine learning method
called case-based reasoning, which allows a computer to learn from its experiences,
and she used its underlying model to design a project-based approach to education
called Learning by Design. She is lead author of a comprehensive middle-school
science curriculum, Project-Based Inquiry Science, and collaborated on other
curriculum design projects. Kolodner was Founding Director of Georgia Tech’s
EduTech Institute, and she coordinated Georgia Tech’s Cognitive Science program
for many years. She is founding Editor-in-Chief and Editor-in-Chief Emerita of The
Journal of the Learning Sciences. She is a co-founder of the International Society
for the Learning Sciences and served as its first Executive Officer. Kolodner was a
program officer at the US National Science Foundation and led efforts to establish
and sustain the Cyberlearning Program (now called SETTL). Currently, she is co-
director of Boston College’s MA program in Learning Engineering, and she led its
development.

Prasad Ram
                         Dr. Prasad Ram (“Pram”) is the Founder, CEO of Gooru.
                         While at Google, he developed the prototype of Gooru Nav-
                         igator—a GPS for Learning. Gooru since has become an
                         independent nonprofit organization that provides Gooru
                         Navigator with web and mobile applications for students,
                         teachers, parents and school leaders. Students seeking to
                         learn a topic are presented with an adaptive pathway of
learning activities and teachers, parents and school leaders obtain real-time action-
able insights to personalize interventions. Gooru reaches over 7 million learners
through over sixty collaborators. Previously, Pram led Google Books for Education
in Mountain View and before that he was the head of Google R&D in India and led
projects on Google Maps, News, Language Technologies, Search, and Ads. Pram
received the Founders’ Award at Google for his work on Google Ads. Prior to joining
Google, Pram led engineering at Yahoo! and was a scientist at Xerox Research. Pram
has a PhD in Computer Science from UCLA, and a BTech in Computer Science from
Indian Institute of Technology-Bombay, India.

EdMatrix.org directory of Learning Tech standards. He helped found three success-
ful startups and is co-inventor of three patents. He has a BS in Computer Science
and an MBA from Brigham Young University.

     Steve Ritter
                             Steve Ritter is Founder and Chief Scientist at Carnegie
                             Learning. Dr. Ritter was instrumental in the development
                             and evaluation of the Cognitive Tutors for mathematics. He
                             is the author of numerous papers on the design, architecture
                             and evaluation of Intelligent Tutoring Systems and other ad-
                             vanced educational technology and is recognized as an ex-
                             pert on the design and evaluation of educational technology
     and on educational analytics. He is lead author of an evaluation that is one of the
     few to be judged by the US Department of Education’s What Works Clearinghouse
     as meeting their standards without reservations. He has received several awards,
     including the Best Paper award at the International Conference on Educational Data
     Mining. Carnegie Learning’s mathematics curricula are currently used by over
     500,000 students each year.

                              Bror Saxberg served most recently as the VP of Learning
                              Science at the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, where he worked
                              on how to bring more evidence-based practices into the
                              learning ecosystem as a whole. He previously served as the
                              Chief Learning Officer at Kaplan, Inc, where he worked to
                              convert Kaplan into a “learning engineering” organization.

     Learning Officer at K12, Inc., where he helped build evidence-based learning for
     practical use at scale across K-12. Prior to this, he had assignments at Knowledge

     and BS in Electrical Engineering from the University of Washington, both received
     in 1980. He received an MA in Mathematics from Oxford University, and also re-
     ceived his PhD in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science from MIT in 1989

Sae Schatz
                        Sae Schatz, PhD, serves as the Director of the Advanced
                        Distributed Learning (ADL) Initiative, a science, technology,
                        and policy program under the Office of the Secretary of
                        Defense in the US Department of Defense. Before joining
                        the government in 2015, Sae worked as an applied human–
                        systems scientist, with an emphasis on human cognition
                        and learning, adaptive systems, learning technologies,
performance assessment, and modeling and simulation. Sae has authored numerous
peer-reviewed scholarly publications, and she led development of three military
textbooks. More recently, she co-edited the book, Modernizing Learning: Building
the Future Learning Ecosystem, which defines a blueprint for revolutionizing
training and education through technology, data, next-generation learning science,
and innovation. Sae has worked in defense training and education for over a decade,
and she holds a doctorate in Modeling and Simulation from the University of Central
Florida.


Jordan Richard Schoenherr
                        Jordan Richard Schoenherr, PhD, is an Assistant Professor
                        (Department of Psychology, Concordia University), an Ad-
                        junct Research Professor in the Department of Psychology
                        and a member of the Institute for Data Science at Carleton
                        University, and a research and ethics consultant. He has
                        published widely in the areas of cognitive psychology, med-
                        ical education, ethics, and scientific integrity, served on the
Executive Board for the International Society for Psychophysics, worked on IEEE
Standards Association working group for Adaptive Instructional Systems and Adap-
tive Instructional Systems Ethics, and was a member of the Alan Turing Institute’s
AI in Cybersecurity working group. His research interests focus on understanding
judgment and decision-making in pure and applied domains including ethics.
420 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Robert Sottilare
                             Dr. Robert Sottilare is the Director of Learning Sciences
                             at Soar Technology, Inc. (soartech.com) and Chairman of
                             the Board for the not-for-profit Adaptive Instructional Sys-
                             tems (AIS) Consortium (aisconsortium.org), an industry
                             group focused on enhancing the effectiveness of tailored
                             training and educational solutions. He has nearly 40 years
                             of experience as a researcher, developer and evaluator of
     instructional technology and training systems. Dr. Sottilare received his doctorate in
     modeling and simulation with a focus on intelligent systems. His experience spans
     government (US Army and Navy science and technology organizations), industry
     and academia. His recent research has focused on adaptive instruction including
     learner and team modeling, automated authoring tools, AI-based real-time instruc-
     tional management, and evaluation methods for intelligent tutoring systems. At the
     US Army Research Laboratory, he founded and led the adaptive training science and
     technology program and is the father of the award-winning Generalized Intelligent
     Framework for Tutoring (GIFT), an adaptive instructional architecture. Dr. Sottilare
     is widely published with over 250 technical papers with over 2,500 citations. He is
     a senior member of the IEEE and founding Chair of the IEEE AIS Working Group
     and the HCII AIS Conference.


     Khanh-Phuong (KP) Thai
                              Dr. KP Thai is Director of Learning Sciences and Learning
                              Analytics at Age of Learning, Inc. She works on innovative
                              applications of learning sciences, game design, and analyt-
                              ics for advancing early childhood learning with adaptive
                              learning technology. Previously, she was Founder and Chief
                              Learning Scientist of an educational research consultancy
                              that provided research and analytic services to help orga-
     nizations develop evidence-based coursework, digital learning media, games, and
     assessments. She also led adaptive system design research as a Senior Learning and
     Data Scientist at Squirrel AI Learning. KP is co-editor of the Handbook of Research
     on Innovative Approaches to Early Childhood Development and School Readiness.
     She obtained her PhD in Cognitive Psychology with a minor in Quantitative Meth-
     ods from University of California, Los Angeles.
                                                               About the Authors | 421



Richard Tong
                          Richard Tong is an experienced technologist, executive,
                          entrepreneur and one of the leading evangelists for global
                          standardization efforts for learning technologies and
                          artificial intelligence. Richard Tong is currently the Chief
                          Architect and General Manager of US Operations, Squirrel
                          AI Learning. He also currently serves as the Chair of IEEE
                          Learning Technology Standards Committee and Chair
of IEEE artificial intelligence Standards Committee. Prior to Squirrel AI, he was
the Head of Implementation, Greater China Region for Knewton, and Director of
Solution Architecture for Amplify Education. He also served as the CTO of Phoenix
New Media (NYSE:FENG). On the academic and research front, he has organized
conferences such as the AIAED conferences (2018, 2019, 2020), and organized
workshops such as multimodal artificial intelligence for education workshops
(AIMA4EDU) at the IJCAI 2019, IJCAI 2020, IJCAI 2021, the ed-tech standard-
ization and collaboration workshops at the AIED 2019, AIED 2020 and AIED
2021. He was the workshop co-chair of AIED 2020, industry co-chair of ITS 2021
and AIED 2021 and held several track-chair positions in various AI and EdTech
conferences.


JJ Walcutt
                         Dr. JJ Walcutt is a scientist, innovator, and digital learn-
                         ing engineer who specializes in strategic development and
                         reform across education, military, and government. Her
                         current work focuses on leveraging digital transformation
                         capabilities for optimizing human cognition to drive deci-
                         sion superiority and improved performance across a wide
                         spectrum of programs including neuro-informed military
training, data-driven knowledge management, and talent development optimization.
Dr. Walcutt also recently served in the US Government as the Director of Innovation
at the Advanced Distributed Learning (ADL) Initiative under the Office of the Sec-
retary of Defense and as a Human Innovation Fellow under the Office of Personnel
Management. In her role at the Pentagon, Dr. Walcutt focused on promoting the sci-
ence of learning through the stewardship of policy and her service as a US Delegate
to NATO, Partnership for Peace, and as a national and international keynote speaker.
